title: Information Exploration
text:
Preface
This book teaches you how to use information to identify interesting real-world problems and generate insight. You will learn to find, collect, assemble, and organize data to inspire new questions, make predictions, generate deliverables, and work toward solutions. Along the way, you will develop the practical skills of exploratory data analysis using Python and SQL — two tools that appear in virtually every data-oriented career.
If the word “exploratory” feels a little abstract, that is intentional. Real analysis is rarely linear. You load data, discover it is messier than you expected, go back to clean it, realize you need a different angle, and iterate. This book teaches that rhythm — not only the individual techniques, but the habits of mind that turn a pile of rows into a defensible conclusion.

title: Information Exploration
text:
Who This Book Is For
This book is written for undergraduate students who have completed one introductory course in Python. You should be comfortable with variables, loops, conditionals, functions, and lists. If you can write a for loop that iterates over a list and appends results to another list, you have enough Python background to begin.
No prior experience with pandas, SQL, databases, or the command line is required. The book introduces each of these tools from scratch, with careful attention to the concepts that trip up newcomers.

title: Information Exploration
text:
What You Will Learn
By the time you finish this book, you will be able to:

Load tabular data from CSV, Excel, and SQLite sources into a working environment — and recognize the silent failures (wrong headers, misparsed types, hidden footnotes) that happen along the way.
Clean messy real-world data using type coercion, string methods, missing-value handling, and deduplication, and document every decision you make in a Known Issues Log.
Derive new information from existing datasets with grouped aggregation, pivot tables, and multi-table joins, auditing each operation to confirm nothing silently broke.
Translate the same analytical moves between pandas and SQL, so you can choose the right tool for the job regardless of where the data lives.
Characterize distributions, build visualizations with matplotlib and seaborn, and run simple hypothesis tests to answer real questions about public data.
Read Python error messages and tracebacks fluently, turning “walls of red text” into actionable diagnostic information.

You do not need to memorize every function or argument you encounter. Learning to navigate documentation is one of the most valuable skills this book will help you build — and you will come back to the docs for the rest of your career.

title: Information Exploration
text:
The Load–Clean–Extend–Reshape Framework
Data analysis is often taught as a grab bag of techniques. This book takes a different approach: it organizes the entire curriculum around a four-stage workflow that applies regardless of the tool you are using.
Loading is the process of getting data from its source — a CSV file, an Excel spreadsheet, a database — into your working environment. This stage seems simple, but it is where many invisible problems originate: wrong delimiters, misidentified headers, encoding errors, and columns silently cast to the wrong type.
Cleaning addresses the problems that loading reveals. You will learn to identify and fix data type mismatches, standardize inconsistent categories, handle missing values, detect duplicates, and document every decision you make along the way.
Extending means deriving new information from what you already have. This includes computing new columns, building grouped summaries and aggregation tables, and combining information from multiple sources. The goal is to transform raw observations into metrics that can support analysis.
Reshaping is the process of changing the structure of your data — pivoting between wide and long formats, merging tables on shared keys, and auditing the results to confirm nothing was lost or duplicated in the process.
These four stages are not strictly sequential. Real analysis involves cycling back through them as you discover new issues. But the framework gives you a reliable starting point and a vocabulary for describing where you are in any analysis workflow.

title: Information Exploration
text:
How This Book Is Organized
The book is divided into five parts. Part I builds the conceptual and technical foundation. Parts II and III each apply the Load–Clean–Extend–Reshape framework with a different tabular tool. Part IV extends the toolkit to non-tabular data (spatial, network, text, time series, and web-structured formats). Part V applies the results to analysis.
Part I: Foundations covers the paratechnical skills that most courses skip but that every analyst needs — navigating the terminal, organizing project files, writing documentation, managing Python environments, and reading error messages — as well as the conceptual and ethical vocabulary for working with public data. Four chapters: workspace setup, documentation and errors, research-question framing, and public-interest data science. These short chapters are designed to be referenced throughout the rest of the book.
Part II: Python introduces the pandas library for tabular data analysis. Across four chapters, you will learn to load CSV and Excel files with read_csv and read_excel, clean data using string methods, type coercion, and dictionary-based recoding, extend datasets with derived columns, groupby aggregation, and pivot_table, and reshape data using melt, merge, and concat. The datasets in this section include Colorado cannabis sales data, Boulder County voter registration records, Lahman Baseball statistics, and Colorado population estimates from the State Demography Office.
Part III: SQL teaches you to perform the same operations using Structured Query Language and the SQLite database engine. You will set up a database from the command line, design schemas, load cleaned CSVs, write diagnostic queries, use GROUP BY, subqueries, and Common Table Expressions (CTEs), and join multiple tables while auditing join quality. The SQL chapters use Colorado public health datasets — CDC mortality data, Colorado Bureau of Investigation crime statistics, and population estimates — culminating in a multi-table join that produces an analysis-ready dataset.
Part IV: Non-Tabular Data extends the book’s toolkit to data that does not arrive as rows and columns. Five chapters introduce spatial data with GeoPandas, network data with NetworkX, unstructured text with NLTK, time series with statsmodels and Prophet, and structured web data (JSON, XML, HTML) with requests and Beautiful Soup.
Part V: Analysis applies the conceptual vocabulary from Part I to the clean, well-structured data you have built. You will characterize distributions and identify outliers through descriptive statistics, build a visual vocabulary for exploring patterns using matplotlib and seaborn, and apply simple hypothesis tests from scipy.stats — t-tests, chi-square tests, and correlation coefficients — interpreting their results in plain language. Part V uses the same datasets from earlier chapters, so that the analytical questions grow naturally from the data preparation work you have already done.
Two Appendices document the datasets used throughout the book (sources, structures, known quirks) and disclose the author’s use of AI assistance during drafting.

title: Information Exploration
text:
Recurring Themes
Several principles recur throughout the book, regardless of whether you are working in pandas or SQL.
Inspect before you act. Every chapter begins by looking at the data before transforming it. The post-load checklist — shape, headers, types, missingness, distributions, duplicates, plausibility, and a quick visual scan — appears in every loading chapter and becomes second nature.
Validate after every transformation. Cleaning and reshaping operations can silently drop rows, create duplicates, or introduce null values. The book teaches you to check row counts, compare totals, and cross-verify results after every step.
Make invisible problems visible. Many data errors are silent — a join that drops rows because of trailing whitespace in a key column, a column that looks numeric but contains a hidden sentinel value, a merge that inflates row counts because of an unexpected many-to-many relationship. A central goal of this book is to train your eye to catch these problems before they corrupt your analysis.
Document your decisions. Every cleaning choice involves a judgment call. The book introduces the Known Issues Log and Data Dictionary as lightweight documentation tools that make your workflow reproducible and your assumptions transparent.

title: Information Exploration
text:
Conventions Used in This Book
Code blocks contain Python or SQL that you can run in a Jupyter notebook or SQLite shell. When a code example includes output, it appears directly below the code block. Exercises appear throughout each chapter with solutions hidden in collapsible blocks — try the exercise before revealing the answer.
Terminal commands are shown in bash code blocks. On Windows, equivalent commands are noted where they differ.

title: Information Exploration
text:
Accompanying Materials
All datasets used in this book are available in the accompanying GitHub repository. Each chapter assumes you have downloaded the relevant data files to your working directory. Instructions for setting up your environment appear in 1  Setting Up Your Workspace.

title: Information Exploration
text:
A Note on Difficulty
Some chapters will feel harder than others, and that is not a reflection of your ability. Data work is a craft that rewards patience — you will write code that does not work, read error messages that look like nonsense, and spend an hour tracking down a single stray character. This is completely normal. Every experienced data analyst goes through the same cycle, and the difference between a beginner and a professional is not that professionals do not get stuck. It is that they have learned which moves to try first when they do.
If something in this book feels confusing, slow down rather than push through. Re-read the section. Run the code one line at a time. Print the intermediate results. The concepts in this book will click with practice — often on the third or fourth real project, not the first. Stay with it.

title: Information Exploration
text:
Further Reading






Tip📚 General resources that complement this book






Python documentation — the official Python tutorial is an excellent reference when you need to refresh syntax or a standard-library module.
pandas user guide — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/ is longer than you want it to be but worth bookmarking. The “10 minutes to pandas” page is a useful quick reference.
SQLite documentation — sqlite.org/docs.html includes the full SQL syntax reference. SQLZoo and Mode’s SQL tutorial are both free, interactive, and beginner-friendly.
Real Python — realpython.com publishes high-quality, beginner-friendly tutorials on almost every topic in this book.
“Python for Data Analysis” by Wes McKinney (the creator of pandas) — freely available at wesmckinney.com/book/. A natural next read after this book.

title: Information Exploration
text:
Acknowledgments
This book grew out of the INFO 3401: Information Exploration course at the University of Colorado Boulder. It reflects the contributions of many students whose questions, mistakes, and feedback shaped the material into its current form.

title: 1  Setting Up Your Workspace
text:
1.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 1  Setting Up Your Workspace
text:
Open a terminal on macOS, Windows, and Linux, navigate the file system with pwd, ls/dir, and cd, and distinguish relative from absolute paths.
Organize a data project using data/raw, data/clean, notebooks/, and output/ directories, with machine-friendly filenames and a short README.md.
Write portable relative paths in notebooks instead of brittle absolute paths.
Explain what a Python environment is, and use conda or pip to install and verify packages.
Diagnose command not found, ModuleNotFoundError, and environment-mismatch errors by reasoning about PATH and kernels.
Verify a working SQLite installation on macOS, Windows, and Linux.

title: 1  Setting Up Your Workspace
text:
1.2 Why Setup Matters
A lot of beginning data-science frustration is not really about data science — it’s about the surrounding machinery. Your file manager hides where your files really live. Your editor assumes a different Python than your terminal. Your notebook installs a package that your imports cannot find. This chapter teaches you the paratechnical skills that keep those problems from happening in the first place: how to find your way around the file system, how to lay out a project, and how to know which Python environment is actually running when you press shift+enter.
You are learning a new interface to your own computer. Fluency comes with repetition. If the commands below feel unfamiliar, that’s normal — every experienced analyst was here once, and the muscle memory arrives faster than you’d expect.






TipWant more detail?



The Paratechnical Computing Handbook covers terminal commands, environment management, and project setup in much greater depth. Bookmark it as a reference — you do not need to memorize everything here.

title: 1  Setting Up Your Workspace
text:
1.3 The Terminal
The terminal is a text interface to your computer. Every graphical application — Jupyter Notebook, VS Code, your file manager — is a visual wrapper around operations that happen at the terminal. Learning it directly gives you access to the same operations without the graphical middleman, and it’s how tools like SQLite, git, and package managers are actually designed to be used.

1.3.1 Opening a terminal

macOS — Terminal app (Applications → Utilities, or search with Spotlight).
Windows — Anaconda Prompt from the Start menu. This is a terminal preconfigured with Python access. PowerShell and Windows Terminal also work, but some commands differ.
Linux — your distribution’s terminal emulator.

When the terminal opens, you see a prompt — typically ending in $ on Mac/Linux or > on Windows — followed by a cursor. Type a command, press Enter, and the terminal runs it and prints any output below.


1.3.2 The navigation triad: pwd, ls, cd
Your terminal session is always “inside” one directory, called the working directory. Three commands cover almost all navigation:
pwd                  # "where am I?"  (Windows: cd with no arguments)
ls                   # "what's here?"  (Windows: dir)
cd Documents         # "go there"
cd ..                # "go up one level"
cd ~                 # "go to my home directory"  (Windows: cd %USERPROFILE%)
Build reflex memory for these three. Everything else builds on them.


1.3.3 Absolute vs. relative paths
A relative path starts from your current directory (cd Documents/projects). An absolute path starts from the root of the file system (/Users/you/Documents/projects on Mac, or C:\Users\you\Documents\projects on Windows). Absolute paths work from anywhere; relative paths move when you do.


1.3.4 Creating and viewing files
mkdir my_project                  # make a directory
mkdir -p my_project/data/raw      # make nested directories in one step
cp original.csv backup.csv        # copy a file
mv old.csv new.csv                # move or rename
head -5 my_data.csv               # first 5 lines of a text file
On Windows, the equivalents are copy, move, and Get-Content my_data.csv -Head 5 in PowerShell.


1.3.5 The PATH and command not found
When you type python or sqlite3, your terminal searches a list of directories called the PATH. If the program is not in any of those directories, you get a command not found error — even if it is installed somewhere on your computer. The fix is usually either to install the program through a package manager that updates PATH for you, or to add its directory manually.






WarningThe most common Windows mistake



After installing a new tool or editing PATH, open a new terminal window. The old window will not see the change.

title: 1  Setting Up Your Workspace
text:
1.4 Organizing a Project
A well-organized project is one you can return to after six months and understand immediately. Good structure costs almost nothing to set up and saves enormous time later.

1.4.1 A minimal project structure
my_project/
├── data/
│   ├── raw/           # Original, untouched data files
│   └── clean/         # Cleaned versions ready for analysis
├── notebooks/         # Jupyter notebooks (analysis work)
├── output/            # Tables, figures, exported results
└── README.md          # What this project is about
The key principle is the separation between raw/ and clean/. Raw data files are sacred — never modify them directly. Your notebooks read from raw/, transform, and write results to clean/ or output/. If something goes wrong, you can always start over from the original files.


1.4.2 Naming conventions
Use lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens, and underscores. Avoid spaces, special characters, and accented letters — they cause escaping headaches in the terminal. Include ISO dates (YYYY-MM-DD) when versioning matters, so files sort chronologically. Be specific: colorado-cannabis-sales-by-county-2014-2024.csv tells you exactly what’s inside without opening the file.


1.4.3 The README.md
Every project should have a README.md in its root directory, answering three questions: what is this project about, what data does it use, and how do you run it?
# Cannabis Sales Analysis

Analysis of Colorado cannabis sales by county (2014–2024) for INFO 3401.

## Data

- `data/raw/colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv`: Colorado Department of
  Revenue. Downloaded 2026-02-10.

## Notebooks

- `notebooks/01-loading.ipynb`: Load and inspect raw data.
- `notebooks/02-cleaning.ipynb`: Clean columns, fix types, remove totals.
Writing this file takes five minutes and makes your project immediately legible to anyone — including a future version of yourself.


1.4.4 Relative paths in notebooks
When your notebook lives in my_project/notebooks/ and your data lives in my_project/data/raw/, use a relative path that goes up and back down:
df = pd.read_csv('../data/raw/cannabis_sales.csv')
The .. means “go up one directory.” This path works on any computer with the same project structure. Avoid absolute paths like /Users/you/Documents/my_project/data/raw/cannabis_sales.csv — they break the moment anyone else tries to run your notebook.


1.4.5 .gitignore and version control
As your projects grow, you’ll benefit from git, which tracks every change to every file. Git is beyond the scope of this book, but if you use it, add a .gitignore file listing files you do not want tracked — large raw data files, credentials, and temporary outputs are typical exclusions. GitHub’s Git Handbook is the friendliest starting point.

title: 1  Setting Up Your Workspace
text:
1.5 Python Environments
A Python environment is a specific Python interpreter plus a set of installed packages. When you installed Python — probably through Anaconda — you installed an interpreter along with hundreds of packages (pandas, NumPy, matplotlib, Jupyter, and others) into a specific directory. That collection is your environment.
Understanding environments matters because most “it worked yesterday” problems are really environment problems: a package that’s installed in one environment but missing from the one your notebook is actually using.

1.5.1 conda: the primary package manager
Anaconda ships with conda, which handles installing, updating, and removing packages.
conda --version           # check conda is installed
conda list                # show installed packages
conda install pandas      # install a package
conda update pandas       # update a package
When you install Anaconda, it creates a default environment called base. That is the environment that activates when you open Anaconda Prompt. For the purposes of this book, the base environment is enough. Your prompt usually shows the active environment in parentheses: (base) $.


1.5.2 pip: the fallback
Python ships with a second package manager, pip, which installs packages from the Python Package Index. Prefer conda install when a package is available through conda; fall back on pip install for packages conda does not carry. Mixing the two in one environment can occasionally cause conflicts, so install everything you can with conda first.
pip install some-package
A requirements.txt file lists the packages a project depends on, one per line. If a project includes one, you can recreate its environment with pip install -r requirements.txt.


1.5.3 Jupyter kernels: the hidden bug source
When a Jupyter notebook runs, it uses a kernel — a specific Python environment. If you have multiple environments, each can register its own kernel. A common confusion is having a package installed in one environment but running the notebook with a different environment’s kernel. To check which Python your notebook is actually using, run this in a code cell:
import sys
print(sys.executable)
The output path should point to the Python interpreter inside the environment you expect.


1.5.4 SQLite
SQLite is used in Part III of this book. On macOS and Linux it’s usually preinstalled — check with sqlite3 --version. On Windows, install it through conda with conda install -c anaconda sqlite, or download binaries from sqlite.org/download.html and add the folder to your PATH.






TipWant more detail?



For platform-specific install troubleshooting, PATH editing, and working with more advanced environment managers like mamba or venv, the Paratechnical Computing Handbook goes deeper than this chapter can.

title: 1  Setting Up Your Workspace
text:
1.6 Common Issues in This Chapter






Warningcommand not found or 'cd' is not recognized



What it means: The shell cannot find the program you typed. You may be using a Windows-only command on Mac (or vice versa), the program may not be installed, or it may be installed but not on your PATH.
How to fix it: Confirm the command is correct for your OS (ls on Mac/Linux, dir on Windows Command Prompt). If the program is new, close and reopen your terminal. Re-read the installer’s output for any PATH lines it asked you to add.








WarningFileNotFoundError in a notebook that used to work



What it means: Your notebook’s working directory changed, or the file moved. The relative path no longer resolves.
How to fix it: Run import os; print(os.getcwd()) in the notebook. Confirm the notebook is running from where you expect. Either move the notebook back to notebooks/ or update the relative path.








Warningconda: command not found or 'conda' is not recognized



What it means: Your terminal cannot find the conda executable. On Windows, you’re probably in Command Prompt or PowerShell instead of Anaconda Prompt. On macOS/Linux, conda is not on your PATH.
How to fix it: On Windows, open Anaconda Prompt from the Start menu. On macOS/Linux, run conda init and restart your terminal. See the conda docs if that does not fix it.








WarningModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pandas'



What it means: The package is not installed in the environment your notebook is using — which may not be the environment where you installed it.
How to fix it: Run import sys; print(sys.executable) in a notebook cell. Then in your terminal, confirm that path matches the environment where you installed pandas. If they differ, install pandas in the notebook’s environment, or switch the notebook’s kernel.








WarningA package installed but still fails to import



What it means: The Jupyter kernel started before the install finished, so it still points at the old environment state.
How to fix it: Restart the Jupyter kernel (Kernel → Restart) and rerun the imports. This is one of the most common “ghost” errors and is almost always solved by a kernel restart.

title: 1  Setting Up Your Workspace
text:
1.7 Key Takeaways

The terminal is a text interface to the same operations your graphical tools perform. pwd, ls, and cd are the navigation triad.
Separate raw/ from clean/ data and never modify raw files in place. A five-minute README.md makes your project legible to a future reader.
Use relative paths (../data/raw/file.csv) so your notebook runs on any computer with the same structure.
A Python environment is a specific interpreter plus installed packages. sys.executable tells you definitively which Python your notebook is running.
Most ModuleNotFoundError and command not found issues are environment or PATH problems, not broken code. A fresh terminal or a kernel restart fixes most of them.

title: 1  Setting Up Your Workspace
text:
1.8 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on setup and environments






Paratechnical Computing Handbook — brianckeegan.com/ParatechnicalComputingHandbook/book/ is the companion reference to this chapter with deeper coverage of each topic.
The Missing Semester of Your CS Education (MIT) — missing.csail.mit.edu is an excellent free course on the terminal and developer tooling.
Conda user guide — docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/stable/user-guide/ is the definitive reference.
Cookiecutter Data Science — cookiecutter-data-science.drivendata.org/ is a widely used project template that scales up from the structure in this chapter.
GitHub’s Git Handbook — docs.github.com/get-started/using-git is the friendliest gateway to version control.

title: 1  Setting Up Your Workspace
text:
1.9 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Set up a course project





Open a terminal and create a project directory for this course using the structure described above. Add a README.md describing the project. Download one of the course datasets into data/raw/. Create a notebook in notebooks/ that loads the dataset using a relative path and prints its shape.









NoteExercise 2: Environment audit





Open a terminal and run conda list. Verify that pandas, numpy, matplotlib, and jupyter are installed. Open a Jupyter notebook and run import sys; print(sys.executable) along with import pandas; print(pandas.__version__). Confirm the notebook’s Python matches the environment you audited.









NoteExercise 3: Verify SQLite





Run sqlite3 --version in your terminal. If it reports a version, you’re set. If not, install it via conda install -c anaconda sqlite or follow the platform-specific instructions above. Verify with sqlite3 test.db ".tables" and confirm test.db appears in your directory.

title: 2  Documentation, Markdown, and Errors
text:
2.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 2  Documentation, Markdown, and Errors
text:
Write basic Markdown — headings, emphasis, lists, links, code blocks, and tables — in notebooks, README files, and GitHub.
Structure a notebook with alternating narrative Markdown and code cells using the goal/method/interpretation pattern.
Maintain a Known Issues Log and a Data Dictionary as living documents while you work.
Write code comments that explain why rather than what.
Read a Python traceback from the bottom up and recognize the most common exceptions (FileNotFoundError, KeyError, TypeError, ValueError, AttributeError, ModuleNotFoundError).
Apply a systematic five-step debugging process rather than guessing.

title: 2  Documentation, Markdown, and Errors
text:
2.2 Why This Chapter Matters
Documentation is how you talk to your future self. Error messages are how Python talks back to you. Both are forms of writing, both are forms of diagnostic information, and both are skills that make every other skill in this book easier. When you write clearly about what you are doing and learn to read clearly what the interpreter tells you, “mysterious failures” stop being mysterious.
A persistent fantasy in data work is that you will finish the analysis first and document it later. In practice, “later” never arrives, and by then you have forgotten the reasoning. Why did you drop those 23 rows? Why did you recode "OTH" as missing? Why did you use a left join? Decisions are judgment calls, not mechanical steps; they have to be recorded when you make them, not reconstructed afterward.
If reading a red-text traceback makes your stomach drop, you are in good company. Every programmer feels that at some point. The difference between a beginner and an expert is not that experts stop getting errors — they get plenty. They have built the habit of reading what the error actually says before reacting to the feeling of having broken something.

title: 2  Documentation, Markdown, and Errors
text:
2.3 Markdown Basics
Markdown is a lightweight formatting language used in Jupyter notebooks, README files, GitHub, Quarto, and many other tools. Plain text with a few markers becomes formatted output when rendered.

2.3.1 Headings
Use # symbols for headings. More # symbols mean smaller headings:
# Top-Level Heading
## Section Heading
### Subsection Heading


2.3.2 Emphasis
*italic text*
**bold text**
`inline code`


2.3.3 Lists
Unordered lists use dashes or asterisks; ordered lists use numbers:
- First item
- Second item

1. Step one
2. Step two


2.3.4 Links
[Link text](https://example.com)


2.3.5 Code blocks
For multi-line code, use triple backticks with an optional language identifier:
```python
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv')
df.head()
```


2.3.6 Tables
| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| county | str  | County name |
| year   | int  | Calendar year |
These few elements cover almost everything you need for data documentation. You do not need to learn the full Markdown specification — these basics are enough.

title: 2  Documentation, Markdown, and Errors
text:
2.4 Documentation in Jupyter Notebooks
A Jupyter notebook interleaves Markdown cells with code cells, which makes it the ideal environment for literate analysis — explanations sitting directly alongside the code they describe.

2.4.1 The goal / method / interpretation pattern
Before each code section, add a Markdown cell explaining what you are about to do and why. After a code section that produces output, add a cell that interprets the result. Think of three elements: the goal (what question are you answering?), the method (what operation are you performing?), and the interpretation (what did you learn?).
A notebook with interleaved narrative and code tells a story. A notebook with only code is a puzzle your future self has to solve.


2.4.2 The Known Issues Log
One of the most valuable documentation habits is a running log of problems and decisions. Place a Markdown cell near the top or bottom of your notebook and add entries as you work:
## Known Issues and Cleaning Decisions

### 2026-02-17: Party field standardization
**Problem:** Party stored inconsistently — "Dem", "DEM", "D", "Democrat"
all mean the same thing.
**Fix:** Created party_map dictionary, normalized to "DEM", "REP", "UNA".
**Impact:** Reduced unique values from 47 to 3; no rows lost.
**Remaining issue:** 23 rows have party="OTH" — unclear what this means.
Each entry records four things: problem, fix, impact (especially any data loss), and any remaining uncertainty. Writing the entry before you run the cleaning cell — as a plan, not a post-hoc summary — keeps the log synchronized with the code.


2.4.3 The Data Dictionary
A Data Dictionary describes each column in your dataset. It is the single most useful piece of documentation you can write, and it takes only a few minutes:
| Column | Type | Description | Valid Values | Cleaning Applied |
|--------|------|-------------|--------------|------------------|
| county | str  | County name | 64 CO counties | .str.strip().str.upper() |
| year   | int  | Calendar year | 2014–2024 | pd.to_numeric() |
| sales  | float | Sales in USD | >= 0 | Removed $ and commas |
Update it whenever you add derived columns or recode values. It is a living document, not a one-time artifact.

title: 2  Documentation, Markdown, and Errors
text:
2.5 Comments in Code
Markdown cells handle narrative documentation. Code comments handle the technical layer — explaining why a line of code does what it does, not what (which should be clear from the code itself).
# Good comment: explains WHY
# Replace 0 AB with NaN to avoid division-by-zero in batting average
batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))

# Bad comment: restates WHAT the code does
# Divide H by AB
batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB']
Useful heuristic: if the code does exactly what it says (df.head(), print(x)), it does not need a comment. If it involves a non-obvious decision — why errors='coerce', why a left join, why AB >= 100 — that is worth a comment.

title: 2  Documentation, Markdown, and Errors
text:
2.6 Reading Error Messages
Error messages are not punishments. They are diagnostic information — often the most useful information you’ll receive when something goes wrong. Learning to read them systematically is one of the highest-leverage skills you can develop.
When Python encounters a problem, it produces a traceback — a report showing the sequence of function calls that led to the error, ending with the specific error type and message:
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
FileNotFoundError                         Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[3], line 1
----> 1 df = pd.read_csv('my_data.csv')

File ~/anaconda3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pandas/io/parsers/readers.py:948
    948     return _read(filepath_or_buffer, kwds)

FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'my_data.csv'
Read from the bottom up. The last line tells you what went wrong. The first ----> line tells you which line of your code triggered it. The middle lines are pandas’ internal code — you can usually skip them.
The two most important pieces of information are always at the bottom: the error type (e.g., FileNotFoundError) and the error message (e.g., No such file or directory: 'my_data.csv').

title: 2  Documentation, Markdown, and Errors
text:
2.7 Common Error Types

2.7.1 FileNotFoundError
What it means: Python looked for the file at the path you gave and could not find it there relative to where your code is running.
How to fix it: Check the spelling and capitalization. Verify the working directory with import os; print(os.getcwd()). If the file is in a subdirectory, use a relative path like data/raw/my_data.csv.


2.7.2 KeyError
What it means: You tried to access a column (or dict key) that does not exist. Almost always a spelling or capitalization problem.
How to fix it: Print df.columns.tolist(). Watch for trailing spaces ('County ' vs 'County'), inconsistent capitalization, and unexpected characters.


2.7.3 TypeError
What it means: You tried to perform an operation on a type that does not support it — for instance, adding a number to a string. In pandas, this often happens when a column you expected to be numeric is stored as text because it contains non-numeric values like $1,234 or N/A.
How to fix it: Check the dtype with df['column'].dtype. If it shows object when you expected a number, inspect df['column'].unique() to find the non-numeric entries, then clean them before converting.


2.7.4 ValueError
What it means: A conversion received a value it cannot handle — commonly, trying to cast a column containing NaN to integer type.
How to fix it: Either fill or drop NaNs before converting, or use pandas’ nullable integer type (Int64 with a capital I).


2.7.5 AttributeError
What it means: You tried to use a method that does not exist on that object. For example, .str only works on a Series, not a whole DataFrame.
How to fix it: Confirm you are calling the method on the right object. df['column'].str.strip(), not df.str.strip().


2.7.6 ModuleNotFoundError
What it means: Python cannot find the package you tried to import. Either it is not installed, or your notebook is running in a different environment than the one where you installed it.
How to fix it: conda install pandas or pip install pandas. If it is already installed, confirm that your notebook’s kernel matches the environment (see Chapter 1).

title: 2  Documentation, Markdown, and Errors
text:
2.8 The DtypeWarning
Not an error, but common enough to deserve attention:
DtypeWarning: Columns (3) have mixed types. Specify dtype option on import
or set low_memory=False.
What it means: pandas found both numeric and non-numeric values in the same column and defaulted to storing everything as strings (object type). Arithmetic on that column will not work as expected.
How to fix it: Investigate the column, then clean the non-numeric values or specify the type explicitly at load time with dtype={'col': str}.

title: 2  Documentation, Markdown, and Errors
text:
2.9 A Systematic Debugging Process
When you encounter an error, resist the urge to randomly change things and re-run. Instead:

Read the last line of the traceback. What is the error type? What does the message say?
Read the first arrow line. Which line of your code caused the error?
Reproduce minimally. Can you trigger the same error with a simpler version of the operation? Break chained method calls into individual steps.
Inspect the inputs. Print the shape, dtype, and a sample of the data going into the operation that failed. Is it what you expected?
Search with the error message. Copy the error type and message into a search engine. Someone has almost certainly hit the same problem.







TipA mindset shift



Beginners often feel that getting an error means they did something wrong. Experienced programmers get errors constantly — the difference is that they read the error message, understand what it says, and use it to fix the problem. Errors are information, not failures.

title: 2  Documentation, Markdown, and Errors
text:
2.10 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningYour Markdown renders as raw text instead of formatted output



What it means: The cell in Jupyter is set to “Code” instead of “Markdown.”
How to fix it: Select the cell and press M (in command mode, not edit mode), or use the dropdown at the top of the notebook. Rerun the cell with Shift+Enter.








WarningYour Known Issues Log gets out of sync with the code



What it means: You cleaned something and forgot to log it. Weeks later, you cannot tell why a row count changed.
How to fix it: Write the log entry before running the cleaning cell, as a plan. The log entry is the intent; the code is the execution.








WarningYour notebook is all code with no narrative



What it means: You have a working analysis but no one reading it — including you in three months — can tell what question you were answering or what you concluded.
How to fix it: Add a top-level Markdown cell with the research question. Before each section of code, add a one-sentence goal. After each result, add a one-sentence interpretation.








WarningYou tried every error-fix you could think of and nothing worked



What it means: You’ve stopped reading the error and started guessing. Guessing is slower than reading.
How to fix it: Go back to step 1. Reread the last line of the traceback carefully. Print the inputs to the failing call. If you are still stuck after 15 minutes, search the exact error message verbatim.

title: 2  Documentation, Markdown, and Errors
text:
2.11 Key Takeaways

Documentation is a habit, not a final step. The Known Issues Log and Data Dictionary are living documents you maintain while working.
Markdown is tiny — headings, emphasis, lists, links, code blocks, tables — and covers almost everything you need for data documentation.
Code comments should explain why you made a decision, not what a line of code does.
Read tracebacks from the bottom up. The last line tells you the error type and message. The first arrow line tells you where in your code it happened.
Reproduce minimally. Break chained expressions into single steps. Print the inputs. Search the exact error message.
Errors are feedback, not failures. The same message that feels like a wall today will feel like a map once you have read a few dozen.

title: 2  Documentation, Markdown, and Errors
text:
2.12 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on documentation, Markdown, and debugging






Markdown Guide — markdownguide.org is the cleanest reference; the “Cheat Sheet” page is worth bookmarking.
GitHub Flavored Markdown Spec — github.github.com/gfm/ covers GitHub’s dialect, including task lists and tables.
Python Built-in Exceptions — docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html lists every exception type in the standard library.
Python Errors and Exceptions tutorial — docs.python.org/3/tutorial/errors.html is the official tutorial including try/except.
Real Python: Python Exceptions — realpython.com/python-exceptions/ is an accessible walkthrough with examples.
pandas: Common pitfalls — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/gotchas.html covers pandas-specific behavior that commonly surprises newcomers.

title: 2  Documentation, Markdown, and Errors
text:
2.13 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Write a Known Issues Log entry





Open one of the course datasets and identify a data quality issue (a column with mixed types, an unexpected value, missing data in a key field). Write a Known Issues Log entry using the four-part structure: Problem, Fix, Impact, Remaining Issue.









NoteExercise 2: Build a data dictionary





Choose a dataset you have worked with recently. Write a data dictionary as a Markdown table that describes every column, its type, valid values, and any cleaning that was applied. If you have not cleaned the data yet, note what cleaning you think will be needed.









NoteExercise 3: Diagnose from a traceback





Given the traceback below, identify: (a) the error type, (b) the specific problem described in the message, (c) which line of user code caused the error, and (d) what you would do to fix it.
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
KeyError                                  Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[7], line 1
----> 1 df['Retial_Sales'].sum()

KeyError: 'Retial_Sales'






NoteSolution






KeyError. (b) The column 'Retial_Sales' does not exist — note the typo: “Retial” instead of “Retail”. (c) Line 1 of cell 7. (d) Correct the column name to 'Retail_Sales' — confirm the exact name with df.columns.tolist().

title: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
3.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:
crumbs: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
Translate a vague curiosity into a specific, measurable research question using the “outcome across groups / time / conditions” template.
Identify the unit of analysis for a dataset and recognize common mistakes that flow from misunderstanding it.
Distinguish between-subjects from within-subjects comparisons and cross-sectional from longitudinal analyses.
Explain why observational data supports associations but not causal claims, and name common confounds in public datasets.
crumbs: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs
section: 3.2 Why This Chapter Comes Before the Code
text:
3.2 Why This Chapter Comes Before the Code
The technical chapters ahead teach you to load, clean, extend, and reshape data. Those are essential mechanical skills. But mechanics alone do not produce insight — you also need to ask the right questions and understand what your data can and cannot tell you. This chapter introduces the conceptual vocabulary for formulating research questions with tabular data, recognizing the structure of the comparisons your data supports, and identifying the limitations that prevent you from drawing conclusions the data does not warrant.
Reading this chapter first — before writing a line of pandas or SQL — gives you a frame for the technical work ahead. The datasets you will use, and the analyses you will eventually run, are shaped by the questions you are trying to answer.
None of this requires new code. It requires new thinking.
crumbs: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs
section: 3.3 What Makes a Good Research Question?
text:
3.3 What Makes a Good Research Question?
A good research question is specific enough to be answered with data and interesting enough to be worth answering. Compare these two questions:

“Is baseball different now?” — too vague to answer. Different how? Compared to when?
“Has the league-wide home run rate per at-bat changed between the 1990s and the 2010s?” — specific, measurable, and answerable with historical batting data.

A useful template is: “Is there a difference in [outcome] between [groups] / over [time] / across [conditions]?” The components of this template — outcome, groups, time, conditions — map directly to columns in your data. If you cannot point to specific columns that represent each component, the question is not yet answerable with the data you have.
crumbs: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
3.4 The Unit of Analysis
Before you can answer any question, you need to know what a single row in your dataset represents. This is the unit of analysis, and getting it wrong is one of the most common sources of error in data work.
Consider a baseball-statistics dataset where each row is a player-season-stint: one player’s statistics for one team in one season. If you compute the average batting average across all rows, you are computing the average across player-seasons — which weights a player with 10 at-bats the same as a player with 600. That is almost certainly not what you intend.
Consider a county-population dataset where each row is a county-year-age: one county’s population for one single year of age in one year. If you sum totalpopulation without grouping by county and year first, you will add up every age group and get a number far larger than any county’s actual population.
The discipline you’ll build in later chapters — always checking .shape, always understanding what a row represents — is more than a cleaning habit. It is the foundation of valid analysis.








Example dataset shape
Unit of analysis
Common mistake




Player-level batting stats
Player-team-season
Averaging rates without weighting by at-bats


County population estimates
County-year-age
Summing population without grouping by county-year


Monthly sales by jurisdiction
County-month-year
Treating monthly data as annual without aggregation


Mortality counts by cause
County-year-age group-cause
Double-counting deaths across cause categories
crumbs: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
3.5 Between-Subjects vs. Within-Subjects Comparisons
Most analytical questions involve comparisons, and the structure of the comparison determines which methods are appropriate.
Between-subjects (or between-groups) comparisons look at different entities measured under different conditions. “Do urban counties have higher crime rates than rural counties?” compares different counties — each county is measured once, and the comparison is across groups. In tabular data, this means filtering to different subsets of rows and comparing an outcome column.
Within-subjects (or repeated-measures) comparisons look at the same entity measured at multiple time points or under multiple conditions. “Has Boulder County’s dependency ratio changed from 2000 to 2020?” tracks a single county across time. In tabular data, this means filtering to one entity and examining how an outcome column changes across another column (usually time).
The distinction matters because between-subjects comparisons are confounded by all the ways the groups differ beyond the variable of interest. Urban and rural counties differ in population density, but they also differ in demographics, economic base, healthcare access, and dozens of other factors. A within-subjects comparison controls for many of these differences (Boulder County’s geography does not change between 2000 and 2020), but introduces the possibility that other things changed simultaneously (state policy, national trends).

3.5.1 Recognizing the structure in your data
Ask yourself: “Am I comparing different entities, or the same entity at different times?”

import pandas as pd

# Between-subjects: compare counties in a single year
# Each county appears once → between-subjects
# pop_2020 = pop[pop['year'] == 2020].groupby('county')['totalpopulation'].sum()

# Within-subjects: track one county across years
# Same county appears multiple times → within-subjects
# boulder_trend = pop[pop['county'] == 'Boulder'].groupby('year')['totalpopulation'].sum()
crumbs: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
3.6 Cross-Sectional vs. Longitudinal Analysis
A closely related distinction is whether your analysis examines one point in time or multiple points.
Cross-sectional analysis looks at a snapshot: all counties in 2020, all players in the 2022 season. It is good for describing the current state of things and identifying patterns across entities, but it cannot tell you about change over time.
Longitudinal analysis tracks the same entities across time: Boulder County from 1990 to 2024, league-wide batting averages from 1900 to 2022. It can reveal trends, turning points, and the effects of interventions (a new policy, a rule change), but it requires careful handling of time-related confounds.
Most of the datasets in this book support both types of analysis. The cannabis sales data can be analyzed cross-sectionally (which counties had the highest sales in 2023?) or longitudinally (how have Denver’s sales changed since legalization?). The choice depends on the question, not the data.
crumbs: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs
section: 3.7 Observational Data and the Limits of Causation
text:
3.7 Observational Data and the Limits of Causation
Every dataset in this book is observational — the data was collected by recording what happened, not by experimentally manipulating conditions and measuring the effect. This is the norm in data analysis outside of controlled experiments, and it imposes a fundamental limitation: observational data can show associations, but it cannot prove causation.
Consider a dataset that merges baseball batting statistics with player salaries. Suppose you find that higher-paid players tend to have better batting statistics. Can you conclude that paying players more causes them to hit better? No. Teams pay more for players who have already demonstrated high performance. The causal arrow likely runs from performance to salary, not the other way around. And both might be influenced by a third factor — age, position, market size — that you have not accounted for.
This does not mean observational analysis is useless. It means you must be disciplined about the language you use. Say “counties with higher population density tend to have higher crime rates” rather than “high population density causes higher crime rates.” The first is a description of a pattern in the data. The second is a causal claim that the data cannot support.

3.7.1 Common confounds in the course datasets







Apparent relationship
Possible confound




Higher salary → better batting stats
Teams pay for past performance; age and experience drive both


More cannabis sales → more crime
Both correlate with county population; urban counties have more of both


Higher dependency ratio → lower income
Rural counties have both older populations and less economic diversity


More deaths → larger county
Death counts scale with population; rates are the meaningful comparison



The last example deserves emphasis. Comparing raw counts across groups of different sizes is one of the most common analytical mistakes. Denver will always have more deaths, more crimes, and more cannabis sales than San Juan County — because Denver has 700,000 people and San Juan has 700. The meaningful comparison is the rate: deaths per 100,000 population, crimes per capita, sales per resident. Computing rates requires joining your outcome data to population data — which is why multi-table joins are more than a technical exercise. They are an analytical necessity.
crumbs: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
3.8 Formulating Your Question
Before writing any analytical code, answer these four questions in a Markdown cell:

What is the outcome I am measuring? (Which column? What does a higher or lower value mean?)
What am I comparing? (Groups? Time periods? Conditions?)
What is the unit of analysis? (What does each row represent? Do I need to aggregate first?)
What can I conclude vs. what can I only speculate about? (Association or causation? What confounds exist?)

This takes two minutes and prevents hours of misguided analysis. Do this framing work before writing any analytical code throughout the rest of the book.
crumbs: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
3.9 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningYour research question is too broad to answer



What it means: You wrote something like “Is housing broken?” — there is no specific column you can point to as the outcome, and no clear group you are comparing.
How to fix it: Rewrite using the “outcome across groups / time / conditions” template. Force yourself to name the specific column that represents the outcome and the specific filter that defines the groups. If you cannot, the question is not yet answerable with the data you have.








WarningYour summary statistic does not mean what you think it means



What it means: You computed a mean, median, or sum without accounting for the unit of analysis. Averaging batting averages across player-seasons, or summing population across age groups, gives a number that sounds meaningful but is not.
How to fix it: Before any aggregation, write down what a single row represents. Decide whether you need to aggregate to a different unit first. If you are averaging rates, decide whether to weight by the underlying sample size.








WarningYou compared raw counts across unequal populations



What it means: Denver has more of everything than San Juan County. Conclusions based on raw counts are really conclusions about population size.
How to fix it: Convert to per-capita rates before comparing. “Deaths per 100,000 population” is almost always the right denominator for cross-county comparisons.








WarningYou slipped into causal language



What it means: Your write-up says “X causes Y” when the data only shows that X and Y are correlated.
How to fix it: Reread your conclusions and replace causal verbs (causes, leads to, drives) with correlational language (is associated with, tends to co-occur with). Name at least one plausible confound explicitly so the reader knows you considered it.
crumbs: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
3.10 Key Takeaways

A good research question names a specific outcome, a specific comparison, and a specific unit of analysis.
The unit of analysis — what a single row represents — determines which aggregations are meaningful. Checking .shape is more than hygiene; it is analytical discipline.
Between-subjects vs. within-subjects and cross-sectional vs. longitudinal are two independent distinctions. Know which you are doing before you start.
Observational data supports associations, not causal claims. Use language that matches what your data can actually show.
Per-capita rates, not raw counts, make cross-group comparisons meaningful.
crumbs: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
3.11 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on research questions and study design






“A Field Guide to Lies and Statistics” by Daniel Levitin — an accessible introduction to common statistical fallacies in public reporting.
“Causal Inference: The Mixtape” by Scott Cunningham — mixtape.scunning.com is a free textbook on causal inference, written for people with modest math backgrounds.
Data Skeptic podcast — dataskeptic.com regularly discusses study design and the limits of data-driven conclusions.
Investigative Reporters and Editors: Story ideas from data — ire.org has story-driven examples of good research questions in data journalism.
crumbs: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs
text:
3.12 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Classify the comparison





For each question below, identify whether it is a between-subjects or within-subjects comparison, and whether it is cross-sectional or longitudinal.

Do National League teams hit more home runs than American League teams in 2022?
Has Colorado’s statewide dependency ratio increased from 2000 to 2020?
Is there a correlation between county population and cannabis sales in 2023?
Did batting averages decline after the introduction of the designated hitter rule?







NoteSolution






Between-subjects (different teams), cross-sectional (one season). b. Within-subjects (same state), longitudinal (multiple years). c. Between-subjects (different counties), cross-sectional (one year). d. Within-subjects in a sense (same league), longitudinal (before/after a rule change) — though it is comparing different players across eras, making it partly between-subjects too.













NoteExercise 2: Identify the confound





A colleague shows you a scatter plot demonstrating that Colorado counties with more cannabis dispensaries also have more traffic accidents. They conclude that cannabis causes traffic accidents. Write a paragraph identifying at least two confounds and explaining why this conclusion is not supported by the data.






NoteSolution





Both dispensary count and traffic accidents correlate with county population — larger counties have more of everything. They also correlate with urbanization, road density, and commuter traffic. Without controlling for population (converting to per-capita rates) and other covariates, the correlation between dispensaries and accidents may be entirely explained by the fact that both are proxies for county size. Observational data like this can show association but cannot establish causation.












NoteExercise 3: Formulate a research question





Choose one of the course datasets and write a research question using the template from this chapter. Then answer the four framing questions (outcome, comparison, unit of analysis, limitations). Be specific enough that another student could run the analysis from your description alone.
crumbs: 3  Research Questions and Study Designs

title: 4  Public Interest Data Science
text:
4.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 4  Public Interest Data Science
text:
Identify three dynamics — enclosure, erosion, and exemption — that shape what public data looks like and what it omits.
Recognize the telltale symptoms of each dynamic in real datasets (title blocks, format mismatches, suppression codes, absent records).
Apply the three pillars of public interest practice — openness, oversight, and ownership — using the Known Issues Log, join-quality reports, and Data Dictionary.
Frame your analytical conclusions in language that matches what the data can actually support.

title: 4  Public Interest Data Science
text:
4.2 Why This Chapter Matters
Data analysis is never neutral. Every dataset reflects choices about what to count, how to count it, who gets to count, and who is counted. This chapter introduces a framework for thinking about those choices — not to make you suspicious of data, but to make you a more careful reader of it.
The datasets in this book come from government agencies, public records, and open archives. They are about cannabis sales, voter registration, crime, mortality, and population. They are, in a meaningful sense, about people. Understanding how they were produced — and what was left out — is part of doing the analysis well.

title: 4  Public Interest Data Science
section: 4.3 Three Dynamics That Shape Public Data
text:
4.3 Three Dynamics That Shape Public Data
Public data is shaped by three recurring dynamics that appear, in different forms, in nearly every dataset you will work with in this book.

4.3.1 Enclosure: Data Designed for Humans, Not Machines
Enclosure happens when data that is technically public is formatted in ways that make it difficult to use programmatically. The data exists, but accessing it requires significant effort — and that effort is unevenly distributed.
The Colorado cannabis sales file is a clear example. It is published by a state agency and available to download, but it arrives as an Excel export with a two-row title block at the top, eight footnote rows at the bottom, and sales figures formatted as currency strings ($1,234,567). None of this matters if you are reading the spreadsheet in Excel. All of it matters if you are trying to load it into pandas.
The CDC WONDER database is a more extreme example. Mortality data is available, but it must be requested through a web form that limits what you can query at once. The results are delivered as HTML tables — not CSV files, not an API — which means extracting the data requires additional parsing steps that are not documented or supported.
These are not accidents. They are the result of publishing workflows optimized for human readers in browsers and spreadsheets. The practical effect is that the people who can most easily use this data are those with the technical skills to work around its formatting — a form of enclosure through interface design.






NoteWhat to look for



When you load a new dataset, the problems you encounter in the first few cells — extra header rows, currency strings, aggregated totals, merged cells — are often symptoms of enclosure. The dataset was designed for a different audience than you.




4.3.2 Erosion: Infrastructure Decay Made Concrete
Erosion happens when data infrastructure degrades over time, typically because no single agency is responsible for maintaining consistency across systems.
The county name mismatch in the course datasets is a concrete example. Three Colorado government agencies each publish data about Colorado counties:

The CDC WONDER database formats county names as "Adams County, CO".
The Colorado Bureau of Investigation uses "Adams County".
The Colorado State Demography Office uses bare names: "Adams".

No one decided that these formats should differ. They are the accumulated result of three agencies building their own systems, at different times, with different conventions and no coordination mandate. The mismatch is invisible until you try to join the tables — at which point it silently drops every row, because "Adams County, CO" does not equal "Adams County" does not equal "Adams".
This is infrastructure erosion made concrete: a technical inconvenience that embodies a real governance problem. The cost is borne by anyone who tries to analyze these datasets together — which is exactly the analysis you would want to do if you were studying, say, the relationship between crime rates, death rates, and population in Colorado counties.






NoteWhat to look for



When joining tables from different agencies, always audit your key columns before joining. SELECT DISTINCT county FROM deaths ORDER BY county; run against all three tables will immediately reveal format mismatches that would otherwise produce a silent empty result.




4.3.3 Exemption: What Data Does Not Exist
Exemption is the hardest dynamic to see, because it involves absence rather than presence. It happens when data that would be useful for public accountability is not collected, not retained, or not released.
The CDC suppression rule is a visible example of exemption by policy. When death counts in a county-year-cause cell are below 10, the CDC reports the value as "Suppressed" rather than a number. The stated reason is privacy protection — individuals in a small cell might be identifiable. This is a legitimate concern, but the effect is that the counties with the smallest populations — often rural counties, often with higher rates of specific causes of death — have the most missing data. The places where data would be most informative are the places where data is least available.
Less visible forms of exemption include data that is never collected at all: housing displacement records at the parcel level, detention facility population data, stop-and-frisk incident logs in jurisdictions that do not publish them. These absences are not accidents either. They reflect choices about what is worth measuring and who is worth measuring it for.






NoteWhat to look for



When a column has missing values, ask why. "Suppressed" in the CDC data is not a data quality problem — it is a policy decision. Understanding why data is missing is different from knowing that it is missing, and it matters for how you handle it analytically and how you communicate about it.

title: 4  Public Interest Data Science
section: 4.4 Three Pillars of Public Interest Data Practice
text:
4.4 Three Pillars of Public Interest Data Practice
Understanding these dynamics does not make data analysis impossible. It makes it more honest. Three practices, which appear throughout this book, help translate awareness into better work.

4.4.1 Openness — The Known Issues Log
Openness means making your data problems visible rather than hiding them. The Known Issues Log introduced in Chapter 2 is a transparency tool: a running record of every quality problem you found, what you decided to do about it, and why.
Publishing a Known Issues Log alongside your analysis lets readers evaluate your decisions rather than taking your conclusions on faith. It also protects you: documenting that you knew about a data problem and addressed it deliberately is very different from having it discovered later as an unacknowledged flaw.
Openness is not about being embarrassed by data problems. Every real-world dataset has them. It is about treating data quality as a fact to communicate rather than a weakness to conceal.


4.4.2 Oversight — The Join-Quality Report
Oversight means auditing your data pipelines to catch silent failures. The join-quality report introduced in Chapter 12 is an audit trail: a structured check of what matched, what was dropped, and whether the result is plausible.
A join that silently drops 40% of rows because of a county name format mismatch is not a software bug — it is a logical error that produces a confident-looking wrong answer. Oversight practices — checking row counts before and after joins, validating totals, auditing unmatched records — are what prevent silent errors from becoming invisible conclusions.
In a public interest context, the stakes are higher. An analysis of county-level health outcomes that silently drops rural counties is not only technically wrong — it is systematically biased against the populations that are hardest to count.


4.4.3 Ownership — The Data Dictionary
Ownership means tracking provenance: where data came from, who collected it, when it was accessed, and what decisions were made about it. The Data Dictionary introduced in Chapter 2 is a provenance record.
Provenance matters for reproducibility — someone else should be able to reconstruct your analysis from the same sources. It also matters for accountability. Knowing that a dataset comes from the Colorado Department of Revenue’s Marijuana Enforcement Division, downloaded on a specific date from a specific URL, is different from knowing only that you “got the cannabis data from the state.”
In an era of frequently updated government datasets, provenance records also let you know whether your analysis is based on data that has since been revised or retracted.

title: 4  Public Interest Data Science
section: 4.5 How These Pillars Appear in This Book
text:
4.5 How These Pillars Appear in This Book
The three pillars are not add-ons — they are woven into the recurring practices that appear in every chapter of this book.








Recurring practice
Pillar
Where introduced




Known Issues Log
Openness

Ownership
Chapter 2


Post-load checklist
Openness

Validate after every transformation

throughout

Oversight

County name standardization
Oversight (erosion response)

na_values documentation

Documenting why data is missing
Openness

Reading the technical chapters with these pillars in mind transforms a series of data manipulation exercises into a coherent practice. You are not only learning how to rename columns and join tables. You are learning how to be accountable for what you find.

title: 4  Public Interest Data Science
text:
4.6 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningYou treat missing values as random



What it means: Code that drops NaNs or fills them with a mean assumes the missingness is random. When the missingness is systematic — the CDC suppression rule, for example — that assumption biases your analysis.
How to fix it: Before any missing-value imputation, ask why the value is missing. Document the answer in your Known Issues Log. Treat policy-driven suppression differently from data-entry omissions.








WarningA join silently drops rows because of format mismatches



What it means: You joined two agency tables on county, but one uses "Adams County, CO" and the other uses "Adams". The result is confident-looking, systematically wrong.
How to fix it: Run a join-quality report (Chapter 12): compare row counts before and after the join, and list any unmatched keys. Standardize keys in a cleaning step before joining.








WarningYour analysis does not account for absent data



What it means: The counties or demographic groups with the most missing rows are often the ones your analysis would most benefit from including. Silence about absence is not the same as representativeness.
How to fix it: When you drop or exclude rows, document what was excluded and speculate about what the exclusion means for your conclusions. A limitation section in your writeup is not weakness — it is credibility.

title: 4  Public Interest Data Science
text:
4.7 Key Takeaways

Public data is shaped by three recurring dynamics — enclosure, erosion, and exemption — that are invisible until you know to look for them.
Three practices — openness (Known Issues Log), oversight (join-quality reports), and ownership (Data Dictionary) — translate awareness into better analysis.
The technical habits in this book (inspect before acting, validate after every transform, document your decisions) are not separate from ethics. They are what ethical data practice looks like in code.
Good data work makes absent data and uncertain decisions visible, not invisible.

title: 4  Public Interest Data Science
text:
4.8 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on public interest data practice






“Data Feminism” by D’Ignazio and Klein — a free online edition at data-feminism.mitpress.mit.edu develops this book’s themes at length.
“Weapons of Math Destruction” by Cathy O’Neil — an accessible survey of how opaque algorithms and missing data harm communities.
Ada Lovelace Institute: Data justice — adalovelaceinstitute.org publishes short, readable reports on data governance and equity.
Investigative Reporters and Editors: Data library — ire.org has public-interest datasets and story pitches.
CDC WONDER documentation — wonder.cdc.gov/wonder/help/main.html documents the suppression rules referenced above.

title: 4  Public Interest Data Science
text:
4.9 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Identify the dynamic





For each of the following data problems, identify which dynamic (enclosure, erosion, or exemption) it represents and explain your reasoning:

A county health department publishes monthly reports as PDF scans of printed tables.
Two state agencies use different ZIP code formats — one includes the ZIP+4 suffix, the other does not — making it impossible to join their records directly.
A police department publishes use-of-force data only in aggregate, not at the incident level.
A state unemployment database uses 999 to indicate “not applicable” in some columns and null in others.










NoteExercise 2: The absent dataset





Look at the datasets described in Appendix A. For each dataset, identify one question you would like to answer that the dataset cannot answer — either because the relevant data is not collected, is suppressed, or is not in this dataset. For each gap, suggest what additional data source might fill it and whether that source is likely to be publicly available.









NoteExercise 3: Known Issues Log entry





Load the Colorado cannabis sales dataset as described in Chapter 5. Write a Known Issues Log entry for the suppressed NR values in the sales columns. Your entry should document: (1) what the problem is, (2) which rows are affected, (3) what you decided to do about it, and (4) why that decision is defensible.

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
text:
5.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
text:
Load CSV and Excel files into a pandas DataFrame using pd.read_csv() and pd.read_excel().
Control how pandas interprets a file using header, skipfooter, na_values, sep, dtype, and encoding.
Run the post-load checklist — shape, headers, types, missingness, distributions, duplicates, plausibility, and a quick visual scan — on every new dataset.
Diagnose FileNotFoundError, DtypeWarning, and other loading errors, and choose an appropriate fix.
Navigate pandas’ documentation using help(), the ? operator, and the online API reference.

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
section: 5.2 Why Loading Is Harder Than It Looks
text:
5.2 Why Loading Is Harder Than It Looks
Every analysis begins with loading data — getting information from a file on disk into a pandas DataFrame where you can inspect and manipulate it. This chapter covers the mechanics of loading CSV and Excel files using pd.read_csv() and pd.read_excel(), the parameters that control how pandas interprets the file, and the post-load inspection routine that catches problems before they propagate into your analysis.
Loading data is where most silent errors originate. A column that looks numeric but was loaded as text, a header row mistakenly treated as data, a delimiter that splits values in the wrong places — these problems are invisible unless you actively look for them. The discipline of inspecting data immediately after loading is one of the most valuable habits you will develop in this course.
This chapter builds on the terminal and project-organization skills from Chapter 1. If a pd.read_csv call fails with FileNotFoundError, review that chapter — the fix is almost always a path or working-directory issue.

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
text:
5.3 Setup
You will use three libraries in this chapter: pandas for the DataFrame, numpy for numerical arrays, and matplotlib for the quick visualization at the end.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt







WarningIf you get an ImportError on any of these





If you see ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pandas' (or numpy, or matplotlib), the package is not installed in the Python environment your notebook is using. Install it with:
conda install pandas numpy matplotlib
Or, if you are not using conda:
pip install pandas numpy matplotlib
After installing, restart the Jupyter kernel (Kernel → Restart) and rerun the import cell. If the error persists, the notebook’s kernel is probably pointing at a different environment than the one you installed into — see Chapter 1 for how to check sys.executable and align the two. Full reference: Chapter 2.

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
text:
5.4 How to Read pandas Documentation
Before you run your first load, take a minute to learn how to read the documentation. You will come back to the docs constantly — not because you are a beginner, but because experienced data scientists do the same. Nobody memorizes every parameter of pd.read_csv (there are more than 30).
There are three ways to access documentation from inside a notebook:

The ? operator (IPython / Jupyter only): type pd.read_csv? in a cell and run it. A help panel appears with the function’s signature and docstring.
The help() function (works anywhere): help(pd.read_csv) prints the same information as text.
The online docs: pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.read_csv.html shows the same content formatted for the web, plus examples and cross-references.

A function signature looks like this:
pd.read_csv(filepath_or_buffer, *, sep=NoDefault.no_default, header='infer',
            names=None, index_col=None, usecols=None, dtype=None,
            engine=None, ..., encoding=None, skipfooter=0, ...)
The first argument (filepath_or_buffer) is required — you must pass it. Everything after the * is a keyword argument — it has a default value and you can leave it out unless you need to change it. The documentation tells you what each parameter does, what types it accepts, and what the default behavior is.






TipDocs are a reference, not a tutorial



Pandas documentation is terse by design. It describes behavior, not motivation. When you are learning a new function, skim the signature and the first paragraph, then jump to the examples at the bottom — that is usually the fastest way to understand what the function does. Come back to the parameter list when you hit a specific problem.

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
text:
5.5 A First Load
The Colorado Department of Revenue publishes monthly cannabis sales by county. The data is distributed as a CSV file originally exported from Excel, which means it has some quirks we need to handle. Let’s start with a naive load and see what happens.






TipBefore you run pd.read_csv: where is the file?



pd.read_csv('colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv') looks for the file in your notebook’s current working directory. If you get a FileNotFoundError, the file is not there — the file name is mistyped, the file lives in a subdirectory (data/raw/colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv), or your notebook is running in a different folder than you expected. Run import os; print(os.getcwd()) to confirm where you are. See Chapter 2 for a deeper walkthrough of FileNotFoundError.



df = pd.read_csv('colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv')

Before doing anything else, look at the data.

df.shape


df.head()


df.tail(10)

Something is wrong. The real column headers appear to be on row 2, not row 0. The first two rows contain a title block from the original Excel file. And the bottom of the file likely contains footnotes or totals that are not data rows.
This is a common pattern with government data — the files are formatted for human reading, not for machine ingestion. Your job is to tell pandas how to read the file correctly.

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
section: 5.6 Public Interest Context: Enclosure by Design
text:
5.6 Public Interest Context: Enclosure by Design
The cannabis sales file is technically public — it is published by a Colorado state agency and freely downloadable. But its formatting serves a different audience than you.
