The two-row title block, the eight-row footnote section, and the currency-formatted numbers are there because the file was designed for humans reading it in Excel, not for analysts loading it into pandas. The NR values in the sales columns are a separate form of enclosure: the Colorado Department of Revenue suppresses sales figures for counties with fewer than three active licensees, so the counties with the least cannabis activity — often smaller, rural counties — are also the ones with the least data.
The work you do with header=, skipfooter=, and na_values= is not just a technical step. It is the labor of making nominally “open” data actually usable — labor that is unevenly distributed across the people who nominally have access. When you encounter a file like this, it is worth asking: who can realistically use this dataset as published, and who cannot?






NoteConnection to Chapter 4



This section connects to the concept of enclosure introduced in Chapter 4. Public data formatted for Excel rather than pandas is a form of enclosure by interface — access is technically possible, but practically gated by who has the tooling and skill to clean it.

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
text:
5.7 The Post-Load Checklist
Before you touch any data, run through this checklist. It takes less than a minute and catches the majority of loading problems.

Shape: df.shape — does the row and column count match expectations?
Headers: df.columns.tolist() — are column names unique, non-empty, and meaningful?
Types: df.dtypes — are numbers numeric, dates datetime, and text object?
Missingness: df.isna().sum() — how many missing values per column?
Distributions: df['col'].value_counts() — do categorical values look right?
Duplicates: df.duplicated(subset=[key_cols]).sum() — is there a candidate key?
Plausibility: df.describe() — any impossible ranges, negative counts, future dates?
Quick look: df.head(), df.tail(), df.sample(5) — do rows look right?

You will apply this checklist in every loading chapter in this book, in both Python and SQL. Memorize it.

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
section: 5.8 Fixing the Load with read_csv Parameters
text:
5.8 Fixing the Load with read_csv Parameters
The pd.read_csv() function has dozens of parameters that control how the file is interpreted. For most real-world files, you will need to specify at least a few of them. Here are the most important ones.

5.8.1 header: where the column names are
If the header row is not the first row of the file, specify its zero-indexed position:

df = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2
)

df.head()



5.8.2 skipfooter: removing trailing rows
Government data files often have footnotes, totals, or metadata appended to the bottom. The skipfooter parameter removes a specified number of rows from the end. Note that skipfooter requires setting engine='python' because the default C parser does not support it.

df = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2,
    skipfooter=8,
    engine='python'
)

df.tail()



5.8.3 na_values: declaring custom missing value codes
Many datasets use codes like NR (Not Reported), -999, ., or empty strings to represent missing values. The na_values parameter tells pandas to treat these as NaN:

df = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2,
    skipfooter=8,
    engine='python',
    na_values=['NR']
)

df.tail()



5.8.4 sep: specifying the delimiter
CSV stands for “comma-separated values,” but many files use other delimiters — tabs (\t), pipes (|), or semicolons (;). If your file uses something other than a comma, specify it with sep:

# Example: pipe-delimited file
# df = pd.read_csv('voter_data.txt', sep='|')



5.8.5 dtype: controlling column types
By default, pandas guesses each column’s type. You can override this with a dictionary mapping column names to types. This is especially useful for columns that look numeric but should remain strings — like zip codes or ID numbers that have leading zeros:

# Example: preserve leading zeros in zip codes
# df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', dtype={'zip_code': str, 'voter_id': str})

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
text:
5.9 Building Up Iteratively
Notice the pattern above: you load the file, check the result, add a parameter to fix a problem, and reload. This iterative approach — get something to work, then move on to the next issue — is how experienced analysts work with messy files.
In a finished notebook, you would consolidate everything into a single cell. But when you are first working with a new dataset, spread the steps across multiple cells so you can see the effect of each parameter.

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
section: 5.10 What the Parameters Fixed — and What Remains
text:
5.10 What the Parameters Fixed — and What Remains
The read_csv parameters handle structural problems at the boundary between file and DataFrame: they put the header in the right place, skip rows that are not data, and mark sentinel values as missing. Run the checklist again to see what they accomplished:






NoteFrom loading to cleaning



The column names, currency formatting, and statewide aggregate rows still need attention. Those are post-load cleaning operations — renaming columns, stripping currency symbols, filtering rows — covered in Chapter 6, which begins by completing this dataset before moving to more complex cleaning scenarios.

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
text:
5.11 Running the Post-Load Checklist

print('Shape:', df.shape)
print('\nDtypes:')
print(df.dtypes)
print('\nMissing values:')
print(df.isna().sum())


for col in ['Month', 'Year', 'County']:
    print(df[col].value_counts(), '\n\n')


for col in ['Medical', 'Retail']:
    print(df[col].describe(), '\n\n')

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
section: 5.12 A Quick Preview: What You Can Do with Clean Data
text:
5.12 A Quick Preview: What You Can Do with Clean Data
With the data loaded and cleaned, you can immediately begin exploring. Here is a pivot table and a simple visualization — both of which we will cover in depth in later chapters:

pivot_df = pd.pivot_table(
    data=df,
    index='County',
    columns='Year',
    values='Retail',
    aggfunc='sum'
)

pivot_df.head()


pivot_df.loc['Las Animas'].plot(title='Las Animas County Retail Cannabis Sales by Year')

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
section: 5.13 The read_csv Parameters You Should Know
text:
5.13 The read_csv Parameters You Should Know








Parameter
Purpose
Example




header
Which row contains column names
header=2


skiprows
Rows to skip at the top
skiprows=3


skipfooter
Rows to skip at the bottom
skipfooter=8 (requires engine='python')


sep
Column delimiter
sep='|', sep='\t'


na_values
Custom missing value codes
na_values=['NR', '-999', '.']


dtype
Force column types
dtype={'zip': str}


encoding
Character encoding
encoding='latin-1'


usecols
Load only specific columns
usecols=['County', 'Year', 'Sales']

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
text:
5.14 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningFileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory



What it means: Pandas looked for the file at the path you gave and did not find it.
How to fix it: Run import os; print(os.getcwd()) to see where your notebook is looking. Confirm the file name spelling and extension. Use a relative path like '../data/raw/filename.csv' if the file lives in a sibling directory. See Chapter 2.








WarningDtypeWarning: Columns (N) have mixed types



What it means: Pandas found both numeric and non-numeric values in the same column and fell back to object (string) type. Your later arithmetic will silently fail.
How to fix it: Inspect the column with df['col'].value_counts(). If the mixing comes from a stray header row or footer, set skiprows or skipfooter. If the column has sentinel codes like NR or -999, pass them via na_values. If you want to force the type, use dtype={'col': str} (or float, int, etc.).








WarningUnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte



What it means: The file uses a non-UTF-8 encoding (common in files exported from older Windows software or government systems).
How to fix it: Try encoding='latin-1' or encoding='cp1252' in your pd.read_csv call. If you are on the command line, file my_data.csv (macOS/Linux) reports the encoding. Re-save the file as UTF-8 if you will use it repeatedly.








WarningNumbers import as strings because of $ or ,



What it means: A column like $1,234,567 is stored as text. df['Retail'] + 100 will concatenate a string rather than add a number.
How to fix it: Strip the currency/comma characters, then convert: df['Retail'] = pd.to_numeric(df['Retail'].str.replace(r'[$,]', '', regex=True), errors='coerce'). See Chapter 6 for the full pattern.

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
text:
5.15 Key Takeaways

pd.read_csv and pd.read_excel have dozens of parameters; you rarely need all of them, but knowing header, skipfooter, na_values, sep, dtype, and encoding will cover most real-world files.
Run the post-load checklist on every new dataset: shape, headers, types, missingness, distributions, duplicates, plausibility, head/tail/sample.
Load → inspect → adjust → reload. This iterative pattern is how experienced analysts work with messy files.
Loading errors are the easiest class of errors to fix because the diagnostic message is usually precise. When in doubt, read the error, check the docs, and consult Chapter 2.
Documentation is a reference, not a tutorial. Get comfortable with help(), ?, and the online API page — they will save you hours over the course.

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
text:
5.16 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on loading data with pandas






pandas IO tools guide — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/io.html covers every input/output format pandas supports (CSV, Excel, JSON, Parquet, SQL, and more).
pd.read_csv full reference — pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.read_csv.html is the canonical source on every parameter.
pandas cheat sheet (PDF) — pandas.pydata.org/Pandas_Cheat_Sheet.pdf is the official one-page quick reference.
Real Python: Reading and Writing CSVs With Pandas — realpython.com/pandas-read-write-files/ is an accessible tutorial with worked examples.
10 Minutes to pandas — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/10min.html is the official whirlwind tour. Worth reading every few weeks until every section feels familiar.

title: 5  Loading Data with pandas
text:
5.17 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Load a pipe-delimited file





Load the Boulder County voter data file (boco_voter_data.txt), which uses a pipe (|) delimiter. Run the post-load checklist. How many rows and columns does it have? What are the column dtypes? Are there any columns that should be loaded as strings but were guessed as numeric?






NoteSolution






df = pd.read_csv('boco_voter_data.txt', sep='|')
print('Shape:', df.shape)
print('\nDtypes:')
print(df.dtypes)
print('\nFirst few rows:')
df.head()

Columns like RES_ZIP_CODE, VOTER_ID, and PRECINCT may contain leading zeros and should be loaded as strings using the dtype parameter.












NoteExercise 2: Diagnose a mixed-type warning





Load the Colorado State Demography Office population file (sya-county.csv). You may see a DtypeWarning about mixed types. Investigate which column triggered the warning by checking .dtypes and inspecting the column’s unique values. What non-numeric value is present, and what should you do about it?






NoteSolution






sya_df = pd.read_csv('sya-county.csv', header=1)
print(sya_df.dtypes)
print(sya_df.head())

The DtypeWarning typically indicates that a column contains both numbers and text (such as a header row mixed into the data, or a metadata row). Investigate with .value_counts() and address by specifying dtype on import or by filtering out the problematic rows.












NoteExercise 3: Build a complete load cell





Choose one of the course datasets and write a single cell that loads it cleanly — specifying all necessary read_csv parameters, renaming columns to clean names, converting types as needed, and removing any non-data rows. End the cell by printing the shape and a sample of rows.

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
Convert string columns to numeric values using pd.to_numeric and interpret the errors parameter (coerce, ignore, raise).
Normalize text columns using the pandas .str accessor (.str.strip(), .str.upper(), .str.replace()).
Standardize categorical columns using .replace() and .map(), recognizing when each is appropriate.
Detect and handle duplicate rows with df.duplicated() and df.drop_duplicates().
Validate every transformation with before/after value_counts() and null-count comparisons.
Write reusable cleaning functions that return both the transformed data and a summary of what changed.

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.2 Why Cleaning Is the Work
Loading data gets it into your environment. Cleaning data makes it usable. This chapter covers the systematic process of identifying and fixing data quality problems — type mismatches, inconsistent categories, duplicate records, hidden sentinel values, and the ever-present challenge of missing data. The techniques here map directly to operations you may have performed in spreadsheets, but pandas gives you the ability to apply them reproducibly, document them precisely, and scale them to datasets too large for any spreadsheet.
The central discipline of this chapter is validation: after every transformation, you check that the data changed in the way you intended and that nothing was silently lost or corrupted.
If this chapter starts to feel like a lot of moving parts, that is a fair reaction. Data cleaning is not one technique — it is a collection of small patterns that you apply as needed. Expect to reread sections; expect to copy snippets; expect your first attempt at any new dataset to take longer than you thought. This is a normal part of becoming fluent.

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.3 Setup
This chapter uses pandas, numpy, and matplotlib.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

pd.options.display.max_columns = 100
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt







WarningIf you get an ImportError on any of these





If any of numpy, pandas, or matplotlib is missing, install it with:
conda install numpy pandas matplotlib
Or with pip: pip install numpy pandas matplotlib. After installing, restart the kernel before re-running the import cell. See Chapter 1 for how to check which Python environment your notebook is using.

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.4 Completing the Cannabis Load
Before introducing the voter registration data, we complete the cannabis dataset cleaning started in Chapter 5. That chapter used read_csv parameters to fix the header, skip footers, and mark NR as missing. Four post-load operations remain: fixing column names, stripping whitespace, converting currency strings to numbers, and removing aggregate rows. This dataset is a simple cleaning scenario — each fix can be applied in a single line.

6.4.1 Fixing column names
The read_csv call in Chapter 5 produced auto-generated integer column names. Replace them with descriptive names:

import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2,
    skipfooter=8,
    engine='python',
    na_values=['NR']
)

df.columns = ['Month', 'Year', 'County', 'Medical', 'Retail']
df.tail()



6.4.2 Stripping whitespace
County names contain leading or trailing whitespace that would cause join failures later:

df['County'] = df['County'].str.strip()



6.4.3 Converting currency strings to numbers
The sales columns contain values like $1,234,567 — dollar signs and commas that prevent numeric operations. The .str.replace() method removes them, and pd.to_numeric() converts the result:

for col in ['Medical', 'Retail']:
    df[col] = pd.to_numeric(
        df[col]
        .str.replace('$', '', regex=False)
        .str.replace(',', '', regex=False)
    )

df.tail()







NoteThe leading-dot style



Notice the chain of .str.replace() calls written on separate lines. This “leading dot” style makes method chains easier to read and debug — you can comment out individual steps to isolate problems.




6.4.4 Removing aggregate rows
The dataset includes rows where County == 'Total' — monthly statewide totals that would corrupt any county-level analysis:

df = df[df['County'] != 'Total']



6.4.5 The complete load cell
In a finished notebook, all of these steps belong in a single reproducible cell:

df = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2,
    skipfooter=8,
    engine='python',
    na_values=['NR']
)

df.columns = ['Month', 'Year', 'County', 'Medical', 'Retail']
df['County'] = df['County'].str.strip()

for col in ['Medical', 'Retail']:
    df[col] = pd.to_numeric(
        df[col]
        .str.replace('$', '', regex=False)
        .str.replace(',', '', regex=False)
    )

df = df[df['County'] != 'Total']
df.tail()

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.5 Loading the Voter Registration Data
We now turn to a more complex example. Boulder County voter registration data arrives as a pipe-delimited text file with a mix of data types, inconsistent categories, and the kinds of quality issues that appear in virtually every real-world dataset.

df = pd.read_csv('boco_voter_data.txt', sep='|')
df.head()

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.6 Numbers Stored as Text
One of the most common data quality problems is numeric values that pandas loaded as strings. This happens when a column contains any non-numeric values — even one — or when leading zeros need to be preserved (zip codes, ID numbers).
In a spreadsheet, you might notice this when numbers sort alphabetically (1, 10, 100, 2, 20) instead of numerically, or when formulas like SUM() return zero. In pandas, the symptom is a column with dtype: object that you expected to be numeric.

6.6.1 Forcing types at load time
The safest approach is to declare column types when you load the file, before pandas has a chance to guess:

dtype_d = {
    'RES_ZIP_CODE': str,
    'BALLOT_ZIP': str,
    'VOTER_ID': str,
    'PRECINCT': str,
}

df = pd.read_csv('boco_voter_data.txt', sep='|', dtype=dtype_d)



6.6.2 Converting after loading
If you need to convert a column from string to numeric after loading, use pd.to_numeric() with the errors parameter to control how conversion failures are handled:

df['YOB'] = pd.to_numeric(df['YOB'], errors='coerce')

The three options for errors each serve a different purpose. Using 'coerce' converts unparseable values to NaN, which is safe but loses information about what the original values were. Using 'ignore' leaves the column unchanged if any conversion fails, preserving data but keeping the column as strings. Using 'raise' throws an error on the first bad value, which is useful for debugging because it tells you exactly which value caused the problem.
Always check what you lost after coercing:

nulls_before = df['VOTER_ID'].isna().sum()
df['VOTER_ID_CLEAN'] = pd.to_numeric(df['VOTER_ID'], errors='coerce')
nulls_after = df['VOTER_ID_CLEAN'].isna().sum()
print(f"Conversion introduced {nulls_after - nulls_before} new nulls")

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.7 String Normalization
Text fields in real data are messy in predictable ways: inconsistent capitalization, extra whitespace, non-printing characters, and encoding artifacts. Pandas provides the .str accessor for vectorized string operations — the same operations you would apply with TRIM(), UPPER(), and CLEAN() in a spreadsheet, but applied to an entire column at once.

6.7.1 Trimming and case standardization

# Chain operations for readability
df['STREET_NAME'] = (
    df['STREET_NAME']
    .str.strip()                              # remove leading/trailing whitespace
    .str.upper()                              # standardize casing
    .str.replace(r'\s+', ' ', regex=True)     # collapse internal whitespace
)



6.7.2 The validation pattern
Always compare value_counts() before and after cleaning to verify the transformation did what you intended:

# Before cleaning
print(df['RES_CITY'].value_counts())

# After cleaning
df['RES_CITY'] = df['RES_CITY'].str.strip().str.upper()
print(df['RES_CITY'].value_counts())

# Check for unintended consequences
print('New nulls:', df['RES_CITY'].isna().sum())



6.7.3 Common string artifacts
Real-world data contains predictable string problems that survive import and need active cleaning. Extra whitespace includes more than leading and trailing spaces: it also includes non-breaking spaces (Unicode U+00A0), which are common in web-scraped data, and tabs or newlines embedded in fields. Inconsistent punctuation appears as variations like "Boulder, CO" vs. "Boulder CO" vs. "Boulder,CO", or "St. Louis" vs. "St Louis" vs. "Saint Louis". Encoding artifacts show up as garbled characters like "resumÃ©" (UTF-8 decoded as Latin-1) or "caf\xc3\xa9".

# Remove specific characters
df['VOTER_NAME'] = df['VOTER_NAME'].str.replace(',', '', regex=False)

# Replace non-breaking spaces with regular spaces
df['VOTER_NAME'] = df['VOTER_NAME'].str.replace('\xa0', ' ', regex=False)

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.8 Categorical Standardization
When a field has a controlled set of valid values — party affiliations, state abbreviations, product categories — the values in your data rarely match that set exactly. You will find abbreviations, misspellings, inconsistent capitalization, and historical codes that need to be mapped to a standard vocabulary.

6.8.1 Dictionary-based recoding with .replace()
The .replace() method takes a dictionary that maps old values to new values. Unmapped values are left unchanged:

df['PARTY'].value_counts()


party_map = {
    'DEM': 'DEM',
    'REP': 'REP',
    'UAF': 'UNA',
    'LBR': 'LBR',
    'GRN': 'GRN',
}

df['PARTY'] = df['PARTY'].str.strip().str.upper()
df['PARTY'] = df['PARTY'].replace(party_map)

# Check for unmapped values
print(df['PARTY'].value_counts())

Build the mapping dictionary by examining .value_counts() output, not by guessing. The data tells you which variants exist; your job is to decide which standard value each variant maps to.


6.8.2 .map() vs. .replace()
The .map() method is stricter: it replaces everything, and unmapped values become NaN. Use .map() when you have a controlled vocabulary and anything outside it is an error:

valid_parties = {
    'DEM': 'Democrat',
    'REP': 'Republican',
    'UNA': 'Unaffiliated',
    'LBR': 'Libertarian',
    'GRN': 'Green'
}

df['PARTY_LABEL'] = df['PARTY'].map(valid_parties)

# Find codes with no mapping
missing_labels = df[df['PARTY_LABEL'].isna() & df['PARTY'].notna()]
print(missing_labels['PARTY'].value_counts())

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.9 Sentinel Values
Sentinel values are fake entries that masquerade as real data. They are placeholders inserted to represent missing information without leaving a cell blank. Common examples include -999, 9999, or -1 in numeric fields, and "N/A", ".", "-", or "unknown" in text fields. Dates like 01/01/1900 or 12/31/9999 are date sentinels.
You can declare sentinel values at load time:

# df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', na_values=['-999', '9999', '-1', 'N/A', '.', '-', 'unknown'])

Or replace them after loading:

sentinel_to_null = {-999: np.nan, 9999: np.nan, -1: np.nan}
# df = df.replace(sentinel_to_null)

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.10 Handling Duplicates
Duplicate records are rows that share the same values on one or more key columns. They arise from data entry errors, overlapping date ranges in source files, or deliberate denormalization. The critical question is always: are these duplicates errors, or do they represent legitimately repeated observations?

6.10.1 Identifying duplicates

# Fully identical rows
full_dupes = df.duplicated()
print(f"Fully identical rows: {full_dupes.sum()}")

# Duplicates on key columns
key_dupes = df.duplicated(subset=['VOTER_ID'], keep=False)
print(f"Rows with duplicate VOTER_ID: {key_dupes.sum()}")



6.10.2 Understanding the pattern
Before removing duplicates, examine what varies across the repeated rows:

key_counts = df['VOTER_ID'].value_counts()
example_id = key_counts[key_counts > 1].index[0]
example_rows = df[df['VOTER_ID'] == example_id]

for col in df.columns:
    if example_rows[col].nunique() > 1:
        print(f"{col} varies: {example_rows[col].unique()}")



6.10.3 Three strategies for deduplication
Keep first or last when a more recent record supersedes an earlier one:

# df_deduped = df.sort_values('SALE_DATE').drop_duplicates(subset=['ACCOUNTNO'], keep='last')

Aggregate when duplicates represent multiple measurements:

# df_agg = df.groupby('ACCOUNTNO', as_index=False).agg({'SALE_PRICE': 'last', 'SALE_DATE': 'max'})

Flag for review when you are not sure what is right:

df['is_duplicate'] = df.duplicated(subset=['VOTER_ID'], keep=False)
df_clean = df[~df['is_duplicate']]
df_review = df[df['is_duplicate']].sort_values('VOTER_ID')
print(f"Clean: {len(df_clean)}, Needs review: {len(df_review)}")

Always audit totals before and after deduplication to understand the impact.

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.11 Extracting Structured Data from Text
A common problem is multiple pieces of information crammed into one field — an address field containing street, city, state, and zip code, or a name field containing first and last names.

6.11.1 Splitting on a delimiter

# Split a name column into parts
df['VOTER_NAME'].str.split(' ', expand=True).head()

The expand=True parameter returns a DataFrame instead of a Series of lists, which makes it easy to assign the parts to new columns.


6.11.2 Regular expression extraction
For more complex patterns, use .str.extract() with a named capture group regex:

# Example: extract components from a structured address
# pattern = r'^(?P<street>.*?),?\s*(?P<city>[A-Za-z\s]+),?\s*(?P<state>[A-Z]{2})\s*(?P<zip>\d{5})$'
# extracted = df['full_address'].str.extract(pattern)

Always check the extraction success rate — how many rows matched the pattern? — and investigate the failures.

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.12 Building Reusable Cleaning Functions
When you find yourself repeating the same cleaning logic across multiple columns or datasets, wrap it in a function. Functions reduce copy-paste errors, make the cleaning logic self-documenting, and enable systematic testing.

def clean_currency_column(df, col_name, new_col_name=None):
    """
    Remove currency symbols and commas, convert to numeric.

    Parameters:
        df: DataFrame
        col_name: name of column to clean
        new_col_name: name for cleaned column (default: col_name + '_clean')

    Returns:
        DataFrame with new cleaned column added.
        Prints summary of changes.
    """
    if new_col_name is None:
        new_col_name = col_name + '_clean'

    nulls_before = df[col_name].isna().sum()

    cleaned = (
        df[col_name]
        .astype(str)
        .str.replace('$', '', regex=False)
        .str.replace(',', '', regex=False)
        .str.strip()
    )

    df[new_col_name] = pd.to_numeric(cleaned, errors='coerce')

    nulls_after = df[new_col_name].isna().sum()
    nulls_introduced = nulls_after - nulls_before

    print(f"Cleaned {col_name} → {new_col_name}")
    print(f"  Nulls before: {nulls_before}")
    print(f"  Nulls after: {nulls_after}")
    print(f"  Conversion failures: {nulls_introduced} ({nulls_introduced/len(df):.2%})")

    return df

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
section: 6.13 Documentation: Known Issues Log and Data Dictionary
text:
6.13 Documentation: Known Issues Log and Data Dictionary
Every cleaning session should produce two documentation artifacts. The Known Issues Log records each problem you found, how you fixed it, and what impact the fix had — see Chapter 2 for the full format. The Data Dictionary describes every column in the cleaned dataset, including its type, valid values, and any cleaning that was applied.
These are not bureaucratic exercises. They are the only reliable way to remember your decisions when you return to the analysis later or when someone else needs to reproduce your work.

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.14 Public Interest Context: Openness
Every cleaning decision is an analytical choice. Stripping $ and , from a sales column is mechanical; deciding that "OTH" rows should be dropped, or that NR should map to NaN, or that two rows with the same VOTER_ID should be treated as duplicates, are not. They are judgments that shape the conclusions downstream — and they are invisible to anyone who only sees your final DataFrame.
The Known Issues Log from Chapter 2 is the practice that makes these judgments visible. A cleaning run that leaves no record is a black box: later readers cannot evaluate whether the drops, recodings, and deduplications were reasonable. Treat your cleaning cells as if an auditor will review them tomorrow, and write down each decision as you make it. Which of the cleaning moves you performed above would you defend in writing, and which are you relying on being unnoticed?






NoteConnection to Chapter 4



This section connects to the concept of openness introduced in Chapter 4. Openness in public interest data work means making your cleaning decisions visible so that readers can evaluate them — the Known Issues Log is that practice in code.

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.15 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningAttributeError: Can only use .str accessor with string values



What it means: You called .str.strip() (or similar) on a column whose dtype is not object. The column was probably loaded as numeric or mixed.
How to fix it: Check df['col'].dtype. If the column really is strings with one stray number, cast with .astype(str) first. If the column is genuinely numeric, you do not need the .str accessor at all.








Warningpd.to_numeric returns all NaN



What it means: Every value in the column failed to parse as a number — usually because a currency symbol or comma is still present.
How to fix it: Strip the currency characters first: df['col'].str.replace(r'[$,]', '', regex=True), then call pd.to_numeric(..., errors='coerce'). Inspect df['col'].unique()[:20] to confirm what values remain.








Warning.map() turned some values into NaN



What it means: .map() replaces every value using the dictionary — anything not in the dictionary becomes NaN. You almost certainly wanted .replace(), which only changes the mapped values and leaves the rest alone.
How to fix it: If you want to leave unmapped values untouched, switch to .replace(). If you want NaN as a way to detect unexpected values (a data-quality check), .map() is the right tool — document the unmapped values in your Known Issues Log.








Warningdrop_duplicates dropped more (or fewer) rows than expected



What it means: The default compares all columns. If you only care about a subset of identifying columns, you need to pass them explicitly.
How to fix it: Use df.drop_duplicates(subset=['VOTER_ID']). Always print df.shape before and after and inspect the diff. If the drop count surprises you, investigate a few of the dropped rows with df[df.duplicated(subset=['VOTER_ID'], keep=False)].

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.16 Key Takeaways

Every real dataset needs cleaning. The problems are predictable: mixed types, sentinel values, whitespace, inconsistent categories, duplicates.
Validate every transformation. Compare value_counts() and null counts before and after. A transformation that silently changes your row count is a bug waiting to surface downstream.
Use .replace() when you want to leave unmapped values alone; use .map() when anything outside your controlled vocabulary should become NaN.
Write reusable cleaning functions. If you are repeating the same five lines on three columns, that is a signal to abstract.
Your Known Issues Log and Data Dictionary are as important as the code itself — they record your analytical judgment so it can be reviewed later.

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.17 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on data cleaning with pandas






pandas: Working with text data — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/text.html is the canonical reference on the .str accessor.
pandas: Working with missing data — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/missing_data.html covers NaN, NA, and the nullable integer/boolean types.
pandas: Duplicate labels and duplicated rows — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/duplicates.html is the reference on dedup APIs.
Real Python: The pandas DataFrame — realpython.com/pandas-dataframe/ walks through selection, filtering, and assignment patterns used throughout this chapter.
regex101 — regex101.com is an interactive regex tester that explains what each part of a pattern does. Essential when you use .str.extract() or .str.replace() with regex.

title: 6  Cleaning Data with pandas
text:
6.18 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Clean the property data





Load the Boulder County residential assessment data (ar-rec-2025-residential.xlsx). Identify at least three data quality issues using the post-load checklist. Clean them, validating each transformation by comparing value_counts() or null counts before and after.






NoteSolution






arc_df = pd.read_excel('ar-rec-2025-residential.xlsx')
print('Shape:', arc_df.shape)
print('\nDtypes:')
print(arc_df.dtypes)

# Issue 1: ACCOUNTNO has trailing whitespace
arc_df['ACCOUNTNO'] = arc_df['ACCOUNTNO'].str.strip()

# Issue 2: Duplicates on ACCOUNTNO
print(f"\nDuplicate ACCOUNTNO values: {arc_df.duplicated(subset=['ACCOUNTNO']).sum()}")

# Issue 3: Check for mixed-type or sentinel values in numeric columns
print('\nSALE_PRICE sample:')
print(arc_df['SALE_PRICE'].describe())













NoteExercise 2: Write a categorical standardization function





Write a function called standardize_categorical() that takes a DataFrame, a column name, and a mapping dictionary, and returns the DataFrame with a new standardized column. The function should print a before-and-after comparison of value_counts() and report how many values were not covered by the mapping.






NoteSolution






def standardize_categorical(df, col_name, mapping_dict, new_col_name=None):
    if new_col_name is None:
        new_col_name = col_name + '_std'

    print(f"Before ({col_name}):")
    print(df[col_name].value_counts())

    df[new_col_name] = df[col_name].replace(mapping_dict)

    valid_values = set(mapping_dict.values())
    unmapped = df[~df[new_col_name].isin(valid_values)]

    print(f"\nAfter ({new_col_name}):")
    print(df[new_col_name].value_counts())
    print(f"\nUnmapped rows: {len(unmapped)} ({len(unmapped)/len(df):.1%})")

    return df

title: 7  Extending Data with pandas
text:
7.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 7  Extending Data with pandas
text:
Create derived columns using arithmetic, conditional logic, and pd.cut binning.
Parse dates from integer columns using pd.to_datetime and apply time-series operations like resampling.
Compute grouped summaries using df.groupby() with .agg().
Choose between groupby().agg(), pivot_table, and pivot based on whether aggregation and reshaping are needed.
Distinguish .agg() (one row per group) from .transform() (same shape as input) and pick the right tool for a given question.

title: 7  Extending Data with pandas
text:
7.2 Why Extending Matters
Loading and cleaning produce a reliable dataset. Extending transforms that dataset into something that can answer questions. This chapter covers two core operations: creating derived columns from existing data, and computing grouped summaries that collapse many rows into meaningful aggregates. These are the programmatic equivalents of writing formulas and building pivot tables in a spreadsheet — but with the power to handle millions of rows, chain operations together, and reproduce every step exactly.
This chapter assumes you have completed Chapter 6. The batting and population data used here must be loaded and cleaned before extending.

title: 7  Extending Data with pandas
text:
7.3 Setup
This chapter uses pandas and numpy.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.3f}'.format)







WarningIf you get an ImportError





Install missing packages with conda install pandas numpy (or pip install pandas numpy) and restart the Jupyter kernel. See Chapter 1 for how to verify your notebook’s kernel matches the environment where you installed.

title: 7  Extending Data with pandas
text:
7.4 Datasets
This chapter uses two datasets that illustrate different aspects of extending data.
The Lahman Baseball Database contains historical statistics for every Major League Baseball player and team. Each row in the Batting table represents one player’s performance for one team in one season. We will compute derived metrics like batting average and slugging percentage, then aggregate them across teams and decades.
The Colorado State Demography Office publishes single-year-of-age population estimates by county. Each row represents one county, one year, and one single-year age group. We will bin ages into demographic bands and compute the dependency ratio — a standard population metric.

batting = pd.read_csv('Batting.csv')

print('Shape:', batting.shape)
print('Year range:', batting['yearID'].min(), '–', batting['yearID'].max())
batting.head()


url = 'https://storage.googleapis.com/co-publicdata/sya-county.csv'
pop = pd.read_csv(url, skiprows=1, dtype={'countyfips': str})

print('Shape:', pop.shape)
print('Year range:', pop['year'].min(), '–', pop['year'].max())
print('Datatype values:')
print(pop['datatype'].value_counts())

title: 7  Extending Data with pandas
text:
7.5 Derived Columns
A derived column is a new column computed from existing columns using arithmetic, conditional logic, or binning. The key principle is that derived columns should be transparent — anyone reading your code should be able to see exactly how the new value was computed from the originals.

7.5.1 Arithmetic: Batting Average
Batting average is hits divided by at-bats. The formula is simple, but the implementation requires a decision about what to do when at-bats is zero:

batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))

print('BA stats:')
print(batting['BA'].describe())
print('Rows with NaN BA:', batting['BA'].isna().sum())

Replacing zero with NaN before dividing avoids a ZeroDivisionError and produces NaN for players with no at-bats — which is the correct semantic meaning: their batting average is undefined, not zero.


7.5.2 Multi-step formulas: Slugging Percentage and OBP
More complex metrics require computing intermediate values. Slugging percentage measures batting productivity by weighting different types of hits:

batting['TB'] = (
    batting['H']
    + 2 * batting['2B']
    + 3 * batting['3B']
    + 4 * batting['HR']
)

batting['SLG'] = batting['TB'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))

On-base percentage measures how frequently a batter reaches base. It uses a formula with four variables in the numerator and denominator:
\[OBP = \frac{H + BB + HBP}{AB + BB + HBP + SF}\]

batting['HBP'] = batting['HBP'].fillna(0)
batting['SF'] = batting['SF'].fillna(0)

numerator = batting['H'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP']
denominator = batting['AB'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'] + batting['SF']
batting['OBP'] = numerator / denominator

print(batting['OBP'].min(), batting['OBP'].max())

Notice the .fillna(0) calls. The HBP (hit by pitch) and SF (sacrifice fly) columns have missing values for early seasons when those statistics were not tracked. Filling with zero is a documented decision: we are treating “not tracked” as “did not happen.” This is debatable, and the Known Issues Log is where you would note this assumption.


7.5.3 Binning with pd.cut()
Binning transforms a continuous variable into categories. The Colorado population data has single-year ages (0 through 100); we want three demographic bands:

bins = [0, 18, 65, 101]
labels = ['Children (0-17)', 'Working Age (18-64)', 'Seniors (65+)']

pop['age_band'] = pd.cut(pop['age'], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)

print('Age band counts:')
print(pop['age_band'].value_counts())

The right=False parameter makes bins left-inclusive: [0, 18) means ages 0 through 17. This puts 18-year-olds in the Working Age band, which is the standard demographic convention.
Always verify that no values fell outside the bins:

print('NaN age bands:', pop['age_band'].isna().sum())



7.5.4 Integer division for decades
A quick way to create decade bins from year values:

batting['decade'] = (batting['yearID'] // 10) * 10

print('Decades:')
print(batting['decade'].value_counts().sort_index())



7.5.5 Parsing Dates: Cannabis Sales
Government datasets frequently store dates as separate integer columns — Month and Year rather than a single date column. The cannabis sales data is a typical example. pd.to_datetime() can reconstruct a proper datetime from integer month and year values:

cannabis = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2,
    skipfooter=8,
    engine='python',
    na_values=['NR']
)
cannabis.columns = ['Month', 'Year', 'County', 'Medical', 'Retail']
cannabis['County'] = cannabis['County'].str.strip()
for col in ['Medical', 'Retail']:
    cannabis[col] = pd.to_numeric(
        cannabis[col].str.replace('$', '', regex=False).str.replace(',', '', regex=False)
    )
cannabis = cannabis[cannabis['County'] != 'Total']

# Construct a date column from integer Month and Year
cannabis['date'] = pd.to_datetime(
    cannabis[['Year', 'Month']].assign(Day=1).rename(
        columns={'Year': 'year', 'Month': 'month'}
    )
)

print(cannabis[['Month', 'Year', 'date']].head())

pd.to_datetime() accepts a DataFrame with year, month, and day columns. The .assign(Day=1) adds a constant day-of-month, and .rename() converts the column names to lowercase as required.
Once parsed as a proper datetime, the column unlocks time-series operations — sorting chronologically, computing time differences, and resampling by period:

# Quarterly retail sales, statewide
quarterly = (
    cannabis
    .set_index('date')
    ['Retail']
    .resample('QE')
    .sum()
)

print(quarterly.tail(8))







TipStoring dates as integers is not a mistake



Storing month and year as separate integer columns is a deliberate design in many government systems — it makes SQL filtering simple (WHERE year = 2022) without requiring date functions. When you encounter this pattern, pd.to_datetime() with assign(Day=1) is the standard reconstruction approach.

title: 7  Extending Data with pandas
text:
7.6 Grouped Summaries
The groupby() → agg() pattern is the programmatic equivalent of a spreadsheet pivot table. It splits the data into groups, applies an aggregation function to each group, and combines the results.

7.6.1 Basic groupby: Total HR per Team

latest_year = batting['yearID'].max()
yr_df = batting[batting['yearID'] == latest_year]

team_hr = (
    yr_df
    .groupby('teamID')
    .agg({'HR': 'sum'})
    .reset_index()
    .sort_values('HR', ascending=False)
)

team_hr.head()



7.6.2 Filtering before aggregating
When computing averages, filter to meaningful subsets first. A player with 2 at-bats and 1 hit has a .500 batting average, but that tells you nothing useful:

qualified = batting[batting['AB'] >= 100]

decade_ba = (
    qualified
    .groupby('decade')['BA']
    .agg('mean')
)

decade_ba.plot(title='Mean BA by Decade (players with ≥ 100 AB)')



7.6.3 The dependency ratio: a real-world grouped summary
The dependency ratio — the number of children and seniors per working-age adult — is a standard demographic metric that requires grouped aggregation to compute:

estimates = pop[pop['datatype'] == 'Estimate'].copy()
co_2020 = estimates[estimates['year'] == 2020]

band_summary = co_2020.groupby(['county', 'age_band']).agg(
    pop=('totalpopulation', 'sum')
).reset_index()

# Validate: grouped total should match original
print(f'Grouped total:  {band_summary["pop"].sum():,.0f}')
print(f'Original total: {co_2020["totalpopulation"].sum():,.0f}')

title: 7  Extending Data with pandas
text:
7.7 Three Tools, One Job
Pandas offers three tools for creating cross-tabular summaries, and understanding when to use each saves time and prevents errors.








Tool
Aggregates?
Use when




groupby().agg()
Yes
You need flexibility, named aggregations, or long format for further computation


pivot_table()
Yes
You want aggregation and reshaping in one step (closest to an Excel pivot)


pivot()
No
Data is already one-row-per-key; you only need to reshape




7.7.1 groupby().agg() — long format output

modern = batting[(batting['yearID'] >= 1990) & (batting['yearID'] <= 2022)].copy()

hr_long = (
    modern
    .groupby(['teamID', 'decade'])['HR']
    .agg('sum')
    .reset_index()
    .rename(columns={'HR': 'total_HR'})
)

print('Shape:', hr_long.shape, '← one row per (team, decade)')
hr_long.head()



7.7.2 pivot_table() — wide format in one step

hr_wide_pt = modern.pivot_table(
    index='teamID',
    columns='decade',
    values='HR',
    aggfunc='sum',
    fill_value=0
)

print('Shape:', hr_wide_pt.shape, '← one row per team, one column per decade')
hr_wide_pt.head()



7.7.3 pivot() — reshape only, no aggregation
pivot() requires every (index, columns) combination to appear exactly once in the data. It will raise a ValueError if there are duplicates, because it cannot aggregate:

# This WORKS because hr_long has one row per (team, decade)
hr_wide_pv = hr_long.pivot(
    index='teamID',
    columns='decade',
    values='total_HR'
)

print('Shape:', hr_wide_pv.shape)



7.7.4 Verifying all three agree

total_long = hr_long['total_HR'].sum()
total_pt = hr_wide_pt.values.sum()
total_pv = hr_wide_pv.fillna(0).values.sum()

print(f'groupby().agg() total HR: {total_long:,}')
print(f'pivot_table()   total HR: {total_pt:,}')
print(f'pivot()         total HR: {total_pv:,}')
print(f'All match: {total_long == total_pt == total_pv}')

title: 7  Extending Data with pandas
text:
7.8 agg() vs. transform()
Both operate on groups, but they produce fundamentally different outputs. The agg() method produces one row per group — the output is smaller than the input. The transform() method broadcasts the group-level result back to every row in the original DataFrame — the output has the same number of rows as the input.

# agg() — summary table
team_totals = (
    batting[batting['yearID'] == 2022]
    .groupby('teamID')['HR']
    .agg('sum')
    .reset_index()
)
print('agg() shape:', team_totals.shape, '(one row per team)')


# transform() — adds group stat to every row
batting['team_total_HR'] = (
    batting
    .groupby(['yearID', 'teamID'])['HR']
    .transform('sum')
)

batting['hr_share'] = batting['HR'] / batting['team_total_HR'].replace(0, float('nan'))

Use transform() when you want to compute something like “what percentage of team home runs did each player contribute?” — a question that requires both the individual value and the group total on the same row.

title: 7  Extending Data with pandas
text:
7.9 Public Interest Context: Oversight
Derived metrics are interpretive choices dressed up as arithmetic. A rate is a ratio — and the denominator you pick determines the story. “Deaths per 100,000 residents” and “deaths per 100,000 adults” and “deaths per 100,000 people with health insurance” can all point the same way or the opposite way on the same underlying data. A dependency ratio that excludes seniors under 65 who are still working looks different from one that doesn’t. When you create a derived column, you are quietly asserting that this is the right way to compare groups, and your assertion travels downstream into every chart and conclusion.
Grouped summaries have the same property. A groupby that silently drops rows with NaN in the group column, or a bin that accidentally truncates values outside its edges, is hidden data loss that still produces a confident-looking table. Oversight in extending data means auditing the arithmetic: re-deriving totals, comparing group sums to source sums, and explicitly naming the denominator you chose and why. When you hand someone a rate table, can they reconstruct the formula you used to build it?






NoteConnection to Chapter 4



This section connects to the concept of oversight introduced in Chapter 4. Every derived metric is an analytical decision; oversight requires that those decisions be explicit and auditable rather than buried in a one-line arithmetic expression.

title: 7  Extending Data with pandas
text:
7.10 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningZeroDivisionError when computing a rate



What it means: You divided by a column that contains zero. Batting average = hits / at-bats fails when at-bats is 0.
How to fix it: Replace the divisor’s zeros with NaN before dividing: df['H'] / df['AB'].replace(0, float('nan')). This produces NaN for undefined cases, which is the correct semantic.








Warningpivot() raises ValueError: Index contains duplicate entries



What it means: pivot() does not aggregate — it requires each (index, columns) combination to appear exactly once. If your source has multiple rows per combination, pivot() cannot decide which value to use.
How to fix it: Use pivot_table() instead, which takes an aggfunc parameter. Or aggregate first with groupby().agg() so each combination is unique, then call pivot().








Warninggroupby aggregation produces unexpected NaN



What it means: Groups with all-missing input produce NaN in the output, which is easy to misread as a zero.
How to fix it: Decide what missing should mean for your question. For counts, fill_value=0 in pivot_table or .fillna(0) after aggregation is usually appropriate. For means, leaving NaN is often correct — averaging nothing is not zero.








WarningGrouped totals do not match the original total



What it means: Either rows were filtered before grouping, or a groupby column has NaN values (which are excluded by default).
How to fix it: Compare df['col'].sum() before grouping and grouped['col'].sum() after. If they differ, inspect df['group_col'].isna().sum(). Use dropna=False in groupby if you want to keep missing-group rows in the output.

title: 7  Extending Data with pandas
text:
7.11 Key Takeaways

Derived columns should be transparent: reading the code should tell you exactly how a new value was computed. Avoid magic numbers and undocumented assumptions.
groupby().agg() → long format; pivot_table() → wide format in one step; pivot() → reshape only (no aggregation). Pick the tool that matches the question.
.agg() collapses a group to one row. .transform() broadcasts the group result back to every row. Use .transform() when the individual value and the group stat need to coexist.
Validate every aggregation by comparing totals before and after. Silent row drops — from NaN in a groupby key, or from missing data — are the most common source of wrong answers.
Binning continuous variables with pd.cut is a standard way to turn ages, incomes, or scores into analytically meaningful categories.

title: 7  Extending Data with pandas
text:
7.12 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on derived columns and grouped summaries






pandas: Group by: split-apply-combine — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/groupby.html is the canonical reference on grouped operations.
pandas: Reshaping and pivot tables — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/reshaping.html compares pivot, pivot_table, melt, and stack/unstack.
pandas: Time series / date functionality — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/timeseries.html covers to_datetime, resampling, and offsets.
Real Python: pandas GroupBy: Your Guide to Grouping Data — realpython.com/pandas-groupby/ is an accessible tutorial with real datasets.
Lahman Baseball Database documentation — seanlahman.com/baseball-archive/statistics/ explains each statistic used in this chapter.

title: 7  Extending Data with pandas
text:
7.13 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Compute OPS and identify top performers





OPS is On-base Plus Slugging: OPS = OBP + SLG. Add an OPS column to the batting DataFrame. Filter to players with at least 400 at-bats in a single season, and find the top 10 OPS seasons of all time.






NoteSolution






batting['OPS'] = batting['OBP'] + batting['SLG']

top_ops = (
    batting[batting['AB'] >= 400]
    .sort_values('OPS', ascending=False)
    [['playerID', 'yearID', 'teamID', 'AB', 'BA', 'SLG', 'OBP', 'OPS']]
    .head(10)
)
top_ops













NoteExercise 2: Compute the dependency ratio for all counties in 2020





Using the Colorado population data, filter to Estimate rows for 2020, bin ages into the three standard bands, and compute the dependency ratio for each county. Which county has the highest dependency ratio? The lowest?






NoteSolution






co_2020 = estimates[estimates['year'] == 2020].copy()

wide = co_2020.groupby(['county', 'age_band'], observed=True).agg(
    pop=('totalpopulation', 'sum')
).reset_index().pivot_table(
    index='county', columns='age_band', values='pop', aggfunc='sum'
).reset_index()
wide.columns.name = None

wide['dep_ratio'] = (
    (wide['Children (0-17)'] + wide['Seniors (65+)'])
    / wide['Working Age (18-64)']
)

print('Highest:', wide.sort_values('dep_ratio', ascending=False).head(3)[['county', 'dep_ratio']].to_string())
print('\nLowest:', wide.sort_values('dep_ratio').head(3)[['county', 'dep_ratio']].to_string())













NoteExercise 3: Compare pivot_table() and groupby() → pivot() paths





Using the dependency ratio data from Exercise 2, implement the same result using the two-step groupby().agg() → pivot() path. Verify that both approaches produce identical results using pd.testing.assert_frame_equal().






NoteSolution






# Path A: pivot_table (one step)
wide_pt = co_2020.pivot_table(
    index='county', columns='age_band', values='totalpopulation', aggfunc='sum'
)

# Path B: groupby → pivot (two steps)
long_agg = co_2020.groupby(['county', 'age_band'], observed=True).agg(
    pop=('totalpopulation', 'sum')
).reset_index()

wide_pv = long_agg.pivot(index='county', columns='age_band', values='pop')

# Verify
pd.testing.assert_frame_equal(
    wide_pt.sort_index(), wide_pv.sort_index(), check_names=False
)
print('Both paths produce identical DataFrames.')

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
8.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
Convert data between wide and long formats using pd.melt, pivot, and pivot_table.
Stack DataFrames vertically with pd.concat, validating row counts and column alignment.
Combine tables on shared keys using pd.merge with every join type (inner, left, right, outer).
Audit merge quality using the indicator=True parameter and understand why rows fail to match.
Recognize when aggregation must happen before a merge to avoid row inflation.

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
8.2 Why Reshaping Matters
Most analysis questions require data from multiple sources, and those sources rarely arrive in the shape you need. This chapter covers the operations that change the structure of your data: melt() to go from wide to long, pivot() and pivot_table() to go from long to wide, concat() to stack DataFrames vertically, and merge() to combine tables by matching on shared keys. Each operation carries risks — silent row inflation, dropped records, introduced nulls — that you must audit after every transformation.
Reshaping is where silent bugs love to hide. A merge that silently drops 40% of rows because of mismatched dtypes, a concat that creates duplicate columns because of trailing whitespace in a name, a pivot that aggregates values you did not expect to aggregate — all of these produce confident-looking output that is quietly wrong. Build the habit of auditing after every reshape.

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
8.3 Setup
This chapter uses pandas and numpy.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.3f}'.format)







WarningIf you get an ImportError





Install missing packages with conda install pandas numpy or pip install pandas numpy, then restart the Jupyter kernel. See Chapter 1.

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
8.4 Datasets
This chapter continues with the Lahman Baseball Database and Colorado population data from Chapter 7, and adds two new Lahman tables — Salaries and People — to demonstrate multi-table merging.

batting = pd.read_csv('Batting.csv')

# Derived columns from previous chapter
batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['TB'] = batting['H'] + 2*batting['2B'] + 3*batting['3B'] + 4*batting['HR']
batting['SLG'] = batting['TB'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['HBP'] = batting['HBP'].fillna(0)
batting['SF'] = batting['SF'].fillna(0)
batting['OBP'] = (
    (batting['H'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'])
    / (batting['AB'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'] + batting['SF'])
    .replace(0, float('nan'))
)
batting['OPS'] = batting['OBP'] + batting['SLG']
batting['decade'] = (batting['yearID'] // 10) * 10

print('Batting shape:', batting.shape)


salaries = pd.read_csv('Salaries.csv')
print('Salaries shape:', salaries.shape)
print('Year range:', salaries['yearID'].min(), '–', salaries['yearID'].max())


people = pd.read_csv('People.csv')
print('People shape:', people.shape)
people[['playerID', 'nameFirst', 'nameLast', 'birthYear', 'birthState']].head()

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
8.5 Wide vs. Long Format
The same data can be represented in two fundamentally different shapes.
Wide format has one row per entity and multiple columns for repeated measurements. A table of HR by decade with teams as rows and decades as columns is wide format.
Long format (also called “tidy” format) has one row per observation. The same HR data in long format has one row per (team, decade) combination, with a single column for the HR value.
Neither format is inherently better. Wide format is easier for humans to read as a cross-tab. Long format is easier for computation — groupby(), merge(), and plotting functions generally expect long format. The skill is knowing how to convert between the two.

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
8.6 melt(): Wide to Long
The melt() function unpivots a wide DataFrame into long format. You specify which columns stay as identifiers (id_vars) and which columns get “melted” into a single key-value pair (value_vars).

# Build a wide pivot first
c0 = batting['yearID'] >= 1900
c1 = batting['yearID'] < 2020
batting_modern = batting[c0 & c1].copy()

hr_wide = batting_modern.pivot_table(
    index='decade',
    columns='teamID',
    values='HR',
    aggfunc='sum'
).reset_index()
hr_wide.columns.name = None

print('Wide shape:', hr_wide.shape)


team_cols = [c for c in hr_wide.columns if c != 'decade']

hr_long = hr_wide.melt(
    id_vars=['decade'],
    value_vars=team_cols,
    var_name='teamID',
    value_name='total_HR'
)

print('Long shape:', hr_long.shape)
print('Expected rows:', hr_wide.shape[0], '×', len(team_cols), '=', hr_wide.shape[0] * len(team_cols))

After melting, some rows will contain NaN — a team that did not exist in a given decade. Always drop or flag these:

hr_clean = hr_long.dropna(subset=['total_HR'])
print('After dropping NaN:', hr_clean.shape)

The power of long format is that it enables further computation that would be awkward in wide format:

# League-wide mean HR per team by decade — easy in long, awkward in wide
league_avg = (
    hr_clean
    .groupby('decade')['total_HR']
    .agg(n_teams='count', mean_HR='mean', max_HR='max')
    .reset_index()
)

league_avg.set_index('decade')['mean_HR'].plot(title='Mean Team HR by Decade')

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
8.7 concat(): Stacking DataFrames
The concat() function appends rows from multiple DataFrames into one. This is the programmatic equivalent of copy-pasting rows across Excel sheets. Use it when you have multiple files with the same columns — annual exports, regional splits, or train/test splits.

dead_ball = batting[batting['yearID'] < 1920].copy()
live_ball = batting[(batting['yearID'] >= 1920) & (batting['yearID'] < 1970)].copy()
modern = batting[batting['yearID'] >= 1970].copy()

frames = [dead_ball, live_ball, modern]
all_eras = pd.concat(frames, ignore_index=True)

print('Shape after concat:', all_eras.shape)
print('Row count matches original?', all_eras.shape[0] == batting.shape[0])







WarningThe validation pattern after concat()



Always check three things after concatenating: (1) row count equals the sum of input row counts, (2) no new NaN columns appeared (column name mismatches create them silently), and (3) no unexpected duplicates from overlapping date ranges.



8.7.1 Tracking provenance with keys
When files come from different sources, use the keys parameter to record which file each row came from:

era_labels = ['Dead Ball (<1920)', 'Live Ball (1920-1969)', 'Modern (1970+)']

all_eras_labeled = pd.concat(
    frames,
    keys=era_labels,
    ignore_index=False
).reset_index(level=0).rename(columns={'level_0': 'era'})

print(all_eras_labeled['era'].value_counts())

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
8.8 merge(): Combining Tables by Key
Merging is the most powerful and most dangerous reshaping operation. It combines two DataFrames by matching rows on one or more shared key columns — the programmatic equivalent of VLOOKUP in a spreadsheet, but with support for many-to-many relationships, multiple keys, and all join types.






WarningBefore you merge: check your column names and dtypes



The most common merge failure is a key that looks identical in both tables but actually differs. "Adams" vs "Adams ", "12345" (string) vs 12345 (int), or a typo in one column — any of these will silently drop rows.
Before merging, run print(df_left['key'].dtype, df_right['key'].dtype). If they differ, cast one side. Then run set(df_left['key']) - set(df_right['key']) to see unmatched values on the left, and the reverse for the right.



8.8.1 Step 1: Inspect keys before merging
Before any merge, verify that your keys have the same dtype in both tables and understand the cardinality — is the key unique in each table, or can it repeat?

print('Batting playerID dtype:', batting['playerID'].dtype)
print('Salaries playerID dtype:', salaries['playerID'].dtype)
print()
print('Batting: max rows per playerID+yearID:',
      batting.groupby(['playerID', 'yearID']).size().max(),
      '(>1 means multi-stint seasons)')
print('Salaries: max rows per playerID+yearID:',
      salaries.groupby(['playerID', 'yearID']).size().max())

The batting table has players with multiple rows per season — players traded mid-year get one row per team. This means a merge on playerID + yearID alone could produce multiple matches. The solution is to aggregate batting to one row per player-season first:

batting_season = (
    batting
    .groupby(['playerID', 'yearID'])
    .agg(
        AB=('AB', 'sum'), H=('H', 'sum'), TB=('TB', 'sum'),
        BB=('BB', 'sum'), HBP=('HBP', 'sum'), SF=('SF', 'sum'),
        HR=('HR', 'sum'), G=('G', 'sum')
    )
    .reset_index()
)

# Recompute rates on aggregated totals
batting_season['BA'] = batting_season['H'] / batting_season['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting_season['SLG'] = batting_season['TB'] / batting_season['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting_season['OPS'] = batting_season['BA'] + batting_season['SLG']  # simplified

print('Aggregated shape:', batting_season.shape, '(one row per player-season)')



8.8.2 Step 2: Merge with indicator=True
The indicator=True parameter adds a _merge column that shows whether each row matched in both tables, only in the left, or only in the right:

bat_sal = pd.merge(
    left=batting_season,
    right=salaries[['playerID', 'yearID', 'salary']],
    on=['playerID', 'yearID'],
    how='left',
    indicator=True
)

print('Before merge:', batting_season.shape[0], 'rows')
print('After merge:', bat_sal.shape[0], 'rows')
print()
print('Merge audit:')
print(bat_sal['_merge'].value_counts())
print(f'\nMatch rate: {(bat_sal["_merge"] == "both").mean():.1%}')



8.8.3 Step 3: Understand unmatched rows

unmatched = bat_sal[bat_sal['_merge'] == 'left_only']
print('Unmatched year range:', unmatched['yearID'].min(), '–', unmatched['yearID'].max())

The Lahman salary data begins in 1985. All seasons before that have no salary information — this is a structural gap in the data, not an error in the merge. Understanding why rows do not match is as important as knowing that they do not match.


8.8.4 Adding biographical data

bio_cols = ['playerID', 'nameFirst', 'nameLast', 'birthYear', 'birthState', 'birthCountry']
people_slim = people[bio_cols].copy()

bat_sal_bio = pd.merge(bat_sal, people_slim, on='playerID', how='left')

print('Shape after adding bio:', bat_sal_bio.shape)
print('Missing nameFirst:', bat_sal_bio['nameFirst'].isna().sum())

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
8.9 Applied Analysis: Salary and Performance
With a merged dataset, you can answer questions that required data from multiple tables.

# Filter to salary era with meaningful at-bats
qualified = bat_sal_bio[
    (bat_sal_bio['yearID'] >= 1985) &
    (bat_sal_bio['AB'] >= 200) &
    (bat_sal_bio['salary'].notna())
].copy()

qualified['sal_decile'] = pd.qcut(
    qualified['salary'], q=10,
    labels=['D1 (Lowest)', 'D2', 'D3', 'D4', 'D5',
            'D6', 'D7', 'D8', 'D9', 'D10 (Highest)']
)

perf_by_salary = (
    qualified
    .groupby('sal_decile', observed=True)[['BA', 'SLG']]
    .agg('mean')
    .round(3)
)

print('Mean batting stats by salary decile:')
perf_by_salary

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
section: 8.10 MultiIndex: When Groups Become the Index
text:
8.10 MultiIndex: When Groups Become the Index
After groupby().agg() on multiple columns, pandas returns a MultiIndex — a hierarchical index that can be confusing if you are not expecting it. The most common solution is to call .reset_index() immediately to flatten it back to regular columns:

# Without reset_index — produces a MultiIndex
team_stats = (
    batting_modern
    .groupby(['decade', 'teamID'])[['HR', 'BA']]
    .agg(['mean', 'sum'])
)

print('MultiIndex columns:', team_stats.columns[:4])


# Flatten with reset_index and column renaming
team_stats.columns = ['_'.join(col) for col in team_stats.columns]
team_stats = team_stats.reset_index()
team_stats.head()

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
8.11 Public Interest Context: Ownership
Reshaping decides what granularity survives. When you melt a wide table into long format, or aggregate a daily series into a monthly one, or merge two tables and drop their non-overlapping rows, you are choosing what questions can be asked of the result. A county-year dataset cannot answer a county-month question later; an analysis rolled up to age bands cannot be rerun at single-year-of-age without going back to the source. These are ownership decisions: what you keep is what downstream analysts can study, and what you collapse is gone unless you preserved the raw data.
The Data Dictionary from Chapter 2 is the practice that keeps ownership legible. Document which columns are derived, which are joined in from which source, and what grain the final table lives at. A reviewer six months from now should be able to reconstruct your reshaping choices from that dictionary — and know, without rerunning your code, whether the table in front of them can answer their new question or needs to be rebuilt from the source.






NoteConnection to Chapter 4



This section connects to the concept of ownership introduced in Chapter 4. Reshaping collapses some information to expose other information; the Data Dictionary records those choices so that later analysts know what is and is not answerable from the reshaped table.

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
8.12 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningMerge returned more rows than expected (“row inflation”)



What it means: You merged on keys that were not unique in one or both tables. A many-to-many merge explodes: 3 batting rows × 2 salary rows for the same player-year = 6 output rows.
How to fix it: Before merging, run df.groupby(key_cols).size().max() on each table. If it is > 1, aggregate to one row per key first. If you genuinely need the many-to-many, document why.








WarningMerge silently dropped rows (low match rate)



What it means: One of: (1) column-name mismatch — County vs county, (2) dtype mismatch — "2020" vs 2020, (3) whitespace or case — "Adams" vs "Adams " vs "adams", or (4) a legitimate structural gap (salaries start in 1985).
How to fix it: Always use indicator=True. Inspect left_only rows — what years, what keys, what values? Decide whether the gap is legitimate or a bug in your cleaning. Fix the keys upstream if it is a bug.








Warningconcat produced unexpected NaN columns



What it means: One DataFrame has "County" and another has "county". Concatenation aligns by column name, so each DataFrame contributes its own column and the other rows are NaN.
How to fix it: Standardize column names before concatenation: df.columns = df.columns.str.strip().str.lower(). Compare set(df1.columns) ^ set(df2.columns) to see mismatches.








Warningpivot raises ValueError: Index contains duplicate entries



What it means: pivot() does not aggregate and cannot pick between competing values. Your source has more than one row per (index, columns) pair.
How to fix it: Use pivot_table() with an aggfunc, or aggregate first with groupby().agg(). See Chapter 7 for the three-tools comparison.

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
8.13 Key Takeaways

Reshaping operations are both powerful and dangerous. Audit row counts and column names after every one.
Aggregate before you merge when the merge key is not unique. Fix row inflation upstream, not downstream.
Use indicator=True on every merge where correctness matters. The _merge column is your quality report.
Long format is usually easier for computation; wide format is usually easier for human reading. Know how to convert either direction.
concat stacks rows; merge combines tables on keys. Choose the right tool based on what question you are asking.

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
8.14 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on reshaping and merging






pandas: Merge, join, concatenate and compare — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/merging.html is the canonical reference on combining tables.
pandas: Reshaping and pivot tables — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/reshaping.html covers melt, pivot, pivot_table, stack, unstack.
Hadley Wickham: Tidy Data — vita.had.co.nz/papers/tidy-data.pdf is the foundational paper on long-format (“tidy”) data, written for R but conceptually identical in pandas.
Real Python: Combining Data in Pandas With merge(), join(), and concat() — realpython.com/pandas-merge-join-and-concat/ is an accessible tutorial with worked examples.
Lahman Baseball Database readme — github.com/chadwickbureau/baseballdatabank documents every table and join key.

title: 8  Reshaping Data with pandas
text:
8.15 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Melt and validate





Take the wide dependency ratio table from Chapter 7 (counties as rows, age bands as columns). Melt it back to long format. Verify that the population totals match between the wide and long versions.






NoteSolution






age_band_cols = ['Children (0-17)', 'Working Age (18-64)', 'Seniors (65+)']

long_2020 = wide_2020.melt(
    id_vars=['county'],
    value_vars=age_band_cols,
    var_name='age_band',
    value_name='pop'
)

print('Wide sum:', wide_2020[age_band_cols].sum().sum())
print('Long sum:', long_2020['pop'].sum())













NoteExercise 2: Merge with audit





Merge the Lahman Teams.csv table onto the batting data using teamID and yearID as keys. Use indicator=True and report the match rate. How many batting rows have no corresponding team record?






NoteSolution






teams = pd.read_csv('Teams.csv')

bat_team = pd.merge(
    batting,
    teams[['teamID', 'yearID', 'name', 'W', 'L']],
    on=['teamID', 'yearID'],
    how='left',
    indicator=True
)

print(bat_team['_merge'].value_counts())
print(f'\nMatch rate: {(bat_team["_merge"] == "both").mean():.1%}')













NoteExercise 3: Colorado-born player analysis





Using the merged bat_sal_bio DataFrame, find all player-seasons where birthState == 'CO' and AB >= 100. Who are the top 10 Colorado-born players by career OPS?

title: 9  Loading Data with SQL
text:
9.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 9  Loading Data with SQL
text:
Check that SQLite is installed and create a new .db database file from the terminal.
Distinguish SQLite dot-commands from SQL statements, and use .mode, .headers, and .import correctly.
Write a CREATE TABLE schema and load a clean CSV into the table.
Connect to a SQLite database from Python using sqlite3.connect and read query results into a pandas DataFrame with pd.read_sql_query.
Write exploratory SQL using SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, ORDER BY, and LIMIT, and translate between SQL and pandas equivalents.

title: 9  Loading Data with SQL
text:
9.2 Why SQL
This chapter marks the beginning of a new tool — SQL and the SQLite database engine — applied to the same Load–Clean–Extend–Reshape workflow you already know from pandas. Many of the concepts are identical: you are still loading data from files, inspecting it for problems, checking types and counts, and verifying that nothing went wrong. What changes is the interface: instead of calling Python methods on a DataFrame, you write declarative queries that describe what you want, and the database engine figures out how to get it.
SQL (Structured Query Language) has been the standard language for working with databases since the 1970s. You will encounter it in virtually every data-related career. SQLite is a lightweight database engine that stores an entire database in a single file on disk — no server, no installation wizard, no network configuration. This makes it ideal for learning SQL and for working with datasets that fit on a single machine.
The workflow in this chapter moves through four stages: orienting to the terminal, creating a database and schema in the SQLite shell, connecting to the database from Python, and running diagnostic queries with pd.read_sql_query().
You already know the Load–Clean–Extend–Reshape rhythm from Part II. The goal of this part is translation, not starting over. Most of your intuitions about inspecting data, validating transformations, and documenting decisions carry over — only the syntax changes.

title: 9  Loading Data with SQL
text:
9.3 Terminal Orientation
Before touching SQLite, you need three terminal commands. If you completed Chapter 1, these are review.



Command (Mac/Linux)
Command (Windows)
Purpose




pwd
cd (no arguments)
Print the current directory


ls
dir
List files in the current directory


cd folder
cd folder
Move into a folder




9.3.1 Checking SQLite installation
sqlite3 --version
If you see a version number (e.g., 3.43.2), SQLite is installed and on your PATH. If you get command not found, see Chapter 1 for installation instructions.

title: 9  Loading Data with SQL
text:
9.4 The SQLite Shell
The SQLite shell (sqlite3) is a command-line interface for interacting with a database directly. Think of it as a text-based spreadsheet viewer that also lets you define tables and run queries.

9.4.1 Dot-commands vs. SQL statements
The SQLite shell has two distinct types of commands. Dot-commands start with a period, do not end with a semicolon, and control the shell itself (.tables, .schema, .import, .quit). SQL statements are standard SQL, always end with a semicolon, and operate on data (SELECT ...;, CREATE TABLE ...;).
A SQL statement without a semicolon will sit and wait — SQLite thinks you are still typing. Adding a semicolon after a dot-command will produce an error. Getting these conventions straight early saves considerable frustration.


9.4.2 Step 1: Create the database file
Running sqlite3 cannabis.db either opens an existing file or creates a new one:
sqlite3 cannabis.db ".tables"
The file is created on disk when you first write data to it.


9.4.3 Step 2: Prepare the CSV






ImportantSQL builds on Python



The SQL workflow in this chapter depends on the Python workflow from Chapter 5 and Chapter 6. SQLite’s .import command requires a clean, rectangular CSV — exactly one header row, no aggregate rows, no currency formatting, no merged cells. Real-world government files rarely meet these requirements out of the box. The standard approach is to use pandas to load and clean the raw file first, then export a normalized CSV for SQLite to consume. If you have not completed Chapter 5 and Chapter 6, do so before continuing here.


SQLite’s .import command expects a clean, rectangular CSV with exactly one header row and no aggregate rows. The standard approach is to clean the file in pandas first — applying the same operations from Chapter 6 — and export a clean CSV for SQLite to consume.

title: 9  Loading Data with SQL
section: 9.5 Public Interest Context: Enclosure by Interface
text:
9.5 Public Interest Context: Enclosure by Interface
SQL databases are how institutions store and gate-keep their data. A schema is not a neutral technical artifact: it encodes what the institution has decided counts as a record, what columns are worth collecting, and what categories are allowed. The fact that the cannabis file must be cleaned in pandas before SQLite can import it is not accidental — government datasets are typically published through web portals designed for human download, one file at a time, formatted for spreadsheet users. The publishing workflow is optimized for a different kind of user than you.
Understanding schemas is understanding how organizations decide what to count. When an agency publishes data without a schema — or with a schema that does not match the published CSV — the friction is itself a form of enclosure. When you build your own schema (the CREATE TABLE above), you are naming what you consider the meaningful shape of the record. What’s in the schema you’d write if you designed it from scratch, and what’s missing from the one the agency actually publishes?






NoteConnection to Chapter 4



This section connects to the concept of enclosure introduced in Chapter 4. SQL schemas are how institutions structure and gate access to data; understanding them is part of understanding how public data is enclosed in practice.


This chapter uses pandas, numpy, and Python’s built-in sqlite3 module.






WarningIf you get an ImportError





sqlite3 ships with Python’s standard library — if you get ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'sqlite3', your Python install is unusual (this is very rare). For pandas or numpy, run conda install pandas numpy or pip install pandas numpy and restart the kernel. See Chapter 1.




import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df_raw = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2,
    skipfooter=8,
    engine='python',
    na_values=['NR']
)

# Rename to clean, SQL-friendly column names
df_raw.columns = ['month', 'year', 'county', 'medical_sales', 'retail_sales']

# Strip currency formatting and cast to nullable integer
