for col in ['medical_sales', 'retail_sales']:
    df_raw[col] = (
        df_raw[col]
        .str.strip()
        .str.replace('$', '', regex=False)
        .str.replace(',', '', regex=False)
        .pipe(pd.to_numeric, errors='coerce')
        .astype('Int64')
    )

# Remove aggregate Total rows
df_raw = df_raw[df_raw['county'].str.strip() != 'Total']

# Export
df_raw.to_csv('cannabis_clean.csv', index=False)
print(f"Exported {len(df_raw):,} rows to cannabis_clean.csv")







TipSQL naming rules



Use lowercase letters, underscores, and no spaces or special characters for column names. The names in the CSV must exactly match the names in the CREATE TABLE schema you will define next. A mismatch causes the header row to be inserted as a data row — one of the most common SQLite loading errors.



9.5.1 Step 3: Define the schema
Before loading data, you declare the table structure — column names and their storage types:
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS cannabis_sales (
    month INTEGER,
    year INTEGER,
    county TEXT,
    medical_sales INTEGER,
    retail_sales INTEGER
);
SQLite has five storage classes: INTEGER, REAL, TEXT, BLOB, and NULL. For most work, you will use INTEGER, REAL, and TEXT.


9.5.2 Step 4: Load the CSV
The .import command reads a delimited file and inserts rows into a table. Always set .mode csv and .headers on before importing:
sqlite3 cannabis.db << 'EOF'
.mode csv
.headers on
.import cannabis_clean.csv cannabis_sales
EOF






WarningThe heredoc syntax



The << 'EOF' syntax sends multiple lines of input to SQLite through stdin. This is necessary because dot-commands cannot be passed as quoted string arguments on the command line. If you are on Windows, use the Python-side approach described in the next section instead.




9.5.3 Step 5: Post-load diagnostics
Run three checks immediately after loading:
# 1. Row count
sqlite3 cannabis.db "SELECT COUNT(*) AS row_count FROM cannabis_sales;"

# 2. First rows
sqlite3 -column -header cannabis.db "SELECT * FROM cannabis_sales LIMIT 5;"

# 3. Check for accidentally imported header row
sqlite3 cannabis.db "SELECT COUNT(*) AS header_rows FROM cannabis_sales WHERE county = 'county';"
If the header row count is greater than zero, delete it:
DELETE FROM cannabis_sales WHERE county = 'county';

title: 9  Loading Data with SQL
text:
9.6 Connecting from Python
The terminal workflow above is useful for setup and debugging, but your analysis work will happen in Jupyter notebooks using Python’s built-in sqlite3 module and pandas’ pd.read_sql_query().

import sqlite3

conn = sqlite3.connect('cannabis.db')
print("Connection established:", conn)







Warning



If the path is wrong, sqlite3.connect() will silently create a new empty file rather than raising an error. If your queries return “no such table,” check that you are pointing at the right file.



9.6.1 Low-level access with a cursor

cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM cannabis_sales;")
result = cursor.fetchone()
print("Row count:", result[0])



9.6.2 The preferred workflow: pd.read_sql_query()
This function sends a SQL query and returns a DataFrame — exactly like pd.read_csv(), but reading from a database instead of a file:

df = pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM cannabis_sales LIMIT 10;", conn)
df

The result is a real pandas DataFrame. All the methods you learned in previous chapters — .shape, .dtypes, .describe(), .value_counts() — work exactly the same way.

title: 9  Loading Data with SQL
text:
9.7 SQL Syntax Fundamentals
Every query you write follows a standard clause structure. The clauses must appear in this order:
SELECT   county, year, retail_sales      -- 1. Which columns to return
FROM     cannabis_sales                  -- 2. Which table to read from
WHERE    year = 2023                     -- 3. Which rows to keep (filter)
GROUP BY county                          -- 4. How to collapse rows into groups
HAVING   SUM(retail_sales) > 1000000     -- 5. Filter on group-level results
ORDER BY retail_sales DESC               -- 6. How to sort the output
LIMIT    10;                             -- 7. Maximum rows to return
Not every clause is required. A minimal query needs only SELECT and FROM:
SELECT * FROM cannabis_sales;

9.7.1 Keywords and case
SQL is case-insensitive for keywords — SELECT, select, and Select all work. The convention is to capitalize SQL keywords and use lowercase for table names, column names, and aliases. This helps your eye parse the clause structure quickly.


9.7.2 Literals
When you write a value directly in a query, always use single quotes for text strings ('Boulder'), no quotes for numbers (2023), and NULL for missing values. Double quotes in SQLite are for identifiers (column names), not string values.


9.7.3 The NULL gotcha
NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL, not TRUE. You cannot use = to test for missing values. Always use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL.

title: 9  Loading Data with SQL
text:
9.8 Exploratory Queries
With the data loaded and a Python connection established, apply the post-load checklist using SQL:

# Shape equivalent
pd.read_sql_query("SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_rows FROM cannabis_sales;", conn)


# Head equivalent
pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM cannabis_sales LIMIT 5;", conn)


# Value counts equivalent
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT county, COUNT(*) AS n_months
    FROM cannabis_sales
    GROUP BY county
    ORDER BY n_months DESC
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)


# Describe equivalent for a numeric column
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        MIN(retail_sales) AS min_val,
        MAX(retail_sales) AS max_val,
        AVG(retail_sales) AS mean_val,
        COUNT(*) AS n_rows,
        SUM(CASE WHEN retail_sales IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS n_nulls
    FROM cannabis_sales;
""", conn)

title: 9  Loading Data with SQL
9  Loading Data with SQL
text:
9.10 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningError: near "<dot-command>": syntax error



What it means: You tried to run a dot-command ending with a semicolon, or you tried to run dot-commands inside a .sql file.
How to fix it: Dot-commands (like .tables, .mode csv, .import) are shell commands — no semicolon. SQL statements (like SELECT ...;) always end with a semicolon. They belong in different places.








WarningError: no such table: cannabis_sales



What it means: Your connection is pointing at the wrong database file (often a brand-new empty one created accidentally). sqlite3.connect('cannabis.db') silently creates an empty file if cannabis.db is not in the current directory.
How to fix it: Run print(os.getcwd()) in your notebook. Confirm cannabis.db is in that directory. Use an absolute path if necessary. Verify the file has tables with sqlite3 cannabis.db ".tables" from the terminal.








WarningThe header row got imported as data



What it means: You forgot .headers on before .import, so SQLite treated the first row as data.
How to fix it: Run DELETE FROM <table> WHERE <text_col> = '<header_name>'; to remove the stray row. Then rebuild the workflow with .headers on set before .import.








WarningA numeric column contains NULL where you expected numbers



What it means: SQLite does not enforce storage classes strictly — a TEXT value in an INTEGER column is stored as text unless you pre-cleaned it. If you loaded a CSV with $1,234 still present, the column is a mix of text and numbers.
How to fix it: Clean the CSV in pandas before importing. .describe() in pandas and SELECT MIN(...), MAX(...), COUNT(...) in SQL will reveal the issue. See Chapter 6.

title: 9  Loading Data with SQL
text:
9.11 Key Takeaways

SQLite is a file-based database: your whole database is one .db file. No server, no setup wizard.
Always clean CSVs in pandas before .import. SQLite expects rectangular, single-header-row data.
Dot-commands control the shell; SQL statements operate on data. Know which is which.
pd.read_sql_query(sql, conn) is the bridge — it returns a pandas DataFrame, so every pandas skill you built in Part II still applies.
The SQL query structure (SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE ... GROUP BY ... HAVING ... ORDER BY ... LIMIT) appears in a fixed order. Memorize the order; the clauses inside each are flexible.

title: 9  Loading Data with SQL
text:
9.12 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on SQL and SQLite






SQLite documentation — sqlite.org/docs.html is the canonical reference. Start with “SQL syntax” and “Command-line shell.”
Python sqlite3 module — docs.python.org/3/library/sqlite3.html documents the Python driver.
pandas: read_sql_query — pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.read_sql_query.html is the API reference.
SQLZoo — sqlzoo.net is a free, interactive SQL tutorial that runs in the browser. Highly recommended for drilling query syntax.
Mode’s SQL Tutorial — mode.com/sql-tutorial/ walks through SQL with realistic business datasets.

title: 9  Loading Data with SQL
text:
9.13 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Create and load a second table





Clean the Colorado population data in pandas, export it as a CSV, define a schema for a population table, and load it into the same database. Run the three post-load diagnostic checks.






NoteSolution






pop = pd.read_csv('sya-county.csv', skiprows=1, dtype={'countyfips': str})
pop = pop[pop['datatype'] == 'Estimate']
pop.to_csv('population_clean.csv', index=False)

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS population (
    countyfips TEXT,
    county TEXT,
    year INTEGER,
    age INTEGER,
    malepopulation INTEGER,
    femalepopulation INTEGER,
    totalpopulation INTEGER,
    datatype TEXT
);
Then import using .mode csv, .headers on, and .import population_clean.csv population.












NoteExercise 2: Diagnostic queries





Write SQL queries to answer: (a) How many distinct counties are in the cannabis_sales table? (b) What is the year range? (c) How many rows have NULL retail_sales? (d) Which county has the most months of data?






NoteSolution






queries = [
    "SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT county) AS n_counties FROM cannabis_sales;",
    "SELECT MIN(year) AS min_year, MAX(year) AS max_year FROM cannabis_sales;",
    "SELECT COUNT(*) AS null_retail FROM cannabis_sales WHERE retail_sales IS NULL;",
    """SELECT county, COUNT(*) AS n_months FROM cannabis_sales
       GROUP BY county ORDER BY n_months DESC LIMIT 5;"""
]

for q in queries:
    print(pd.read_sql_query(q, conn))
    print()

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
Apply a five-stage Inspect → Clean → Design Schema → Load → Validate workflow to a messy dataset.
Write SQL diagnostic queries for missingness, duplicates, inconsistent categories, and invalid ranges.
Use SQLite’s built-in string functions (TRIM, UPPER, LOWER, REPLACE) and CASE WHEN expressions to clean columns.
Diagnose and repair silent join failures caused by inconsistent key formatting across agency sources.
Consolidate cleaned tables from multiple source databases into a single analysis database using DataFrame.to_sql.

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.2 Why SQL for Cleaning
Cleaning data in SQL follows the same logic you learned in pandas — identify missingness, duplicates, inconsistent categories, and invalid ranges — but uses a different set of tools. SQL excels at diagnostic queries: you can characterize problems across an entire table with a single statement, and the declarative syntax makes your logic explicit and auditable. This chapter applies a structured diagnostic framework to three real Colorado government datasets, loads them into a SQLite database, and demonstrates a critical lesson about silent join failures caused by inconsistent key formatting across sources.
The silent join failure in this chapter is the clearest example in the book of a bug that does not look like a bug. A query runs, returns results, and those results are systematically wrong. Reading this chapter carefully will save you days of confusion in your future data work.

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.3 The Diagnostic Framework
Every cleaning session in this chapter follows the same five stages:
Inspect → Clean → Design Schema → Load → Validate
Within the Inspect stage, we run four standard diagnostics, one per category of data quality issue:

Missingness — which columns have nulls, and how many?
Duplicates — are there repeated rows on the expected key columns?
Inconsistent categories — do text columns have variant spellings, extra whitespace, or unexpected values?
Invalid ranges — are numeric and date columns within plausible bounds?

This is the same INSPECT → TRANSFORM → VALIDATE → DOCUMENT workflow from the pandas chapters, specialized for SQL.

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.4 Setup
This chapter uses Python’s built-in sqlite3 module, pandas, and numpy.

import sqlite3
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', 20)
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', 55)

conn = sqlite3.connect('colorado_public_health.db')
cursor = conn.cursor()







WarningIf you get an ImportError





sqlite3 is part of the Python standard library. For pandas or numpy: conda install pandas numpy or pip install pandas numpy. Restart your Jupyter kernel after installing. See Chapter 1.

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.5 Datasets
This chapter works with three Colorado government datasets that will be joined together in Chapter 12.








File
Source
Description




cdc_deaths.csv
CDC WONDER
Colorado mortality counts by county, year, age group, and ICD-10 chapter


cbi_crime.csv
Colorado Bureau of Investigation
Reported offenses by jurisdiction, date, and offense type


sya-county.csv
State Demography Office
Single-year-of-age population estimates by county and year

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.6 Part 1: CDC Mortality Data

10.6.1 Inspect
Load the raw file in pandas and run each diagnostic.

deaths_raw = pd.read_csv('cdc_deaths.csv')
print('Shape:', deaths_raw.shape)
deaths_raw.head()


10.6.1.1 Missingness

print(deaths_raw.isna().sum())



10.6.1.2 Duplicates

key_cols = ['County', 'Year', 'Age Group', 'ICD Chapter']
print('Duplicates on key:', deaths_raw.duplicated(subset=key_cols).sum())



10.6.1.3 Inconsistent categories

print(deaths_raw['County'].value_counts().head(10))

Pay attention to the format of county names. Does this dataset use 'Adams', 'Adams County', or 'Adams County, CO'? This will matter critically when we join tables later.


10.6.1.4 Invalid ranges

print('Year range:', deaths_raw['Year'].min(), '–', deaths_raw['Year'].max())
print('Deaths range:', deaths_raw['Deaths'].min(), '–', deaths_raw['Deaths'].max())




10.6.2 Clean
Apply cleaning operations in pandas based on what the diagnostics revealed. Strip whitespace from text columns, standardize county names, and handle any sentinel values (CDC WONDER uses “Suppressed” and “Unreliable” as data quality flags).

deaths_clean = deaths_raw.copy()

# Strip whitespace from all text columns
for col in deaths_clean.select_dtypes(include='object').columns:
    deaths_clean[col] = deaths_clean[col].str.strip()



10.6.3 Design Schema
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS deaths (
    county TEXT,
    year INTEGER,
    age_group TEXT,
    icd_chapter TEXT,
    deaths INTEGER
);


10.6.4 Load

deaths_clean.to_csv('deaths_clean.csv', index=False)



10.6.5 Validate
After loading into SQLite, run diagnostic queries to confirm the import matches the pandas source:

pd.read_sql_query("SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_rows FROM deaths;", conn)


pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT county, COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM deaths
    GROUP BY county
    ORDER BY n DESC
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)

Cross-check the SQL count against the pandas count — they should match exactly.

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.7 Part 2: CBI Crime Data
The same five-stage process applies. The crime data introduces additional challenges: jurisdiction names that do not match county names, nullable columns with complex patterns, and timestamp fields that require careful parsing.

crime_raw = pd.read_csv('cbi_crime.csv')
print('Shape:', crime_raw.shape)
crime_raw.head()

Run all four diagnostics (missingness, duplicates, inconsistent categories, invalid ranges), clean the data, design a schema, load it, and validate the load.

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.8 Part 3: Population Estimates

pop_raw = pd.read_csv('sya-county.csv', skiprows=1, dtype={'countyfips': str})
pop_raw = pop_raw[pop_raw['datatype'] == 'Estimate'].copy()
print('Shape:', pop_raw.shape)
pop_raw.head()

The population data has its own quirks: a header row that needs to be skipped, a datatype column that mixes estimates and forecasts, and county names in yet another format.

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.9 Part 4: The Silent Join Failure
This is the most important section of the chapter. After loading all three tables, we discover that county names are formatted differently in each source:



Table
Example county name




deaths
Adams County, CO


crime
Adams County


population
Adams



What happens when you try to join these tables on county name? Nothing matches. The join produces zero rows — silently, with no error message. This is a silent join failure, and it is one of the most dangerous problems in data work because it looks like the query succeeded (it ran without errors) but the result is empty or incomplete.

10.9.1 Demonstrating the failure

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT d.county AS death_county, p.county AS pop_county, COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM deaths d
    JOIN population p ON d.county = p.county
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)

If this returns zero rows, the county formats do not match.


10.9.2 Diagnosing the mismatch

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT DISTINCT county FROM deaths ORDER BY county LIMIT 10;
""", conn)


pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT DISTINCT county FROM population ORDER BY county LIMIT 10;
""", conn)



10.9.3 Fixing the mismatch
The fix is to standardize county names to a common format before loading, or to use SQL string functions to normalize them at query time. The REPLACE() and TRIM() functions in SQLite can help, but the cleanest approach is to fix the data in pandas before loading:

# Standardize all county names to bare names: 'Adams'
# From 'Adams County, CO' → remove ', CO' then remove ' County'
# From 'Adams County' → remove ' County'
# From 'Adams' → already correct



10.9.4 The lesson
County name mismatches across sources are not a quirk of these particular datasets — they are a universal pattern. Any time you join data from different sources on a text key, you must verify that the key values actually match before running the join. The diagnostic pattern is: list the distinct values from each table, compare them, and fix any mismatches before joining.

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.10 Public Interest Context: Erosion
The three county name formats — "Adams County, CO", "Adams County", "Adams" — come from three Colorado government agencies that built their data systems independently, at different times, with no shared naming authority. No one decided to make them incompatible. The mismatch is the accumulated result of decades of decentralized infrastructure development: standards drift, staff turn over, systems migrate. Government databases accumulate inconsistencies the way old buildings accumulate code violations — one small deviation at a time, each rational in context, collectively a mess.
The standardization work you just completed (removing ", CO" and " County") is the maintenance labor that keeps public records usable. The fix lives in your notebook; the underlying erosion persists in the source systems, waiting for the next analyst to rediscover it. What other erosion is baked into the datasets you work with, and how much of your cleaning code is really maintenance labor on infrastructure the public depends on?






NoteConnection to Chapter 4



This section connects to the concept of erosion introduced in Chapter 4. Cross-agency data inconsistencies are the visible face of infrastructure erosion, and SQL cleaning is the labor that makes public records usable despite that erosion.

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.11 SQL Functions for Cleaning
Several SQL functions are useful for cleaning operations:








Function
Purpose
Example




TRIM(col)
Remove leading/trailing whitespace
SELECT TRIM(county) FROM deaths;


UPPER(col)
Convert to uppercase
SELECT UPPER(county) FROM deaths;


LOWER(col)
Convert to lowercase
SELECT LOWER(county) FROM deaths;


REPLACE(col, old, new)
Replace substring
SELECT REPLACE(county, ' County', '') FROM deaths;


CAST(col AS type)
Change data type
SELECT CAST(year AS INTEGER) FROM deaths;


COALESCE(col, default)
Replace NULL with default
SELECT COALESCE(deaths, 0) FROM deaths;


CASE WHEN
Conditional logic
See below




10.11.1 CASE WHEN for conditional cleaning
SELECT
    county,
    CASE
        WHEN deaths IS NULL THEN 'Missing'
        WHEN deaths = 0 THEN 'Zero'
        WHEN deaths < 10 THEN 'Suppressed range'
        ELSE 'Normal'
    END AS death_status
FROM deaths;

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.13 Saving the Cleaned Tables
After validating each dataset, collect the cleaned, analysis-ready versions into a single SQLite database for use in Chapter 12. That chapter expects one deaths table, one crime table, and one population table — all in colorado_public_health.db, with county names standardized to bare county names (e.g., Adams) and all rows validated.
The cleanest approach is to read each table from its source database into a pandas DataFrame, apply the county-name standardization you diagnosed above, and write to the shared database using to_sql:

import pandas as pd
import sqlite3

# Read cleaned tables from their source databases
conn_deaths = sqlite3.connect('cdc_deaths.db')
conn_crime = sqlite3.connect('cbi_crime.db')
conn_pop = sqlite3.connect('population.db')

deaths = pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM deaths", conn_deaths)
crime = pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM crime", conn_crime)
population = pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM population", conn_pop)

# Standardize county names to bare format (e.g., "Adams County, CO" -> "Adams")
deaths['county'] = (deaths['county']
    .str.replace(r',\s*CO$', '', regex=True)   # remove ", CO"
    .str.replace(r'\s+County$', '', regex=True) # remove " County"
    .str.strip()
)

crime['county'] = (crime['county']
    .str.replace(r'\s+County$', '', regex=True)
    .str.strip()
)
# population already uses bare county names

# Write to shared database
conn_ph = sqlite3.connect('colorado_public_health.db')
deaths.to_sql('deaths', conn_ph, if_exists='replace', index=False)
crime.to_sql('crime', conn_ph, if_exists='replace', index=False)
population.to_sql('population', conn_ph, if_exists='replace', index=False)

print("Tables written to colorado_public_health.db:")
for tbl in ['deaths', 'crime', 'population']:
    n = pd.read_sql_query(f"SELECT COUNT(*) AS n FROM {tbl}", conn_ph).iloc[0, 0]
    print(f"  {tbl}: {n:,} rows")

conn_deaths.close(); conn_crime.close(); conn_pop.close(); conn_ph.close()







TipWhy not write directly from SQL?



SQLite does not have a built-in cross-database INSERT INTO ... SELECT FROM for different .db files. The pandas round-trip (read into DataFrame, standardize, write back) is the practical approach for consolidating multiple source databases into one analysis database.

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.14 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningA join on a text key returns zero rows



What it means: A silent join failure. Your keys look similar but are not identical — often "Adams" vs "Adams County" vs "Adams County, CO".
How to fix it: Run SELECT DISTINCT county FROM <table> ORDER BY county on each side of the join. Compare the results. Normalize in pandas (or with SQLite TRIM/REPLACE) before joining.








WarningTRIM does not catch some whitespace



What it means: Non-breaking spaces (Unicode U+00A0) and tab characters are whitespace to humans but not to SQLite’s default TRIM. A column can still fail to match after TRIM if the whitespace is unusual.
How to fix it: Check for unusual whitespace in pandas with df['col'].str.contains(r'\s', regex=True) and explicitly replace \xa0 and other characters before exporting. In SQL, REPLACE(col, char(160), ' ') handles non-breaking spaces.








WarningCAST(x AS INTEGER) returns 0 unexpectedly



What it means: SQLite’s CAST returns 0 for strings that cannot be parsed. A column with $1,234 gets stored as text, and CAST silently produces zeros.
How to fix it: Clean currency/comma characters before loading. In SQLite, chain CAST(REPLACE(REPLACE(col, '$', ''), ',', '') AS INTEGER) — but doing the cleanup in pandas first is clearer and easier to audit.








WarningNULL behaves unexpectedly in conditions



What it means: NULL = NULL is NULL (not TRUE), and NULL = 'Adams' is NULL. Any row with NULL in the compared column is silently excluded.
How to fix it: Always use IS NULL and IS NOT NULL for null checks. Use COALESCE(col, default) when you need a default value.

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.15 Key Takeaways

Cleaning in SQL follows the same Inspect → Clean → Validate → Document rhythm as pandas; only the syntax changes.
The four diagnostic categories — missingness, duplicates, inconsistent categories, invalid ranges — are worth running on every new table.
Silent join failures are the most dangerous SQL bug class. Always run SELECT DISTINCT on join keys before joining.
SQLite’s type system is permissive. Column names and types in your schema do not enforce what is actually stored — audit after every load.
When a technical fix (standardizing county names) encodes a governance problem (three agencies, no shared convention), document the fix in your Known Issues Log and the provenance in your Data Dictionary.

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.16 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on SQL cleaning and SQLite internals






SQLite: Core built-in functions — sqlite.org/lang_corefunc.html lists every built-in function (TRIM, UPPER, COALESCE, etc.).
SQLite: Datatypes in SQLite — sqlite.org/datatype3.html explains SQLite’s flexible (and sometimes surprising) type system.
SQLite: Expression reference — sqlite.org/lang_expr.html covers CASE WHEN, IS NULL, operators, and NULL semantics.
pandas: to_sql API — pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.DataFrame.to_sql.html is the reference for writing DataFrames into SQLite.
CDC WONDER help — wonder.cdc.gov/wonder/help/main.html documents the suppression rules referenced throughout this chapter.

title: 10  Cleaning Data with SQL
text:
10.17 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Missingness by year





Write a SQL query that shows the number of NULL death counts per year in the deaths table. Which years have the most missing data?






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT year,
           COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
           SUM(CASE WHEN deaths IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS n_null
    FROM deaths
    GROUP BY year
    ORDER BY year;
""", conn)













NoteExercise 2: TRIM audit





Write a query that identifies rows in the crime table where the jurisdiction column has leading or trailing whitespace (i.e., jurisdiction <> TRIM(jurisdiction)). How many rows are affected?






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS whitespace_rows
    FROM crime
    WHERE jurisdiction <> TRIM(jurisdiction);
""", conn)













NoteExercise 3: Cross-table county audit





Write queries that list the distinct county names from all three tables. Identify at least two counties whose names differ across sources. Propose a standardization strategy.

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
text:
11.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
text:
Write GROUP BY queries using SQLite’s aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, GROUP_CONCAT).
Distinguish WHERE (filters rows before grouping) from HAVING (filters groups after aggregation).
Use CASE WHEN expressions inside aggregates for conditional counting and cross-tabulation.
Build subqueries in SELECT, WHERE, and FROM clauses.
Write multi-step analytical pipelines using Common Table Expressions (CTEs) with the WITH ... AS syntax.

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
text:
11.2 Why SQL for Extending
Loading and cleaning produce reliable tables. Extending transforms those tables into answers. This chapter introduces the SQL tools for computing metrics and testing analytical claims: GROUP BY with aggregate functions, subqueries that embed one query inside another, and Common Table Expressions (CTEs) that name intermediate results for clarity and reuse. Together, these tools let you write complex analytical pipelines entirely in SQL — pipelines that are readable, auditable, and reproducible.
The dataset for this chapter is the Boulder County Assessor’s buildings file, which contains one row per improvement section recorded for each parcel. It is a rich, messy, real-world dataset with dozens of columns describing building characteristics — year built, square footage, quality grade, heating system, roof type, and more.
CTEs are the single biggest readability upgrade you can make to your SQL. If a query is turning into a wall of nested parentheses, that is a signal to break it apart with WITH. Your reviewer — even your future self — will thank you.

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
text:
11.3 Setup
This chapter uses Python’s built-in sqlite3, pandas, and numpy.

import sqlite3
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', 20)
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', 55)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.2f}'.format)







WarningIf you get an ImportError





sqlite3 is part of Python’s standard library. For pandas or numpy: conda install pandas numpy or pip install pandas numpy, then restart the Jupyter kernel. See Chapter 1.




# Load the CSV, clean, and write to a local SQLite database
buildings_raw = pd.read_csv('buildings.csv')

# Strip trailing whitespace from text columns
for col in buildings_raw.select_dtypes(include='object').columns:
    buildings_raw[col] = buildings_raw[col].str.strip()

# Write to database
conn = sqlite3.connect('boulder_buildings.db')
buildings_raw.to_sql('buildings', conn, if_exists='replace', index=False)

print(f"Loaded {len(buildings_raw):,} rows into buildings table")

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
section: 11.4 Part 1: GROUP BY and Aggregate Functions
text:
11.4 Part 1: GROUP BY and Aggregate Functions

11.4.1 What GROUP BY does
GROUP BY collapses rows that share the same value(s) in one or more columns into a single summary row. It is the SQL equivalent of pandas’ groupby().agg() and the conceptual equivalent of a spreadsheet pivot table.
The key rule: every column in your SELECT must be either listed in GROUP BY or wrapped in an aggregate function. You cannot select a raw column alongside grouped aggregates — SQL would not know which row’s value to display.

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        quality_grade,
        COUNT(*) AS n_buildings,
        AVG(above_ground_sqft) AS avg_sqft,
        MIN(year_built) AS oldest,
        MAX(year_built) AS newest
    FROM buildings
    WHERE quality_grade IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY quality_grade
    ORDER BY n_buildings DESC;
""", conn)



11.4.2 SQLite aggregate functions








Function
Purpose
Notes




COUNT(*)
Number of rows in group
Counts all rows, including NULLs


COUNT(col)
Number of non-NULL values
Skips NULLs — use to measure completeness


SUM(col)
Sum of values
Returns NULL if all values are NULL


TOTAL(col)
Sum of values
Returns 0.0 if all values are NULL (safer)


AVG(col)
Mean of non-NULL values
Ignores NULLs — denominator is count of non-NULL


MIN(col) / MAX(col)
Extremes
Works on text (alphabetical) and numbers


GROUP_CONCAT(col)
Concatenate values into a string
Useful for inventory lists



The distinction between COUNT(*) and COUNT(col) is critical. COUNT(*) counts all rows regardless of NULL values. COUNT(col) counts only rows where col is not NULL. Comparing the two tells you the completeness rate of a column.

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
        COUNT(year_built) AS non_null_year,
        COUNT(*) - COUNT(year_built) AS null_year,
        ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(year_built) / COUNT(*), 1) AS pct_complete
    FROM buildings;
""", conn)



11.4.3 HAVING vs. WHERE
Both filter rows, but they operate at different stages of query execution. WHERE filters individual rows before grouping. HAVING filters groups after aggregation.

# WHERE: filter rows before grouping
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT quality_grade, COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM buildings
    WHERE year_built >= 2000
    GROUP BY quality_grade
    ORDER BY n DESC;
""", conn)


# HAVING: filter groups after aggregation
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT quality_grade, COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM buildings
    GROUP BY quality_grade
    HAVING n >= 100
    ORDER BY n DESC;
""", conn)

A useful mnemonic: WHERE answers “which rows go into the groups?” while HAVING answers “which groups appear in the output?”


11.4.4 CASE WHEN inside aggregation
CASE WHEN expressions inside aggregate functions enable conditional counting — the SQL equivalent of COUNTIF in a spreadsheet. This is one of the most powerful patterns in SQL.

# Cross-tab: count buildings by decade and class
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        (year_built / 10) * 10 AS decade,
        SUM(CASE WHEN bldg_class = 'R' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS residential,
        SUM(CASE WHEN bldg_class = 'C' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS commercial,
        SUM(CASE WHEN bldg_class NOT IN ('R', 'C') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS other,
        COUNT(*) AS total
    FROM buildings
    WHERE year_built IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY decade
    ORDER BY decade;
""", conn)



11.4.5 Working with Dates in SQL: strftime()
Government datasets often store dates as separate integer columns rather than a single date column. The cannabis sales data stores month and year as separate integers. SQL can reconstruct a date string from these using strftime() and string concatenation.

# First, load the cleaned cannabis data if you have it
cannabis_conn = sqlite3.connect('boulder_buildings.db')  # reuse same connection

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        strftime('%Y-Q', year || '-' || printf('%02d', month) || '-01') AS quarter,
        SUM(retail_sales) AS total_retail,
        COUNT(DISTINCT county) AS n_counties
    FROM cannabis_sales
    WHERE county != 'Total'
    GROUP BY quarter
    ORDER BY quarter
    LIMIT 12;
""", conn)

printf('%02d', month) left-pads the month integer with a zero so that 3 becomes 03. SQLite’s strftime() then accepts the reconstructed date string 'YYYY-MM-DD' and formats it however you need.






TipSQLite date functions are string-based



Unlike PostgreSQL or MySQL, SQLite has no native DATE type. Dates are stored as text, integer, or real values, and strftime() operates on text in ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD). As long as your date strings are in that format, strftime() gives you full access to year, month, quarter, day-of-week, and Julian day calculations.

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
text:
11.5 Part 1 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Construction era distribution





Write a query that groups buildings into construction eras — pre-1950, 1950–1979, 1980–1999, 2000–present — and counts the number of buildings in each era. Use CASE WHEN to create the era labels.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        CASE
            WHEN year_built < 1950 THEN 'Pre-1950'
            WHEN year_built < 1980 THEN '1950-1979'
            WHEN year_built < 2000 THEN '1980-1999'
            ELSE '2000-present'
        END AS era,
        COUNT(*) AS n_buildings,
        ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
    FROM buildings
    WHERE year_built IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY era
    ORDER BY MIN(year_built);
""", conn)













NoteExercise 2: Heating system prevalence





Write a query that shows the top 10 heating system types by count, along with the average square footage and average year built for buildings with each heating type. Exclude rows where the heating system is NULL.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT
        heating_type,
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft,
        ROUND(AVG(year_built), 0) AS avg_year
    FROM buildings
    WHERE heating_type IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY heating_type
    ORDER BY n DESC
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
text:
11.6 Part 2: Subqueries
A subquery is a SELECT statement nested inside another query. It lets you compute a value or a set of values dynamically and use the result in a filter, comparison, or derived table — without hardcoding anything.

11.6.1 Scalar subquery: comparing each row to a reference value
A scalar subquery returns a single value. You can use it anywhere a literal value would go:

# Find buildings larger than the overall average
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT strap, year_built, above_ground_sqft
    FROM buildings
    WHERE above_ground_sqft > (SELECT AVG(above_ground_sqft) FROM buildings)
    ORDER BY above_ground_sqft DESC
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)

The subquery (SELECT AVG(above_ground_sqft) FROM buildings) computes the mean once, and the outer query uses it to filter rows. This is more robust than hardcoding the average — if the data changes, the query automatically adapts.


11.6.2 WHERE IN: filtering against a dynamically computed set

# Find buildings in quality grades that have above-average mean square footage
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT strap, quality_grade, above_ground_sqft
    FROM buildings
    WHERE quality_grade IN (
        SELECT quality_grade
        FROM buildings
        WHERE quality_grade IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY quality_grade
        HAVING AVG(above_ground_sqft) > (SELECT AVG(above_ground_sqft) FROM buildings)
    )
    ORDER BY above_ground_sqft DESC
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)



11.6.3 Derived table: wrapping aggregate results
A subquery in the FROM clause creates a temporary table (called a derived table) that the outer query can filter, join, or aggregate further:

# Rank quality grades by their median-like percentile
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT *
    FROM (
        SELECT
            quality_grade,
            COUNT(*) AS n,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
        FROM buildings
        WHERE quality_grade IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY quality_grade
    ) AS grade_stats
    WHERE n >= 50
    ORDER BY avg_sqft DESC;
""", conn)

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
text:
11.7 Part 2 Exercises






NoteExercise 3: Scalar subquery — newest buildings





Write a query that finds all buildings built in the most recent year in the database (without hardcoding the year). How many are there?






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_newest,
           MAX(year_built) AS year
    FROM buildings
    WHERE year_built = (SELECT MAX(year_built) FROM buildings);
""", conn)













NoteExercise 4: WHERE NOT IN





Write a query that finds parcels (strap values) that have only non-residential building sections. Hint: find all parcels that appear in residential rows, then select parcels NOT IN that set.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT DISTINCT strap
    FROM buildings
    WHERE strap NOT IN (
        SELECT DISTINCT strap
        FROM buildings
        WHERE bldg_class = 'R'
    )
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
text:
11.8 Part 3: Common Table Expressions (CTEs)
A CTE is a named temporary result set defined at the top of a query with WITH ... AS. It makes complex queries readable by breaking them into named, sequential steps — the SQL equivalent of assigning intermediate DataFrames to variables in pandas.

11.8.1 The WITH … AS pattern
Compare these two approaches to the same question — “which quality grades have above-average square footage?”:
-- Subquery style (hard to read at depth)
SELECT * FROM (
    SELECT quality_grade, AVG(above_ground_sqft) AS avg_sqft
    FROM buildings GROUP BY quality_grade
) WHERE avg_sqft > (SELECT AVG(above_ground_sqft) FROM buildings);

-- CTE style (name the intermediate result)
WITH grade_stats AS (
    SELECT quality_grade, AVG(above_ground_sqft) AS avg_sqft
    FROM buildings
    GROUP BY quality_grade
)
SELECT *
FROM grade_stats
WHERE avg_sqft > (SELECT AVG(above_ground_sqft) FROM buildings);
Both produce the same result. The CTE version is easier to read, debug, and extend.


11.8.2 Single CTE

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH grade_stats AS (
        SELECT
            quality_grade,
            COUNT(*) AS n,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft,
            ROUND(AVG(year_built), 0) AS avg_year
        FROM buildings
        WHERE quality_grade IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY quality_grade
    )
    SELECT *
    FROM grade_stats
    WHERE n >= 50
    ORDER BY avg_sqft DESC;
""", conn)



11.8.3 Chained CTEs: multi-step analytical pipeline
You can define multiple CTEs separated by commas, each referencing the ones defined before it:

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH era_stats AS (
        SELECT
            (year_built / 10) * 10 AS decade,
            COUNT(*) AS n,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
        FROM buildings
        WHERE year_built IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY decade
    ),
    overall AS (
        SELECT ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS grand_avg
        FROM buildings
        WHERE year_built IS NOT NULL
    )
    SELECT
        e.decade,
        e.n,
        e.avg_sqft,
        o.grand_avg,
        ROUND(e.avg_sqft - o.grand_avg, 0) AS diff_from_avg
    FROM era_stats e, overall o
    ORDER BY e.decade;
""", conn)

This pattern — define intermediate results as named CTEs, then combine them in a final SELECT — is the standard way to build complex SQL analyses. It makes every step visible and auditable.

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
text:
11.9 Part 3 Exercises






NoteExercise 5: CTE — quality grade leaderboard





Write a CTE-based query that ranks quality grades by total square footage. Include the count of buildings, total square footage, and average square footage per building.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH grade_totals AS (
        SELECT
            quality_grade,
            COUNT(*) AS n_buildings,
            SUM(above_ground_sqft) AS total_sqft,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
        FROM buildings
        WHERE quality_grade IS NOT NULL AND above_ground_sqft IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY quality_grade
    )
    SELECT *
    FROM grade_totals
    ORDER BY total_sqft DESC;
""", conn)













NoteExercise 6: Chained CTEs — era comparison





Write a query with two CTEs: one for pre-2000 buildings and one for 2000-and-later buildings. Compare the average square footage, count, and most common quality grade between the two eras.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH pre_2000 AS (
        SELECT
            'Pre-2000' AS era,
            COUNT(*) AS n,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
        FROM buildings
        WHERE year_built < 2000 AND year_built IS NOT NULL
    ),
    post_2000 AS (
        SELECT
            '2000+' AS era,
            COUNT(*) AS n,
            ROUND(AVG(above_ground_sqft), 0) AS avg_sqft
        FROM buildings
        WHERE year_built >= 2000 AND year_built IS NOT NULL
    )
    SELECT * FROM pre_2000
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM post_2000;
""", conn)

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
text:
11.10 SQL ↔︎ pandas Equivalents







SQL
pandas




GROUP BY col
df.groupby('col')


COUNT(*)
.size() or .count()


SUM(col), AVG(col)
.agg({'col': 'sum'}), .agg({'col': 'mean'})


HAVING COUNT(*) > 10
Filter after .groupby().agg()


CASE WHEN ... END
np.where() or pd.cut()


Scalar subquery
Compute value, then use in filter


CTE (WITH ... AS)
Assign intermediate DataFrame to a variable


WHERE col IN (subquery)
df[df['col'].isin(other_df['col'])]

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
text:
11.11 Quick Reference

11.11.1 Subquery patterns

Scalar: returns one value → use in WHERE, SELECT, or HAVING
Column: returns one column → use with IN or NOT IN
Derived table: returns a table → use in FROM clause



11.11.2 CTE pattern
WITH step1 AS (
    SELECT ... FROM ... GROUP BY ...
),
step2 AS (
    SELECT ... FROM step1 WHERE ...
)
SELECT ... FROM step2;
CTEs are evaluated once, named for clarity, and discarded after the query completes. Use them whenever a query needs more than one level of aggregation or filtering.

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
text:
11.12 Public Interest Context: Oversight
The pattern you just learned — write a diagnostic query, aggregate by a meaningful grouping, inspect the result, follow up with a CTE that combines several intermediate queries — is the fundamental move of investigative journalism and algorithmic auditing. When a reporter at The Marshall Project quantifies death-in-custody patterns across jurisdictions, or a civic technologist at Muckrock analyzes FOIA response times across agencies, or an academic audit team quantifies disparate outcomes across institutions, the underlying query looks a lot like the ones in this chapter: group by some institutional unit, aggregate a quality measure, filter with HAVING to surface the outliers, and write it up.
SQL’s value here is oversight in the literal sense: declarative queries leave a trail that someone else can re-run. The CTE you wrote to compute era statistics is, for a later auditor, a reproducible claim. The same cannot be said of a spreadsheet formula that was overwritten or a custom Python loop that depends on a bespoke environment. When you write a SQL query that answers an accountability question, you are also writing infrastructure that others can reuse to ask it again next year.






NoteConnection to Chapter 4



This section connects to the concept of oversight introduced in Chapter 4. SQL aggregation and CTEs are the grammar of reproducible audit questions — the queries that journalists and researchers use to hold institutions accountable.

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
text:
11.13 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningError: misuse of aggregate or non-aggregated columns in SELECT



What it means: Every column in a SELECT with GROUP BY must either appear in GROUP BY or be wrapped in an aggregate function. SQLite enforces this loosely but will return unpredictable values for non-aggregated columns.
How to fix it: Add the missing column to GROUP BY, or wrap it in MIN(), MAX(), or another aggregate. If you want a per-group value that is not an aggregate (like a representative name), think about whether you actually want GROUP BY or whether a window function / CTE is more appropriate.








WarningAVG() silently excludes NULL rows



What it means: AVG(col) returns the mean of non-NULL values. If many rows have NULL in that column, your average is over a smaller group than you expected.
How to fix it: Compare COUNT(*) with COUNT(col) to see the completeness rate. If you want to treat NULL as zero, use AVG(COALESCE(col, 0)). Document the choice in your Known Issues Log either way.








WarningA subquery returns more than one row where a scalar is expected



What it means: You used a subquery in a position that expects a single value (WHERE x > (SELECT ...)), but the subquery returned multiple rows.
How to fix it: Add an aggregate (AVG, MAX, MIN) to the inner query. Or switch to IN (SELECT ...) if you meant “any of these values.”








Warningstrftime returns NULL



What it means: The input string is not in ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD), or the month is stored as 3 instead of 03.
How to fix it: Use printf('%02d', month) to zero-pad single-digit months. Double-check the reconstructed string with a SELECT before applying strftime.

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
text:
11.14 Key Takeaways

GROUP BY + aggregates is the SQL equivalent of df.groupby().agg(). Same idea, different syntax.
WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after aggregation. Getting this order right is half of reading a GROUP BY query correctly.
CASE WHEN inside aggregates is the most powerful pattern in this chapter — it unlocks cross-tabulation, conditional counting, and every other “if-then” summary.
Prefer CTEs over nested subqueries when a query has more than one level of aggregation. They are easier to read, debug, and modify.
SQL queries are declarative: you describe the result, not the procedure. The database engine figures out the execution plan.

title: 11  Extending Data with SQL
text:
11.15 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on SQL aggregation, subqueries, and CTEs






SQLite: Aggregate functions — sqlite.org/lang_aggfunc.html.
SQLite: The SELECT statement — sqlite.org/lang_select.html covers subqueries, HAVING, and clause ordering.
SQLite: WITH clause (CTEs) — sqlite.org/lang_with.html, including recursive CTEs for hierarchical queries.
SQLite: Date and time functions — sqlite.org/lang_datefunc.html documents strftime, julianday, and date arithmetic.
Mode SQL Tutorial: Advanced SQL — mode.com/sql-tutorial includes sections on window functions and optimization that build directly on this chapter’s material.

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
Choose the appropriate join type (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL OUTER) for a given analytical question.
Audit cardinality (grain) in each source table before joining, to prevent row inflation.
Use anti-join patterns (LEFT JOIN ... WHERE IS NULL) to find records present in one table but not another.
Produce a join-quality report documenting matched/unmatched records, row counts, totals, and introduced NULLs.
Replicate a SQL join in pandas as a cross-check on a complex multi-table analysis.

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.2 Why Joining Demands Discipline
This chapter completes the SQL module by joining the three cleaned Colorado public health tables into a single analysis-ready result set — one row per (county, year) — that can support rate calculations and cross-domain comparisons. The chapter is not only about the JOIN syntax. It is about the audit discipline that distinguishes a reliable join from a dangerous one: checking cardinality, identifying unmatched records, verifying row counts, and documenting every decision.
Joining tables is where the most consequential mistakes in data analysis happen — silently. A join that inflates row counts because of unexpected duplicates. A join that drops records because key formats do not match. A left join that introduces hundreds of NULL values you did not anticipate. The only defense is a systematic audit performed at every stage.
This chapter is the culmination of Part III: if you can run a clean, audited, documented three-table join by the end of it, you have the SQL core you need for most analytical work.

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.3 Setup
This chapter uses Python’s built-in sqlite3, pandas, and numpy.

import sqlite3
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', 20)
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', 55)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.2f}'.format)

conn = sqlite3.connect('colorado_public_health.db')
cursor = conn.cursor()







WarningIf you get an ImportError





sqlite3 is part of the Python standard library. For pandas or numpy: conda install pandas numpy or pip install pandas numpy, then restart the Jupyter kernel. See Chapter 1.









WarningBefore you join: verify grain and key formats



Two quick checks will save you hours. Grain: run SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(DISTINCT key_cols) FROM <table>. If the two numbers differ, the table is at a finer grain than the key; aggregate first. Key formats: run SELECT DISTINCT <key> FROM <table> on every source and eyeball the values. "Adams", "Adams County", and "Adams County, CO" will not match each other.


We are working with the three tables loaded and cleaned in Chapter 10: deaths (CDC mortality), crime (CBI offenses), and population (State Demography Office estimates). The county names have been standardized to a common format.

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.4 Part 1: Join Type Review
SQL supports four join types. Each determines what happens to rows that exist in one table but not the other.








Join type
Keeps
Use when




INNER JOIN
Only rows that match in both tables
You want complete cases only


LEFT JOIN
All rows from the left table; NULLs where the right has no match
You want to preserve the left table and add information where available


RIGHT JOIN
All rows from the right table; NULLs where the left has no match
Less common; same as LEFT JOIN with tables swapped


FULL OUTER JOIN
All rows from both tables; NULLs on both sides where no match
You want to see everything, including mismatches (not supported in SQLite)



For most analytical work, you will use LEFT JOIN (to preserve a base table and enrich it) or INNER JOIN (when you only want records present in all sources). The choice between them is a decision you must document — it determines which records appear in your analysis.

12.4.1 Toy example
To build intuition, consider two tiny tables:

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH left_t AS (
        SELECT 'Adams' AS county, 100 AS deaths UNION ALL
        SELECT 'Boulder', 200 UNION ALL
        SELECT 'Denver', 300
    ),
    right_t AS (
        SELECT 'Adams' AS county, 5000 AS pop UNION ALL
        SELECT 'Boulder', 3000 UNION ALL
        SELECT 'Costilla', 400
    )
    SELECT l.county AS l_county, l.deaths, r.county AS r_county, r.pop
    FROM left_t l
    LEFT JOIN right_t r ON l.county = r.county;
""", conn)

Denver appears with NULL population (no match in the right table). Costilla does not appear at all (it is only in the right table, and this is a LEFT JOIN). An INNER JOIN would drop Denver entirely.

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.5 Part 2: Cardinality Auditing
Before joining, you must understand the grain of each table — what combination of columns uniquely identifies a row — and verify that each table has exactly one row per key value. Joining tables with unexpected duplicates causes fan-out: row counts explode silently.

12.5.1 Grain of each table

# What combination of columns is the key for each table?
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT 'deaths' AS tbl,
           COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
           COUNT(DISTINCT county || '-' || year) AS distinct_keys
    FROM deaths
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 'crime',
           COUNT(*),
           COUNT(DISTINCT county || '-' || year)
    FROM crime
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 'population',
           COUNT(*),
           COUNT(DISTINCT county || '-' || year)
    FROM population;
""", conn)

If total_rows exceeds distinct_keys, the table has multiple rows per (county, year) — which means it is at a finer grain than county-year. You must aggregate it down to county-year before joining, or the join will create duplicates.


12.5.2 Demonstrating fan-out

# If deaths has multiple rows per county-year (e.g., one per age group),
# joining directly to population creates duplicates
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT d.county, d.year, COUNT(*) AS n_rows
    FROM deaths d
    JOIN population p ON d.county = p.county AND d.year = p.year
    GROUP BY d.county, d.year
    HAVING n_rows > 1
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.6 Part 3: Unmatched Record Audit
Before joining, check which key values exist in one table but not the other. This tells you how many rows will get NULL values (in a LEFT JOIN) or be dropped entirely (in an INNER JOIN).

12.6.1 Anti-join: counties in one table but not another
An anti-join finds records in one table that have no match in the other. SQL does not have a dedicated anti-join keyword, but you can achieve it with LEFT JOIN ... WHERE right.key IS NULL or with NOT IN:

# Counties in deaths that are NOT in population
pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT DISTINCT d.county
    FROM deaths d
    LEFT JOIN (SELECT DISTINCT county FROM population) p
        ON d.county = p.county
    WHERE p.county IS NULL
    ORDER BY d.county;
""", conn)

If this returns rows, those counties will have NULL population values after joining — which means you cannot compute rates for them. This is essential information to document.


12.6.2 Full cross-table mismatch audit

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH death_counties AS (SELECT DISTINCT county FROM deaths),
         crime_counties AS (SELECT DISTINCT county FROM crime),
         pop_counties AS (SELECT DISTINCT county FROM population)
    SELECT
        COALESCE(d.county, c.county, p.county) AS county,
        CASE WHEN d.county IS NOT NULL THEN 'Y' ELSE 'N' END AS in_deaths,
        CASE WHEN c.county IS NOT NULL THEN 'Y' ELSE 'N' END AS in_crime,
        CASE WHEN p.county IS NOT NULL THEN 'Y' ELSE 'N' END AS in_pop
    FROM death_counties d
    FULL OUTER JOIN crime_counties c ON d.county = c.county
    FULL OUTER JOIN pop_counties p ON COALESCE(d.county, c.county) = p.county
    ORDER BY county;
""", conn)







Note



SQLite does not support FULL OUTER JOIN. To achieve this in SQLite, use a UNION of left joins or create a reference table of all distinct counties and left-join each source table to it.

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.7 Part 4: Granularity Alignment
Each table may have a finer grain than county-year. Before joining, aggregate each table to the target grain — one row per (county, year).

12.7.1 Aggregate deaths to county-year

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS total_deaths, COUNT(*) AS n_detail_rows
    FROM deaths
    GROUP BY county, year
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)



12.7.2 Aggregate crime to county-year

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT county, year, COUNT(*) AS total_offenses
    FROM crime
    GROUP BY county, year
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)



12.7.3 Aggregate population to county-year

pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT county, year, SUM(totalpopulation) AS total_pop
    FROM population
    GROUP BY county, year
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)



12.7.4 Verify uniqueness after aggregation

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH agg_deaths AS (
        SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS total_deaths
        FROM deaths GROUP BY county, year
    )
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
           COUNT(DISTINCT county || '-' || year) AS distinct_keys
    FROM agg_deaths;
""", conn)

Total rows and distinct keys should be equal. If they are not, the aggregation did not produce a unique key and the join will still fan out.

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.8 Part 5: Building the Analysis-Ready Dataset
With each table aggregated to county-year and cardinality verified, we can now join them.

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH agg_deaths AS (
        SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS total_deaths
        FROM deaths
        GROUP BY county, year
    ),
    agg_crime AS (
        SELECT county, year, COUNT(*) AS total_offenses
        FROM crime
        GROUP BY county, year
    ),
    agg_pop AS (
        SELECT county, year, SUM(totalpopulation) AS total_pop
        FROM population
        GROUP BY county, year
    )
    SELECT
        COALESCE(d.county, c.county, p.county) AS county,
        COALESCE(d.year, c.year, p.year) AS year,
        d.total_deaths,
        c.total_offenses,
        p.total_pop,
        ROUND(1.0 * d.total_deaths / p.total_pop * 100000, 1) AS death_rate_per_100k,
        ROUND(1.0 * c.total_offenses / p.total_pop * 100000, 1) AS crime_rate_per_100k
    FROM agg_pop p
    LEFT JOIN agg_deaths d ON p.county = d.county AND p.year = d.year
    LEFT JOIN agg_crime c ON p.county = c.county AND p.year = c.year
    ORDER BY county, year
    LIMIT 20;
""", conn)

The population table serves as the base (left) table because it has the most complete county-year coverage. Deaths and crime data are joined onto it. Counties or years with no death or crime records will show NULL in those columns — a known and documented gap, not an error.

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.9 Part 6: Post-Join Audit
After building the joined dataset, run a systematic audit.

12.9.1 Row count audit

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH agg_pop AS (
        SELECT county, year, SUM(totalpopulation) AS total_pop
        FROM population GROUP BY county, year
    ),
    agg_deaths AS (
        SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS total_deaths
        FROM deaths GROUP BY county, year
    ),
    agg_crime AS (
        SELECT county, year, COUNT(*) AS total_offenses
        FROM crime GROUP BY county, year
    ),
    joined AS (
        SELECT p.county, p.year, d.total_deaths, c.total_offenses, p.total_pop
        FROM agg_pop p
        LEFT JOIN agg_deaths d ON p.county = d.county AND p.year = d.year
        LEFT JOIN agg_crime c ON p.county = c.county AND p.year = c.year
    )
    SELECT
        (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM agg_pop) AS pop_rows,
        (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM agg_deaths) AS death_rows,
        (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM agg_crime) AS crime_rows,
        (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM joined) AS joined_rows;
""", conn)

The joined row count should equal the population row count (the base table in a LEFT JOIN). If it is larger, you have a fan-out problem. If it is smaller, something went wrong with the join.


12.9.2 Totals audit

# Verify that aggregated totals in the joined result match the source tables
pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH joined AS (
        SELECT p.county, p.year,
               d.total_deaths, c.total_offenses, p.total_pop
        FROM (SELECT county, year, SUM(totalpopulation) AS total_pop FROM population GROUP BY county, year) p
        LEFT JOIN (SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS total_deaths FROM deaths GROUP BY county, year) d
            ON p.county = d.county AND p.year = d.year
        LEFT JOIN (SELECT county, year, COUNT(*) AS total_offenses FROM crime GROUP BY county, year) c
            ON p.county = c.county AND p.year = c.year
    )
    SELECT
        SUM(total_pop) AS joined_pop_total,
        SUM(total_deaths) AS joined_death_total,
        SUM(total_offenses) AS joined_crime_total;
""", conn)

Compare these totals to SELECT SUM(...) FROM each source table individually. Any discrepancy indicates a join problem.


12.9.3 NULL audit

pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH joined AS (
        SELECT p.county, p.year,
               d.total_deaths, c.total_offenses, p.total_pop
        FROM (SELECT county, year, SUM(totalpopulation) AS total_pop FROM population GROUP BY county, year) p
        LEFT JOIN (SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS total_deaths FROM deaths GROUP BY county, year) d
            ON p.county = d.county AND p.year = d.year
        LEFT JOIN (SELECT county, year, COUNT(*) AS total_offenses FROM crime GROUP BY county, year) c
            ON p.county = c.county AND p.year = c.year
    )
    SELECT
        COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
        SUM(CASE WHEN total_deaths IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_deaths,
        SUM(CASE WHEN total_offenses IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_crime,
        SUM(CASE WHEN total_pop IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_pop
    FROM joined;
""", conn)

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text: 12.10 Part 7: The Join-Quality Report

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.11 Public Interest Context: Ownership
A SQL query that produces a summary is a reproducible analytical artifact. Given the same database, anyone can re-run your query and get the same result. The CTEs, joins, and aggregations you wrote above are not a one-time piece of code — they are a specification of how the summary was built, which means the next analyst can audit your method as easily as they can audit your numbers. That is what ownership means in practice: the provenance of the result is legible in the query itself.
This matters in a public interest context because “the numbers say X” is often a contested claim. A journalist, a policy analyst, and a partisan think tank can all run similar-sounding queries against the same public database and reach different conclusions — because the joins, filters, and denominators they chose were different. The join-quality report from earlier in this chapter, plus the query itself, is how you make your choices auditable rather than asserted. Could you hand the query you just wrote to a skeptical reviewer and expect them to reproduce your result?






NoteConnection to Chapter 4



This section connects to the concept of ownership introduced in Chapter 4. A SQL query records the exact analytical procedure that produced a summary — a form of ownership that makes public-interest claims reproducible and auditable.


Every multi-table analysis should produce a brief report documenting the join. This is the SQL equivalent of the Known Issues Log from Chapter 2. At minimum, it should cover:

Join structure: which tables, which keys, which join type, and why
Granularity alignment: how each table was aggregated to the target grain
Cardinality checks: confirmation that each table has one row per key after aggregation
Unmatched record audit: which counties/years are missing from each source
Row count audit: joined rows vs. base table rows
Totals audit: aggregated values in joined result vs. source tables
NULL summary: how many NULLs were introduced by the join
Remaining uncertainties: what you could not verify or what might affect interpretation

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.12 Pandas Cross-Check
A valuable validation practice is to replicate the SQL join in pandas and compare results:

# Read the joined result from SQL
sql_result = pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT p.county, p.year,
           SUM(d.total_deaths) AS total_deaths,
           SUM(p.total_pop) AS total_pop
    FROM (SELECT county, year, SUM(totalpopulation) AS total_pop FROM population GROUP BY county, year) p
    LEFT JOIN (SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS total_deaths FROM deaths GROUP BY county, year) d
        ON p.county = d.county AND p.year = d.year
    GROUP BY p.county, p.year
    LIMIT 5;
""", conn)

sql_result


# Replicate in pandas
# deaths_df = pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM deaths", conn)
# pop_df = pd.read_sql_query("SELECT * FROM population", conn)
# ... aggregate and merge in pandas, then compare

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.13 Post-Join Audit Checklist
Use this checklist after every multi-table join:

Row count: joined rows == base table rows (for LEFT JOIN)
No fan-out: no (county, year) appears more than once
Totals match: SUM(col) in joined result matches source tables
NULLs explained: every NULL column has a documented reason
Cross-check: pandas merge produces the same result
Report written: join-quality report documents decisions and uncertainties

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.14 Join Type Decision Guide








Situation
Join type
Rationale




You need all records from your base table
LEFT JOIN
Preserves base; adds NULLs where no match


You need only complete cases
INNER JOIN
Drops any row missing from either table


You want to see what is missing from each side
Anti-join pattern
LEFT JOIN + WHERE IS NULL


You need all records from both sides
FULL OUTER JOIN
Not available in SQLite; simulate with UNION

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.15 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningJoin fan-out (too many rows in the result)



What it means: One or both tables have multiple rows per join key. A many-to-many join explodes: 5 rows × 3 rows for the same county-year = 15 output rows.
How to fix it: Run SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(DISTINCT key_cols) FROM <table> on each side. If they differ, aggregate to one-row-per-key before joining. Add the aggregation as a CTE so the fan-out fix is visible in the query.








WarningJoin returns zero rows despite matching keys



What it means: Silent mismatch — key formats, whitespace, or dtypes differ between the two tables. See Chapter 10 for the full diagnosis.
How to fix it: SELECT DISTINCT <key> FROM <table> on each side. Compare the values literally. Use TRIM, UPPER, or pandas pre-normalization to bring the formats into alignment before the join.








WarningFULL OUTER JOIN raises Error: RIGHT and FULL OUTER JOINs are not currently supported



What it means: Older versions of SQLite (< 3.39, released 2022) do not support FULL OUTER JOIN.
How to fix it: Upgrade SQLite (conda install sqlite), or simulate a FULL OUTER with LEFT JOIN + UNION ALL + the mirror RIGHT-as-LEFT join. The pattern is in Chapter 12 Part 3.








WarningAll your per-capita rates are NaN for some counties



What it means: Those counties are missing from the population table, so deaths / NULL returns NULL. The rate is literally undefined, not zero.
How to fix it: List the unmatched counties explicitly (anti-join), document the gap in your Known Issues Log, and decide whether to drop those counties from the rate analysis or hand-source the population data.

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.16 Key Takeaways

Before joining, audit grain (COUNT(*) vs COUNT(DISTINCT keys)) and key format (SELECT DISTINCT). These two checks prevent most join bugs.
Aggregate to the target grain inside a CTE before joining. Make the aggregation visible in the query.
Always produce a join-quality report: structure, granularity, cardinality, unmatched records, row counts, totals, NULLs, remaining uncertainty.
Cross-check complex joins with a pandas replication. If the two disagree, you learn something; if they agree, your confidence is earned.
Silent join failures are the most dangerous SQL bug class. Treat every multi-table join as a claim that needs evidence.

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.17 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on SQL joins and audit discipline






SQLite: Join operators — sqlite.org/syntax/join-operator.html is the formal grammar.
SQLite: SELECT with joins — sqlite.org/lang_select.html covers join semantics and clause ordering.
Mode: SQL Joins — mode.com/sql-tutorial/sql-joins/ walks through each join type with visual examples.
SQLZoo: Joins tutorial — sqlzoo.net/wiki/The_JOIN_operation provides interactive practice.
Data Validation: What It Is and Why It Matters — a Mode blog post at mode.com/blog/data-validation on audit discipline in analytical work.

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.18 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: INNER JOIN vs. LEFT JOIN impact





Write both an INNER JOIN and a LEFT JOIN version of the three-table join. How many rows does each produce? What counties or years are lost in the INNER JOIN?






NoteSolution





Compare SELECT COUNT(*) from each version. The difference represents county-year combinations present in population but absent from deaths or crime. Document which counties are affected and why.












NoteExercise 2: Top 10 counties by death rate





Using the analysis-ready dataset, find the top 10 counties by death rate per 100,000 in the most recent year available. Only include counties with population greater than 1,000 to avoid extreme rates from tiny counties.






NoteSolution






pd.read_sql_query("""
    WITH agg AS (
        SELECT p.county, p.year, SUM(d.deaths) AS total_deaths, SUM(p.totalpopulation) AS total_pop
        FROM (SELECT county, year, SUM(totalpopulation) AS totalpopulation FROM population GROUP BY county, year) p
        LEFT JOIN (SELECT county, year, SUM(deaths) AS deaths FROM deaths GROUP BY county, year) d
            ON p.county = d.county AND p.year = d.year
        GROUP BY p.county, p.year
    )
    SELECT county, year, total_deaths, total_pop,
           ROUND(1.0 * total_deaths / total_pop * 100000, 1) AS death_rate
    FROM agg
    WHERE year = (SELECT MAX(year) FROM agg)
      AND total_pop > 1000
      AND total_deaths IS NOT NULL
    ORDER BY death_rate DESC
    LIMIT 10;
""", conn)













NoteExercise 3: Write a join-quality report





Write a complete join-quality report for the three-table join you built in this chapter. Follow the seven-section structure described in Part 7. Be specific about numbers — how many rows, how many NULLs, which counties are affected.

title: 12  Reshaping Data with SQL
text:
12.19 SQL ↔︎ pandas Equivalents







SQL
pandas




LEFT JOIN ... ON
pd.merge(how='left', on=...)


INNER JOIN ... ON
pd.merge(how='inner', on=...)


LEFT JOIN ... WHERE IS NULL (anti-join)
merge(indicator=True) then filter _merge == 'left_only'


COALESCE(a, b)
a.fillna(b)


UNION ALL
pd.concat([df1, df2])


CTE → aggregate → join
Assign intermediate DataFrames → merge

title: 13  Spatial Data
text:
13.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 13  Spatial Data
text:
Load and inspect spatial data files (shapefiles and GeoJSON) using GeoPandas.
Explain what a coordinate reference system (CRS) is and use to_crs() to reproject geometries.
Produce a choropleth map using GeoDataFrame.plot() with an appropriate color map and classification scheme.
Perform spatial joins and point-in-polygon lookups with gpd.sjoin().
Recognize how spatial data work connects to questions of public accountability and equity.

title: 13  Spatial Data
text:
13.2 Why Spatial Data Matters
Maps are arguments, not illustrations. When you place data on a map, you make patterns visible that no table of numbers can reveal: redlining boundaries that still predict health outcomes a century later, the cluster of environmental hazards near certain neighborhoods, the odd shapes of gerrymandered districts. Spatial data is how analysts, journalists, and advocates make spatial inequality legible.
You’ve already seen how pandas handles tabular data — rows and columns of numbers and strings. Spatial data adds one more column: a geometry. Each row still represents an entity (a county, a census tract, a parcel), but now that entity carries a shape with it.
This chapter introduces GeoPandas, the Python library that extends pandas with spatial capabilities. GeoPandas is the go-to tool for spatial data analysis in Python — researchers, journalists, and data scientists use it daily to produce the maps you see in news articles and policy reports.
If spatial analysis feels unfamiliar, that’s expected. The concepts click once you start clicking through a few real datasets in the next few sections.

title: 13  Spatial Data
text:
13.3 Setup
You’ll use GeoPandas for spatial DataFrames, Shapely for the geometry objects it contains, pyproj for coordinate transformations, and matplotlib for plotting.

import geopandas as gpd
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt







TipIf you get an ImportError or ModuleNotFoundError





This means the library isn’t installed in your current Python environment. Run this in a new cell:
!pip install geopandas
Then restart your kernel (Kernel → Restart) and run the import cell again. If you’re using Anaconda, try conda install -c conda-forge geopandas instead — GeoPandas has several C-library dependencies that conda handles more reliably than pip. See Chapter 1 if the install still fails.

title: 13  Spatial Data
text:
13.4 Loading Spatial Data
Spatial data arrives in a few common formats. Shapefiles (.shp) are an older but ubiquitous format maintained by the US Census Bureau for boundary data; they actually consist of several files (.shp, .shx, .dbf, .prj) that must stay together in the same folder. GeoJSON (.geojson) is a newer, web-friendly format that packs everything into a single readable JSON file.
Load a shapefile or GeoJSON with gpd.read_file(). Here you load the Census TIGER/Line shapefile for Colorado counties.

counties = gpd.read_file('data/tl_2023_08_county.shp')

print('Shape:', counties.shape)
print('Columns:', counties.columns.tolist())
counties.head()

The result is a GeoDataFrame — a pandas DataFrame with one extra column named geometry that holds a Shapely polygon for each row. Everything you know about pandas still applies: .shape, .head(), .columns, .isna().sum(). The geometry column just adds spatial superpowers on top.

title: 13  Spatial Data
text:
13.5 Coordinate Reference Systems
Here’s a concept that trips up everyone at first. The Earth is roughly spherical, but your map is flat. A projection (or coordinate reference system, CRS) is the mathematical rule that translates points on the globe to points on your flat map. There are thousands of projections in use, and which one you pick matters.
The CRS is identified by an EPSG code — a numeric identifier that refers to a specific well-known projection. Two codes you’ll see constantly:

EPSG:4326 — latitude/longitude in degrees on the WGS84 globe. This is what GPS uses, and what most raw spatial data files ship with.
EPSG:3857 — the “Web Mercator” projection used by Google Maps, OpenStreetMap, and most web mapping tiles. Useful for overlaying your data on a web basemap.

Check the CRS of any GeoDataFrame like this:

print(counties.crs)

If this prints EPSG:4326 or similar, the geometries are in latitude/longitude. To reproject — change the CRS while preserving the underlying locations — use to_crs():

counties_web = counties.to_crs('EPSG:3857')
print(counties_web.crs)







WarningThe silent CRS bug



When you join or plot two GeoDataFrames together, they must be in the same CRS. If they are not, the geometries do not line up — points will appear hundreds of miles from where they should, or spatial joins will silently find zero matches. Always print .crs on both GeoDataFrames and call .to_crs() before combining them.

title: 13  Spatial Data
text:
13.6 Choropleths
A choropleth is a map where each region is shaded according to the value of a variable — for example, each Colorado county shaded by its poverty rate. It’s the single most common map type in data journalism.
Suppose you have a pandas DataFrame poverty_df with columns countyfips and poverty_rate. You merge it onto the county geometries using the same .merge() you learned in Chapter 7, then call .plot() on the resulting GeoDataFrame.

counties_poverty = counties.merge(poverty_df, on='countyfips', how='left')

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))
counties_poverty.plot(
    column='poverty_rate',
    cmap='YlOrRd',
    legend=True,
    edgecolor='white',
    linewidth=0.5,
    ax=ax
)
ax.set_title('Poverty Rate by Colorado County')
ax.set_axis_off()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The column argument names the variable to shade by. The cmap argument picks the color scheme — 'YlOrRd' (yellow → orange → red) is a conventional sequential palette for values that increase in one direction. Use 'RdBu' or 'coolwarm' for diverging palettes when values have a meaningful middle (above/below zero, above/below a reference rate).
The default classification is a linear scale across the data range. For skewed data — most socioeconomic variables are skewed — add scheme='Quantiles' or scheme='NaturalBreaks' to bin counties into equal-count or clustered groups. This requires the mapclassify package (pip install mapclassify).
Take a moment to appreciate what you just did: you loaded a shapefile, joined it to tabular data, and produced a publication-ready map in under ten lines of code.

title: 13  Spatial Data
text:
13.7 Spatial Joins
You’ve already seen how .merge() in Chapter 7 combines DataFrames on a shared key column. A spatial join is the same operation, but the “key” is geometric containment or proximity rather than a matching value. The function is gpd.sjoin().
