The classic use case is point-in-polygon: given a set of points (e.g., business locations) and a set of polygons (e.g., counties), tag each point with the county it falls inside.

# Points: one row per dispensary with lat/lon columns
dispensaries = pd.read_csv('data/dispensaries.csv')
dispensaries_gdf = gpd.GeoDataFrame(
    dispensaries,
    geometry=gpd.points_from_xy(dispensaries['lon'], dispensaries['lat']),
    crs='EPSG:4326'
)

# Make sure both GeoDataFrames share a CRS
dispensaries_gdf = dispensaries_gdf.to_crs(counties.crs)

# Spatial join: for each point, attach the county it falls inside
tagged = gpd.sjoin(
    dispensaries_gdf,
    counties[['countyfips', 'NAME', 'geometry']],
    how='left',
    predicate='within'
)

print(tagged.head())

The predicate argument controls the spatial relationship. Common values:

'within' — each left-hand point must fall inside a right-hand polygon.
'intersects' — any overlap counts.
'contains' — reverse of within.

After the join, tagged has every original dispensary column plus the matched county’s countyfips and NAME. You can now count dispensaries per county with a regular pandas groupby:

per_county = tagged.groupby('NAME').size().sort_values(ascending=False)
print(per_county.head())

If this feels abstract right now, that’s expected — it clicks once you have a real shapefile and a real point dataset in front of you.

title: 13  Spatial Data
text:
13.8 Public Interest Context: Mapping Power
Maps have always been tools of power. Nineteenth-century redlining maps drew color-coded boundaries that denied mortgages to Black and immigrant neighborhoods, and those same boundaries still predict air quality, tree cover, and life expectancy today. Gerrymandered legislative districts use geometry to dilute the votes of entire communities. Environmental justice analyses rely on spatial joins between industrial facility locations, pollution plumes, and demographic data to show who lives closest to the harm.
Spatial data is, in this sense, a public interest superpower: it makes visible what tabular data conceals. But access to that power is unevenly distributed. High-quality parcel-level shapefiles are often behind county GIS paywalls. Geocoding APIs — the services that turn an address into a latitude/longitude — enforce rate limits and terms of service that effectively exclude small-newsroom and student analyses. The infrastructure of spatial analysis is itself a site of enclosure. When you can produce a map, you can make an argument that spreadsheet data cannot.






NoteConnection to Chapter 4



This section connects to the concepts of enclosure and openness introduced in Chapter 4. Spatial analysis reveals inequalities that tabular data hides, but the shapefiles, basemaps, and geocoding services needed to produce that analysis are themselves unevenly accessible.

title: 13  Spatial Data
text:
13.9 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningCRSError: Invalid projection or coordinates look wildly wrong



What it means: You tried to plot or join two GeoDataFrames with different CRSs, or a shapefile loaded without a .prj file so GeoPandas doesn’t know the projection.
How to fix it: Run print(gdf.crs) on each GeoDataFrame. Use .to_crs('EPSG:4326') to bring them into alignment before any spatial operation. If .crs is None, you can assign one with gdf.set_crs('EPSG:4326', inplace=True) — but only if you know for certain that is the correct projection.








Warningfiona.errors.DriverError: unable to open file



What it means: A shapefile was moved or unzipped incompletely. A shapefile is really several files (.shp, .shx, .dbf, .prj) that must sit in the same folder.
How to fix it: Confirm all four files are present. If you downloaded a zip, re-extract it into a folder and point gpd.read_file() at the .shp file inside.








WarningYour spatial join returns zero rows



What it means: Either (1) the two GeoDataFrames have mismatched CRSs (see above), or (2) your predicate argument is wrong — for example, you used 'within' when you meant 'intersects'.
How to fix it: Print the .crs of both frames. Try predicate='intersects' as a diagnostic. Plot both layers on the same figure to confirm they actually overlap geographically.








WarningThe choropleth legend has too many decimal places



What it means: GeoPandas’ default legend formatting is not friendly for percentages, currencies, or large counts.
How to fix it: Pass legend_kwds={'fmt': '{:.1%}'} or legend_kwds={'fmt': '${:,.0f}'} to .plot() to control formatting. For categorical maps, pass categorical=True and a legend_kwds={'title': '...'}.

title: 13  Spatial Data
text:
13.10 Key Takeaways

A GeoDataFrame is a pandas DataFrame with a geometry column. Every pandas skill you have still applies.
Always check the CRS before plotting or joining. Mismatched CRSs are the single most common spatial bug.
Choropleths communicate quickly but can mislead — pick a palette and classification scheme that matches the distribution of your data.
gpd.sjoin() is the spatial cousin of pd.merge(). The predicate argument controls the geometric relationship.
Spatial work turns invisible inequality into visible argument. The technical skills in this chapter are also civic skills.

title: 13  Spatial Data
text:
13.11 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on spatial analysis with GeoPandas






GeoPandas documentation — geopandas.org/en/stable/docs.html is the canonical reference.
GeoPandas: Getting Started — geopandas.org/en/stable/getting_started/introduction.html produces a map from a shapefile in about an hour.
Folium — python-visualization.github.io/folium/ makes interactive web maps from GeoDataFrames.
contextily — contextily.readthedocs.io/ adds basemap tiles (streets, terrain) beneath your data.
Geographic Data Science with Python — geographicdata.science/book/ is a free open textbook with worked examples from housing, mobility, and environmental justice.

title: 13  Spatial Data
text:
13.12 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Map a variable of your choice





Download the Colorado county TIGER/Line shapefile and the county-year dataset you built in Chapter 12. Merge them on countyfips, then produce a choropleth of the death rate per 100,000 for 2020. Use a sequential colormap ('YlOrRd' or 'viridis') and include a title, legend, and formatted numeric labels. What counties stand out? Are there any geographic clusters?









NoteExercise 2: Point-in-polygon audit





Take a CSV with address-level or lat/lon-level records (e.g., cannabis dispensaries or crime incidents). Convert it to a GeoDataFrame using gpd.points_from_xy(). Perform a spatial join against the county shapefile. How many records fall outside any Colorado county boundary? What might explain the misses — bad addresses, records from neighboring states, or geocoding errors? Document your findings in a short Known Issues Log entry.

title: 14  Network and Graph Data
text:
14.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 14  Network and Graph Data
text:
Represent a system of relationships as a graph of nodes and edges using NetworkX.
Distinguish directed, undirected, weighted, and bipartite graphs and pick the representation that matches your data.
Convert between pandas DataFrames and NetworkX graphs using nx.from_pandas_edgelist.
Compute and interpret core network metrics — degree, centrality, clustering, shortest paths — and community detection.
Load and clean real network datasets from ICON, KONECT, and Network Repository.

title: 14  Network and Graph Data
text:
14.2 Why Network Data Matters
Relationships are data too. When the question you care about is about connections — who talks to whom, which papers cite which papers, which airports feed which flights — a table of rows and columns hides the very structure you want to study. A network (or graph) representation puts that structure front and center.
Networks describe systems as a set of nodes (the things) and edges (the relationships between them). Everything from power grids to friendship networks to disease transmission can be modeled this way. The questions you can ask of a network are different from the questions you can ask of a table: Who is central? What are the tight clusters? How far apart are two nodes?
This chapter introduces NetworkX, the standard Python library for network analysis. You’ll build small graphs by hand, compute their basic metrics, and then load real network datasets from public repositories. The mental model is different from pandas — a graph is not a table — but the habits are the same: inspect before acting, validate after every transform, document your decisions.
If this shift from tabular to graph thinking feels disorienting at first, that’s expected. It settles in once you have a network in front of you and start asking “who matters here?”

title: 14  Network and Graph Data
text:
14.3 Setup
You’ll use NetworkX for graph objects and algorithms, pandas for conversion to and from DataFrames, and matplotlib for drawing.

import networkx as nx
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt







TipIf you get an ImportError or ModuleNotFoundError





This means the library isn’t installed in your current Python environment. Run this in a new cell:
!pip install networkx
Then restart your kernel (Kernel → Restart) and run the import cell again. If you’re using Anaconda, try conda install -c conda-forge networkx instead.

title: 14  Network and Graph Data
text:
14.4 Building a Graph by Hand
The simplest way to understand a graph is to build one. Create an empty undirected graph, then add nodes and edges:

G = nx.Graph()
G.add_edges_from([
    ('Alice', 'Bob'),
    ('Bob', 'Carol'),
    ('Alice', 'Carol'),
    ('Carol', 'Dave'),
])

print('Nodes:', list(G.nodes))
print('Edges:', list(G.edges))
print('Number of nodes:', G.number_of_nodes())
print('Number of edges:', G.number_of_edges())

Notice you never had to declare the nodes separately — NetworkX infers them from the edges you add. Four names, four edges, and a small graph takes shape. Draw it with nx.draw():

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 4))
nx.draw(G, with_labels=True, node_color='lightblue', node_size=1200, ax=ax)
plt.show()

title: 14  Network and Graph Data
text:
14.5 Network Types
Four distinctions cover most real-world networks:
Undirected vs. directed. In an undirected graph, “Alice knows Bob” is the same edge as “Bob knows Alice.” In a directed graph (use nx.DiGraph), the two are different — think of Twitter follows, email chains, or citations. The class you choose controls how NetworkX counts edges, computes degree, and runs algorithms.
Unweighted vs. weighted. An edge can carry a numeric attribute — a weight — representing how strong or frequent the relationship is. Friendship ties might be weighted by number of messages sent; flight networks by number of seats; citation networks by number of co-citations.

DG = nx.DiGraph()
DG.add_edge('Alice', 'Bob', weight=3.0)
DG.add_edge('Bob', 'Alice', weight=1.0)   # reverse direction, different weight

Bipartite. Some networks naturally have two kinds of nodes — users and songs, authors and papers, companies and board members — with edges only between the two types. A bipartite graph (nx.Graph with a bipartite attribute on each node) captures that structure. You can later project it onto a single-type graph (e.g., connect authors who share a paper).
Choosing the right type early prevents headaches later. A directed graph analyzed as undirected produces wrong answers; a weighted graph analyzed as unweighted discards the nuance you cared about.

title: 14  Network and Graph Data
section: 14.6 Data Structure: Nodes, Edges, and Attributes
text:
14.6 Data Structure: Nodes, Edges, and Attributes
Every node and edge can carry arbitrary attribute data — like a pandas row, but keyed by the node or edge rather than by an index.

G = nx.Graph()
G.add_node('Alice', age=29, city='Boulder')
G.add_edge('Alice', 'Bob', weight=4.0, since=2020)

print(G.nodes['Alice'])        # {'age': 29, 'city': 'Boulder'}
print(G.edges['Alice', 'Bob'])  # {'weight': 4.0, 'since': 2020}

When your data lives in a pandas DataFrame, use nx.from_pandas_edgelist to convert it to a graph:

edges_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'source': ['A', 'A', 'B', 'C'],
    'target': ['B', 'C', 'D', 'D'],
    'weight': [1.0, 2.5, 0.5, 3.0],
})

G = nx.from_pandas_edgelist(
    edges_df,
    source='source',
    target='target',
    edge_attr='weight',
    create_using=nx.DiGraph
)

Going the other direction is also easy: nx.to_pandas_edgelist(G) returns the edge list as a DataFrame. This round-trip between pandas and NetworkX means you can clean, filter, and reshape with familiar tools, then switch to graph algorithms when the question calls for them.

title: 14  Network and Graph Data
text:
14.7 Network Metrics
Network metrics fall into two categories: metrics of nodes (how important is this node?) and metrics of the whole graph (how tightly connected is the system?).

14.7.1 Degree: the simplest centrality
Degree is the number of edges a node has. In a directed graph, you distinguish in-degree (incoming) and out-degree (outgoing).

degrees = dict(G.degree())
print(sorted(degrees.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1])[:5])

The degree distribution — the histogram of degrees — often reveals whether a network is “star-shaped” (a few hubs, many leaves) or roughly uniform. Many real networks are heavily skewed, with a long tail of low-degree nodes and a short head of very high-degree hubs.


14.7.2 Centrality: who is important, and in what sense?
Different notions of “centrality” answer different questions:

Degree centrality — raw degree normalized by the maximum possible. Who has the most direct connections?
Betweenness centrality — how often a node lies on shortest paths between others. Who are the brokers between clusters?
Closeness centrality — the inverse of the mean shortest-path distance to every other node. Who can reach everyone quickly?
Eigenvector centrality (and its variants, PageRank) — a node is important if its neighbors are important. Who is connected to the well-connected?


bet = nx.betweenness_centrality(G)
close = nx.closeness_centrality(G)
pagerank = nx.pagerank(G)

central_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'betweenness': bet,
    'closeness': close,
    'pagerank': pagerank,
}).sort_values('betweenness', ascending=False)
print(central_df.head())

There’s no single “right” centrality. Journalists investigating a corruption network might care most about betweenness (brokers between otherwise-disconnected groups). Epidemiologists modeling a disease might care more about eigenvector centrality.


14.7.3 Clustering and shortest paths
The clustering coefficient measures how much a node’s neighbors are also connected to each other — how “cliquey” the local neighborhood is. nx.clustering(G) returns per-node values; nx.average_clustering(G) returns the graph-level average.
Shortest paths are the key to thinking about distance in networks. nx.shortest_path_length(G, source, target) gives you the hop distance; nx.average_shortest_path_length(G) gives the graph-wide average (small-world networks have surprisingly short averages).

title: 14  Network and Graph Data
text:
14.8 Network Dynamics
Some of the most interesting questions are about how networks change. Who joins the network first? Which communities form?
Community detection finds clusters of nodes that are more densely connected to each other than to the rest of the graph. NetworkX includes several algorithms under nx.community:

from networkx.algorithms import community

communities = community.greedy_modularity_communities(G)
for i, comm in enumerate(communities):
    print(f'Community {i}: {len(comm)} nodes')

Community detection is powerful and easy to misuse. Different algorithms produce different partitions on the same graph, and every partition is a claim that you’d have to defend. Treat community output as a hypothesis to investigate, not an answer.

title: 14  Network and Graph Data
text:
14.9 Working with Real Network Data
Three repositories publish curated network datasets for research and teaching. Each has thousands of graphs, from social networks and protein interactions to citation graphs and road networks.

ICON — the Colorado Index of Complex Networks, organized by domain with metadata and links.
KONECT — the Koblenz Network Collection, with consistent formatting and precomputed metrics.
Network Repository — a large visual index, searchable by graph size and category.

Most datasets from these repositories arrive as an edge list — a text file where each line lists a pair of nodes (and optionally a weight or timestamp). Parsing one looks like:

edges_df = pd.read_csv(
    'data/soc-karate.edges',
    sep=' ',
    names=['source', 'target'],
    comment='%'     # many KONECT files use '%' as a comment line marker
)

G = nx.from_pandas_edgelist(edges_df, 'source', 'target')
print('Nodes:', G.number_of_nodes(), 'Edges:', G.number_of_edges())







WarningThe header-row gotcha



Edge list files often start with a comment line (beginning with % or #) that describes the data. Pass comment='%' or comment='#' to pd.read_csv so those lines are skipped rather than imported as data. Always check the first few rows of the file with a text editor before loading.


Take a moment to appreciate what you just did: you turned a bare text file of numbers into a graph object that supports hundreds of built-in algorithms.

title: 14  Network and Graph Data
text:
14.10 Public Interest Context: Invisible Structures
Networks are the paradigm case of exemption — data that is known to exist but withheld from public view. Every major platform (Meta, X, TikTok, Amazon, LinkedIn) runs on network data: who follows whom, who buys what, who sees which ads. Those companies know the structure intimately. Researchers, journalists, and regulators almost never do.
What’s visible to the public is usually a partial trace — a sample, a snapshot, a thin proxy. Worker observatories (like Gig Worker’s Rising) reconstruct platform networks from app screenshots and leaked data. Algorithmic audits (like ProPublica’s Machine Bias series) infer network structure from the outputs of recommendation systems. Academic collaborations like Indiana’s Observatory on Social Media (OSoMe) stitch together public API traces before those APIs vanish.
When you analyze a network, it’s worth asking: whose network is this, and who could see the full picture? The gap between your sample and the complete structure is itself a data story.






NoteConnection to Chapter 4



This section connects to the concepts of exemption and oversight introduced in Chapter 4. Platform networks are known to their operators but invisible to the public; reconstructing them from partial traces is how accountability journalism and algorithmic auditing happen.

title: 14  Network and Graph Data
text:
14.11 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningNetworkXError: Node not in the graph



What it means: You tried to compute a path or neighbor of a node that doesn’t exist in the graph — often because of a typo or because the node name changed type (e.g., '5' vs 5).
How to fix it: Print list(G.nodes)[:5] to confirm the actual node type. Cast consistently: edges_df['source'] = edges_df['source'].astype(str) before building the graph.








WarningYour centrality values all come back as 0 or NaN



What it means: The graph is disconnected, or you passed the wrong graph type. Eigenvector centrality, in particular, requires a connected graph.
How to fix it: Check connectivity with nx.is_connected(G) (for undirected) or nx.is_strongly_connected(G) (for directed). Take the largest connected component with G_lcc = G.subgraph(max(nx.connected_components(G), key=len)).copy() and run the metric on that.








Warningnx.draw() produces a tangled blob



What it means: The default layout algorithm does not scale well beyond ~50 nodes. You’re seeing a “hairball.”
How to fix it: For larger graphs, either sample a subgraph or use a specialized layout: pos = nx.spring_layout(G, k=0.5, iterations=50). Better yet, export to an interactive tool like pyvis or Gephi.








WarningBuilding the graph takes forever on a large edge list



What it means: add_edges_from on a Python loop is slow for millions of edges. nx.from_pandas_edgelist is much faster, and graph-tool is faster still.
How to fix it: Always prefer nx.from_pandas_edgelist over manual loops. For very large graphs, switch to graph-tool or use a sampled subgraph for exploration.

title: 14  Network and Graph Data
text:
14.12 Key Takeaways

A graph represents relationships — nodes for entities, edges for connections. The questions you can ask differ from tabular questions.
Pick the right type (directed, weighted, bipartite) early. The wrong type gives wrong answers.
nx.from_pandas_edgelist is the bridge between pandas and NetworkX. Clean and filter in pandas, analyze in NetworkX.
Centrality has multiple definitions. Pick the one that matches your question, and report it alongside the graph you computed it on.
Public interest network analysis almost always works from partial traces. Document the gap between your sample and the full structure.

title: 14  Network and Graph Data
text:
14.13 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on network analysis






NetworkX documentation — networkx.org/documentation/stable/ is the canonical reference.
NetworkX Tutorial — networkx.org/documentation/stable/tutorial.html walks through graph creation, attributes, and basic algorithms.
pyvis — pyvis.readthedocs.io/ turns NetworkX graphs into interactive HTML visualizations.
graph-tool — graph-tool.skewed.de/ is a faster alternative for large graphs and performance-critical work.
“Networks, Crowds, and Markets” by Easley & Kleinberg — a free textbook at cs.cornell.edu/home/kleinber/networks-book/ that develops the conceptual vocabulary behind graph algorithms.

title: 14  Network and Graph Data
text:
14.14 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Build and describe a small network





Download Zachary’s karate club dataset from KONECT or NetworkX’s built-in nx.karate_club_graph(). Load it, print the number of nodes and edges, compute the degree distribution, and identify the top 5 nodes by betweenness centrality. Who are the brokers, and what does Zachary’s original paper say about them?









NoteExercise 2: Pandas → NetworkX round-trip





Take any edge-list CSV from Network Repository with fewer than 5,000 nodes. Load it into pandas, filter to edges with weight above the median, convert to a NetworkX graph, compute PageRank, and convert the PageRank values back into a pandas DataFrame sorted descending. Which nodes rise and fall when the weight threshold changes? Document your cleaning decisions in a short Known Issues Log entry.

title: 15  Unstructured Text Data
text:
15.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 15  Unstructured Text Data
text:
Preprocess raw text using NLTK: tokenize, remove stopwords, and apply stemming or lemmatization.
Convert text into numerical features using bag-of-words and TF-IDF representations.
Compute word frequency distributions, lexical diversity, and simple sentiment scores.
Build a reusable text-preprocessing pipeline that takes raw documents to cleaned tokens.
Recognize how text archives relate to the public interest — and what it means when those archives disappear.

title: 15  Unstructured Text Data
text:
15.2 Why Text Data Matters
Most of the world’s data is text. Court filings, municipal meeting minutes, legislative transcripts, scientific papers, social media posts, news articles, novel manuscripts, survey free-response answers, and customer reviews are all text data. If you only know how to work with numbers and categories, you are walking past the majority of the information that exists.
Text is also messy in ways that tabular data usually isn’t. The same meaning can be expressed in hundreds of ways. Punctuation, capitalization, and spelling drift constantly. The structure — sentence, paragraph, section — is implicit. Turning raw text into something a model or statistic can work with is its own skill, and that’s what this chapter teaches.
NLTK (the Natural Language Toolkit) is a classic, beginner-friendly Python library for text analysis. It’s not the fastest tool — spaCy and Hugging Face are what production teams use today — but NLTK is unbeatable for learning the concepts because it exposes every step in the pipeline.
If text preprocessing feels fiddly right now, that’s expected. The satisfying click comes once you run a pipeline on a real corpus and see meaningful patterns pop out the other end.

title: 15  Unstructured Text Data
text:
15.3 Setup
You’ll use NLTK for tokenization and classic NLP operations, pandas for the DataFrames that hold your corpus, and scikit-learn’s TfidfVectorizer for TF-IDF.

import nltk
import pandas as pd

# First-time setup: download the small corpora NLTK needs
nltk.download('punkt')
nltk.download('stopwords')
nltk.download('wordnet')
nltk.download('vader_lexicon')







TipIf you get an ImportError or ModuleNotFoundError





This means the library isn’t installed in your current Python environment. Run this in a new cell:
!pip install nltk scikit-learn
Then restart your kernel (Kernel → Restart) and run the import cell again. If you’re using Anaconda, try conda install -c conda-forge nltk scikit-learn instead.









Warningnltk.download() downloads to your home directory



The nltk.download() calls above fetch small corpora and pre-trained resources from the internet. If you’re on a restricted network, the call may hang; see the NLTK docs for offline installation. You only need to run these once per environment — NLTK caches the data locally.

title: 15  Unstructured Text Data
text:
15.4 Loading and Inspecting Text
Text data arrives in many containers. A single .txt file (a novel, a speech); a folder of text files (a corpus); a DataFrame with a text column (tweets, reviews); a compressed archive (court filings, Wikipedia dumps). The first step is always to get the text into a list of strings, one per document.

with open('data/inaugural/2021-biden.txt', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    text = f.read()

print(f'Document length: {len(text):,} characters')
print(text[:500])

Notice the encoding='utf-8' argument. Text files can arrive in many encodings (UTF-8, Latin-1, Windows-1252), and picking the wrong one produces garbled characters. UTF-8 is the modern default; fall back to 'latin-1' or 'cp1252' for older files.

title: 15  Unstructured Text Data
text:
15.5 Text Pre-processing
Pre-processing turns raw text into a sequence of normalized tokens that downstream tools can use. The standard pipeline has five steps: tokenization, lowercasing, punctuation removal, stopword removal, and stemming or lemmatization.

15.5.1 Tokenization
Tokenization splits a string into individual words (tokens). NLTK’s word_tokenize handles punctuation and contractions better than a plain text.split().

from nltk.tokenize import word_tokenize, sent_tokenize

tokens = word_tokenize(text)
print('First 20 tokens:', tokens[:20])
print('Total tokens:', len(tokens))

You can also split into sentences with sent_tokenize — useful when sentence boundaries matter (summarization, sentiment analysis).


15.5.2 Case normalization and punctuation
Most analyses treat "The" and "the" as the same word. Lowercase everything, then filter out pure punctuation:

tokens = [t.lower() for t in tokens if t.isalpha()]
print('After normalization:', tokens[:20])

The isalpha() check drops tokens that are not entirely letters — punctuation, numbers, and mixed strings all go. Adjust if numbers matter for your question.


15.5.3 Stopword removal
Stopwords are common words like “the,” “and,” “of” that carry little topical meaning. NLTK ships with stopword lists for dozens of languages:

from nltk.corpus import stopwords

stop = set(stopwords.words('english'))
tokens = [t for t in tokens if t not in stop]
print('After stopwords:', tokens[:20])

Stopword removal is a judgment call, not a rule. For authorship attribution or stylistic analysis, stopwords are the most useful tokens — do not remove them. For topic modeling and TF-IDF, removing them usually improves results.


15.5.4 Stemming vs. lemmatization
Both operations reduce inflected forms to a base form, but differently. Stemming chops off endings with simple rules — fast but sometimes crude ("running" → "run", but "better" → "better"). Lemmatization uses a dictionary to find the canonical form ("better" → "good"). Lemmatization is slower but more linguistically principled.

from nltk.stem import PorterStemmer, WordNetLemmatizer

stemmer = PorterStemmer()
lemmatizer = WordNetLemmatizer()

print('Stemmed:', [stemmer.stem(t) for t in ['running', 'runs', 'ran', 'better']])
print('Lemmatized (verbs):', [lemmatizer.lemmatize(t, pos='v') for t in ['running', 'runs', 'ran']])

Pick one and document the choice. Mixing stemming and lemmatization across a project gives inconsistent results.

title: 15  Unstructured Text Data
text:
15.6 Vectorizing Text
Most downstream tools — classifiers, clustering algorithms, similarity searches — expect numeric input. You need to turn each document into a vector of numbers. Two classic approaches:

15.6.1 Bag-of-words
Bag-of-words represents each document as a vector of word counts. Word order is discarded, so a document is just a multiset of tokens. Crude but surprisingly effective.


15.6.2 TF-IDF
TF-IDF (term frequency × inverse document frequency) is a weighted refinement. Each term’s weight in a document is its frequency in that document, scaled down by how common it is across the whole corpus. Words that appear everywhere (even after stopword removal) get low weights; words that appear in a few documents but repeatedly get high weights.
The scikit-learn implementation hides the messy details:

from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer

corpus = [
    'data science is the study of data',
    'public interest data journalism uncovers hidden stories',
    'machine learning is a branch of artificial intelligence',
]

vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer(stop_words='english')
tfidf = vectorizer.fit_transform(corpus)

tfidf_df = pd.DataFrame(
    tfidf.toarray(),
    columns=vectorizer.get_feature_names_out()
)
print(tfidf_df.round(2))

Each row is a document; each column is a unique term; each cell is the TF-IDF weight. The matrix is usually sparse (most cells are zero), and scikit-learn stores it efficiently as a sparse matrix.
Take a moment to appreciate what you just did: you turned three sentences into a numeric matrix you can now run clustering, classification, or similarity metrics on.

title: 15  Unstructured Text Data
text:
15.7 Text Metrics
Before any advanced modeling, compute a few descriptive statistics about your corpus.

15.7.1 Word frequency

from collections import Counter

freq = Counter(tokens)
print('Most common tokens:')
for word, count in freq.most_common(20):
    print(f'  {word}: {count}')

This is the text analog of .value_counts(). Eyeball the top 20: are the most common tokens meaningful, or do they reveal a preprocessing step you missed?


15.7.2 Lexical diversity
Lexical diversity is the ratio of unique tokens to total tokens — how varied the vocabulary is.

diversity = len(set(tokens)) / len(tokens)
print(f'Lexical diversity: {diversity:.3f}')

High diversity (close to 1) suggests a document with a wide vocabulary; low diversity (close to 0) suggests heavy repetition.


15.7.3 Sentiment
NLTK’s VADER sentiment analyzer is a rule-based tool tuned for short, informal English (reviews, tweets). It returns compound scores from -1 (most negative) to +1 (most positive).

from nltk.sentiment import SentimentIntensityAnalyzer

sia = SentimentIntensityAnalyzer()
score = sia.polarity_scores('This chapter makes NLP feel approachable.')
print(score)

VADER is a good starting point, not a final answer. Modern transformer-based sentiment models outperform it on most benchmarks. Use VADER when you need transparency and speed; reach for Hugging Face when you need accuracy.

title: 15  Unstructured Text Data
text:
15.8 Working with Real Text Data
Two friendly sources for practicing:

Project Gutenberg — tens of thousands of public-domain books in plain text. Great for stylistic analysis, topic modeling, and comparative literature.
NLTK built-in corpora — nltk.corpus.inaugural (US inaugural addresses, 1789–present), nltk.corpus.gutenberg (a selection of classics), and nltk.corpus.reuters (news articles by category).

Here’s a small pipeline that loads every inaugural address, tokenizes it, and computes word counts per president:

from nltk.corpus import inaugural

rows = []
for fileid in inaugural.fileids():
    year = int(fileid[:4])
    president = fileid[5:-4]
    words = [w.lower() for w in inaugural.words(fileid) if w.isalpha()]
    rows.append({
        'year': year,
        'president': president,
        'n_tokens': len(words),
        'n_unique': len(set(words)),
    })

inaugurals = pd.DataFrame(rows)
print(inaugurals.head())
print(inaugurals[['n_tokens', 'n_unique']].describe())

You’re now in familiar pandas territory — group by year ranges, correlate with external data, build a plot. Text analysis is not a separate universe; it’s a pipeline that feeds tokens into the tools you already know.

title: 15  Unstructured Text Data
text:
15.9 Public Interest Context: Disappearing Records
Text archives are eroding. News sites shutter and take their archives down. Social media platforms lock or delete posts. Court records and municipal meeting minutes live on fragile PDF scrolls behind county-specific search interfaces. Link rot — the slow disappearance of URLs — is a known, measured phenomenon in academic citation analysis.
At the same time, the text records of public life that remain are often the only evidence of how institutions behaved. Municipal meeting minutes document when a zoning change was proposed. Legislative transcripts record who said what on the floor. Court filings record every motion in a case. When these text records are intact and searchable, accountability journalism and civic research are possible. When they rot or go behind paywalls, accountability becomes harder.
Text analysis skills connect directly to this landscape: the ability to scrape, preserve, and analyze text archives is a public interest skill. Projects like the Internet Archive, DocumentCloud, and OpenStates exist precisely because the default is erosion.






NoteConnection to Chapter 4



This section connects to the concepts of erosion and ownership introduced in Chapter 4. Text archives erode unless someone invests in preserving them; text-analysis skills help turn those fragile records into evidence that can inform public debate.

title: 15  Unstructured Text Data
text:
15.10 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningLookupError: Resource punkt not found



What it means: You tried to tokenize before downloading NLTK’s tokenizer data.
How to fix it: Run nltk.download('punkt') once, then rerun your tokenizer call. The same pattern applies for stopwords, wordnet, and vader_lexicon.








WarningUnicodeDecodeError when reading a text file



What it means: The file is not UTF-8 encoded. Common for older files from Windows systems.
How to fix it: Try encoding='latin-1' or encoding='cp1252' when calling open(). If in doubt, use chardet (pip install chardet) to detect the encoding programmatically.








WarningTF-IDF weights look suspicious or all zero



What it means: Your corpus is too small for meaningful IDF weights (TF-IDF is designed for dozens to millions of documents), or your preprocessing stripped too many terms.
How to fix it: Check the vocabulary size: print(len(vectorizer.vocabulary_)). If it’s near zero, loosen min_df and max_df. For corpora smaller than ~20 documents, raw counts may be more informative than TF-IDF.








WarningStemming turns meaningful words into nonsense ("university" → "univers")



What it means: You’re using a stemmer when lemmatization would preserve meaning better.
How to fix it: Switch to WordNetLemmatizer. Pass the correct part-of-speech tag (pos='v' for verbs, pos='n' for nouns) to get the best results.

title: 15  Unstructured Text Data
text:
15.11 Key Takeaways

Text preprocessing is a pipeline: tokenize, normalize, filter, stem or lemmatize. Document every step in your Known Issues Log.
Bag-of-words and TF-IDF turn text into numbers. Once vectorized, every tool you know from numeric data is available.
Descriptive text metrics — word frequency, lexical diversity, sentiment — are the equivalent of .describe() for text.
NLTK is a teaching tool and a reliable workhorse; reach for spaCy and Hugging Face when you need speed or modern accuracy.
Public interest text work exists because text archives erode. Preserving and analyzing them is part of accountability infrastructure.

title: 15  Unstructured Text Data
text:
15.12 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on text analysis






NLTK documentation — nltk.org/ is the canonical reference.
NLTK Book — nltk.org/book/ is a free, book-length tutorial that develops from first principles.
Real Python: NLTK tutorial — realpython.com/nltk-nlp-python/ is an accessible walkthrough.
spaCy — spacy.io/ is the production-oriented successor, with fast pretrained models for tagging and entity recognition.
Hugging Face Transformers — huggingface.co/docs/transformers/ is where modern embedding-based NLP lives.

title: 15  Unstructured Text Data
text:
15.13 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Inaugural address corpus





Load all US inaugural addresses with nltk.corpus.inaugural. Compute per-address token counts, unique-word counts, and lexical diversity. Build a pandas DataFrame with one row per address. Which president used the largest vocabulary? Is there a trend over time? Produce a simple line plot of lexical diversity by year.









NoteExercise 2: TF-IDF on a small corpus of your choice





Pick five to ten short documents you care about — news articles on a topic, social media posts from a hashtag, song lyrics by one artist. Build a TF-IDF matrix and look at the top 5 highest-weighted terms in each document. Do the top terms feel representative? What does this tell you about the corpus? Document your preprocessing choices in a short Known Issues Log entry.

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
Parse dates and reshape a DataFrame into a proper time series indexed by a DatetimeIndex.
Identify trend, seasonality, stationarity, and autocorrelation in a series, and visualize each with plot_acf and seasonal_decompose.
Fit an ARIMA model with statsmodels and produce a forecast with confidence intervals.
Use Prophet as a higher-level forecasting tool when automation is more important than fine control.
Load real economic data from FRED, World Bank Open Data, and IMF Data.

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.2 Why Time Series Data Matters
Most policy questions are time-series questions. Is unemployment rising or falling? When did housing prices take off? Did the new policy reduce overdose deaths, and if so, when? Time series data — a value measured repeatedly over time — is the backbone of how agencies, newsrooms, and researchers track change.
This chapter covers the skills you need to work with time series: parsing dates, decomposing a series into trend and seasonality, diagnosing autocorrelation, and producing simple forecasts. You’ll start with statsmodels, which is already installed in most Python environments and gives you direct access to the underlying components of a forecast. Then you’ll meet Prophet, which automates many of the same choices and is worth reaching for when you want a working forecast fast.
If this chapter feels dense, that’s expected. Time series has its own vocabulary — stationarity, differencing, ACF, PACF — that takes a few passes to internalize. Keep the definitions close and rerun the examples with different series until the intuition settles.

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.3 Setup
You’ll use pandas for date handling, statsmodels for decomposition and ARIMA, and matplotlib for plotting.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import statsmodels.api as sm
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf, plot_pacf
from statsmodels.tsa.seasonal import seasonal_decompose
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA







TipIf you get an ImportError or ModuleNotFoundError





This means the library isn’t installed in your current Python environment. Run this in a new cell:
!pip install statsmodels
Then restart your kernel (Kernel → Restart) and run the import cell again. If you’re using Anaconda, try conda install -c conda-forge statsmodels instead.

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.4 Time Series Concepts
Four concepts recur throughout time-series analysis. Learn the names now; the implementations follow.
Trend is the long-run direction of the series — slowly rising, slowly falling, or flat.
Seasonality is a recurring pattern that repeats at a fixed period — monthly retail sales peaking in December, hourly traffic peaking at rush hour, annual birth rates peaking in summer.
Stationarity is the property that the series’ mean and variance do not change over time. Most real series are not stationary as-is; many models require stationarity and use differencing (subtracting the previous value) to achieve it.
Autocorrelation is the correlation between a series and a lagged version of itself. A strong correlation at lag 12 in monthly data signals an annual seasonal pattern.

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.5 Building a Proper Time Series
A pandas DataFrame becomes a time series when its index is a DatetimeIndex. The first step is always: parse the date column, set it as the index, and sort.

unrate = pd.read_csv('data/UNRATE.csv')
unrate['DATE'] = pd.to_datetime(unrate['DATE'])
unrate = unrate.set_index('DATE').sort_index()

print(unrate.head())
print('Index type:', type(unrate.index))
print('Date range:', unrate.index.min(), '→', unrate.index.max())







WarningCheck your frequency



A time series has a frequency: monthly ('MS' = month start, 'ME' = month end), quarterly ('QS'), daily ('D'), etc. Some statsmodels functions need to know the frequency explicitly. After setting the index, run unrate.asfreq('MS') to assert a monthly-start frequency and fill any gaps with NaN. If the result has unexpected NaN rows, there are missing months you need to address.

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.6 Visualizing a Time Series
Before any modeling, plot the series. Eyeballing the trend, seasonality, and outliers prevents a lot of downstream confusion.

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))
ax.plot(unrate.index, unrate['UNRATE'])
ax.set_title('US Unemployment Rate (monthly)')
ax.set_ylabel('Unemployment rate (%)')
ax.axhline(unrate['UNRATE'].mean(), color='gray', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, label='Mean')
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The line plot is usually enough to see the big picture. Recessions show up as sharp spikes; multi-year cycles are visible at a glance. Compare what you see to the known economic history of the series — the 2008 financial crisis and the 2020 COVID shock should be obvious.

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.7 Decomposition
Seasonal decomposition splits the observed series into three components: trend, seasonal, and residual. The additive model assumes observed = trend + seasonal + residual; the multiplicative model assumes the three components multiply.

decomp = seasonal_decompose(unrate['UNRATE'], model='additive', period=12)

fig = decomp.plot()
fig.set_size_inches(10, 8)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

You get four panels: the original series, the extracted trend, the seasonal component, and the residuals. If the seasonal pattern looks messy or meaningless, try period=4 for quarterly data or period=7 for weekly.

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.8 Autocorrelation and Differencing
The autocorrelation function (ACF) plots the correlation between the series and its lagged versions. A slowly decaying ACF suggests a strong trend (non-stationary); sharp peaks at regular lags suggest seasonality.

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(10, 6))
plot_acf(unrate['UNRATE'].dropna(), ax=axes[0], lags=48, title='ACF: Unemployment rate')
plot_pacf(unrate['UNRATE'].dropna(), ax=axes[1], lags=48, title='PACF: Unemployment rate')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

To make a non-stationary series stationary, take the first difference (each observation minus its predecessor):

diff = unrate['UNRATE'].diff().dropna()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 3))
ax.plot(diff.index, diff)
ax.set_title('First-differenced unemployment rate')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The differenced series should have no obvious trend and a roughly constant variance. If one difference isn’t enough, you can difference again.

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.9 Forecasting with ARIMA
ARIMA — AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average — is the classic statistical forecasting model. It has three parameters, written (p, d, q):

p — number of autoregressive lags
d — number of differences applied
q — number of moving-average lags

Picking good (p, d, q) is part art, part ritual. Start with (1, 1, 1) as a baseline, then use ACF/PACF plots and information criteria (AIC, BIC) to refine.

model = ARIMA(unrate['UNRATE'], order=(1, 1, 1))
fit = model.fit()
print(fit.summary())

The summary lists coefficient estimates, standard errors, and fit statistics. Non-significant coefficients (p > 0.05) suggest you can simplify the model.
To forecast the next 24 months:

forecast = fit.get_forecast(steps=24)
mean = forecast.predicted_mean
ci = forecast.conf_int(alpha=0.05)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))
ax.plot(unrate.index, unrate['UNRATE'], label='Observed')
ax.plot(mean.index, mean, label='Forecast')
ax.fill_between(ci.index, ci.iloc[:, 0], ci.iloc[:, 1], alpha=0.2, label='95% CI')
ax.legend()
ax.set_title('Unemployment rate — 24-month forecast')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The shaded band is the 95% confidence interval. The further out you forecast, the wider the band — that’s the model’s honesty about its own uncertainty.
Take a moment to appreciate what you just did: you parsed a monthly series, diagnosed its structure, fit an ARIMA model, and produced a forecast with confidence bands in fewer than twenty lines of code.

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.10 Advanced: Forecasting with Prophet
Prophet (originally from Meta/Facebook) is a higher-level forecasting tool. It automates most of the decisions ARIMA asks you to make — seasonality, trend changepoints, holidays — and exposes a scikit-learn-style fit/predict API. You give up some control; you gain speed.
Prophet expects a specific DataFrame format: a column ds (the date) and a column y (the value).

from prophet import Prophet

df = unrate.reset_index().rename(columns={'DATE': 'ds', 'UNRATE': 'y'})

m = Prophet(yearly_seasonality=True, weekly_seasonality=False, daily_seasonality=False)
m.fit(df)

future = m.make_future_dataframe(periods=24, freq='MS')
forecast = m.predict(future)
m.plot(forecast)
plt.title('Prophet forecast: unemployment rate')
plt.show()

The m.plot_components(forecast) call splits the fit into trend and seasonal components — useful for presenting to non-technical stakeholders.






WarningProphet installation



Prophet requires a working C++ compiler and the cmdstan library, which can make installation tricky — especially on Windows. If pip install prophet fails, try conda install -c conda-forge prophet. If that also fails, the statsmodels ARIMA approach above will serve you well for this course. See the Prophet installation guide for troubleshooting.


When do you pick Prophet over ARIMA? Prophet is a good default for daily or sub-daily data with strong seasonality, for series with obvious changepoints (COVID is the canonical example), and when you need a working forecast in a meeting tomorrow. ARIMA is a better default when you need to understand every component of the model, when your data is short, or when your stakeholders expect statistical reporting (coefficients, p-values).

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.11 Working with Real Economic Data
Three reliable, free sources:

FRED — Federal Reserve Economic Data. Thousands of US macroeconomic series (unemployment, CPI, GDP, interest rates). Free CSV downloads, or use the FRED API (see Chapter 17).
World Bank Open Data — country-level economic, health, and education indicators for ~200 countries from 1960 onward.
IMF Data — international financial statistics, balance of payments, government finance.

Each publishes CSV files with different date formats. Always check the first few rows to see how the publisher encoded dates, missing values, and frequency metadata. FRED uses a single DATE column; the World Bank uses a wide format with one column per year that you’ll need to melt into long format.

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.12 Public Interest Context: Broken Continuity
Time series depend on continuity. Monthly unemployment can only be compared across decades if the Bureau of Labor Statistics kept collecting it the same way, publishing it on the same schedule, defining “unemployed” the same way. When agencies change methodology — as the Census Bureau did for the American Community Survey, or as the CDC did during COVID-19 — the continuity needed for oversight breaks down. A break in a data series is also a break in public accountability.
Some breaks are honest: new measurement technology, more granular categories. Some are less so: politically contested series that are discontinued, renamed, or reclassified under budget pressure. When you work with a long-running government series, it’s worth asking: what changed along the way, and whose story does the gap hide?






NoteConnection to Chapter 4



This section connects to the concepts of erosion and oversight introduced in Chapter 4. A time series breaks whenever methodology or funding changes — and those breaks erode the continuity that oversight depends on.

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.13 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningValueError: Inferred frequency None from passed values does not conform to passed frequency



What it means: There are missing dates in your index, or the index is not monotonically increasing.
How to fix it: Run df = df.asfreq('MS') (or whatever frequency fits). Check for gaps with df[df['value'].isna()]. Fill gaps with .ffill(), .interpolate(), or by imputing from a known baseline.








WarningARIMA fit produces warnings about non-stationarity



What it means: The d parameter is too low — your series is not yet stationary after the number of differences you specified.
How to fix it: Plot the differenced series. If it still has a trend, increase d to 2. Use the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test (statsmodels.tsa.stattools.adfuller) to test stationarity formally.








WarningProphet forecast looks wildly wrong on short series



What it means: Prophet expects at least a year of data to detect yearly seasonality. On shorter series it falls back on trend alone, which can overshoot.
How to fix it: Turn off seasonality components that aren’t supported by your data: Prophet(yearly_seasonality=False, weekly_seasonality=False). Consider ARIMA instead — it’s more conservative on small samples.








WarningDecomposition returns blank trend at the edges



What it means: seasonal_decompose uses a centered moving average, which requires observations on both sides. The first and last period/2 points have NaN trend values.
How to fix it: This is expected behavior. Drop the NaN rows, or use a two-sided rolling mean with min_periods to get edge estimates.

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.14 Key Takeaways

A time series is a DataFrame with a DatetimeIndex. Parse, set, and sort the index before anything else.
Trend, seasonality, stationarity, autocorrelation — the four concepts to get comfortable with. Visualize each one.
ARIMA gives you fine control; Prophet gives you automation. Pick based on what your audience expects and how much time you have.
Always plot your forecast alongside the observed data with a confidence band. A forecast without uncertainty is a dangerous graph.
Long government time series are public infrastructure. Methodology breaks matter.

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.15 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on time series analysis






statsmodels time-series analysis — statsmodels.org/stable/tsa.html is the canonical reference.
Prophet documentation — facebook.github.io/prophet/docs/quick_start.html has a clear walk-through.
sktime — sktime.net/ is a unified interface to many forecasting algorithms (classical, ML, deep learning).
“Forecasting: Principles and Practice” by Hyndman & Athanasopoulos — free online at otexts.com/fpp3/. Uses R, but the concepts transfer directly.
FRED — fred.stlouisfed.org/ is the single best source of US economic time series for teaching and analysis.

title: 16  Time Series Data
text:
16.16 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Decompose a FRED series





Download a monthly FRED series of your choice (e.g., CPIAUCSL for CPI, HOUST for housing starts, RSAFS for retail sales). Set up the time-series DataFrame, run seasonal_decompose with an additive model and period=12, and interpret the four panels in a paragraph. Does the seasonal component match your prior intuition about the series?









NoteExercise 2: Compare ARIMA and Prophet forecasts





Using the same FRED series from Exercise 1, fit an ARIMA(1,1,1) model and produce a 12-month forecast with confidence bands. Then fit a Prophet model and produce a 12-month forecast. Overlay both forecasts on the same plot. Where do they agree? Where do they differ? Write a short paragraph explaining which forecast you would trust more and why. If Prophet fails to install, discuss which choices ARIMA required you to make that Prophet would have automated.

title: 17  Structured Web Data
text:
17.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 17  Structured Web Data
text:
Parse JSON and XML documents into Python dictionaries and pandas DataFrames using the standard library and pd.json_normalize.
Fetch web resources using requests and parse HTML with Beautiful Soup.
Query a public API, handle pagination, and turn the response into a tidy DataFrame.
Practice scraping ethics: robots.txt, rate limiting, caching, and terms of service.
Explain the post-API age and why structured-data skills matter more than they did a decade ago.

title: 17  Structured Web Data
text:
17.2 Why Structured Web Data Matters
Data does not always arrive as a CSV. Government filings are packaged as XML. API responses come back as JSON. News articles embed data tables in HTML that never existed as a separate download. If you limit yourself to spreadsheet formats, you leave most of the web’s data on the table.
This chapter teaches you to work with data as it actually lives on the web. You’ll parse JSON and XML, scrape HTML tables, and query APIs. The goal is resilience: whatever format a publisher chose, you can get it into a DataFrame and move on.
If this chapter feels like a grab bag of small new skills, that’s because it is. Each format — JSON, XML, HTML — has its own conventions, and the only way to internalize them is to parse a few real examples. The click comes once you’ve done the same thing three times with slightly different data.

title: 17  Structured Web Data
text:
17.3 The Post-API Age
For a brief period — roughly 2006 to 2018 — platforms like Twitter, Facebook, Reddit, and YouTube published generous public APIs that let researchers, journalists, and developers query their data on reasonable terms. That era is largely over. Twitter/X’s free tier is effectively dead. Reddit’s 2023 API changes priced out most academic and civic users. Facebook’s CrowdTangle was shut down. The post-API age, a term coined by communication scholar Deen Freelon in a 2018 paper, describes this shift: platforms that were once open are now gated, and the data that supports public understanding of those platforms is shrinking.
What does this mean for you? Two things. First, the skills in this chapter — scraping HTML, parsing structured files, working with the APIs that remain free and public — are more important than ever. When one door closes, you need to know how to find another. Second, when you do scrape or query, document your methodology and preserve your data, because the same source may not be available tomorrow.
The examples in this chapter use APIs that are reliably free and publicly accessible — the ones that are likely to still be here when you reread this chapter in a year:

FRED API — Federal Reserve Economic Data.
Census Bureau API — US demographic and economic data.
OpenAlex API — scholarly publication metadata.
Wikipedia / MediaWiki API — Wikipedia content and metadata.
Open Library API — book and author records.

title: 17  Structured Web Data
text:
17.4 Setup
You’ll use requests for HTTP, Beautiful Soup for HTML parsing, and Python’s standard library json and xml.etree.ElementTree modules for the other two formats.

import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
import json
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
import pandas as pd







TipIf you get an ImportError or ModuleNotFoundError





requests and beautifulsoup4 are not part of the standard library. Run this in a new cell:
!pip install requests beautifulsoup4
Then restart your kernel (Kernel → Restart) and run the import cell again. If you’re using Anaconda, try conda install -c conda-forge requests beautifulsoup4 instead. json and xml.etree.ElementTree ship with Python; if those fail to import, your Python install is broken.

title: 17  Structured Web Data
text:
17.5 JSON
JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is the dominant data format for modern web APIs. It encodes two structures: objects (like Python dicts) and arrays (like Python lists), which can nest arbitrarily.

17.5.1 Parsing a JSON string

raw = '''{
  "title": "Information Exploration",
  "authors": ["Brian Keegan"],
  "year": 2026,
  "chapters": [
    {"num": 1, "name": "Setup"},
    {"num": 17, "name": "Structured Web Data"}
  ]
}'''

data = json.loads(raw)
print(data['title'])
print(data['chapters'][1]['name'])

json.loads() parses a string; json.load() parses an open file. Going the other direction is json.dumps() (to string) and json.dump() (to file).


17.5.2 From nested JSON to a flat DataFrame
Real API responses are usually deeply nested. pd.json_normalize() flattens nesting into columns with dot-separated names.

response = [
  {'id': 1, 'title': 'A', 'author': {'name': 'Alice', 'org': 'ORG1'}},
  {'id': 2, 'title': 'B', 'author': {'name': 'Bob', 'org': 'ORG2'}},
]

df = pd.json_normalize(response)
print(df.columns.tolist())  # ['id', 'title', 'author.name', 'author.org']

If one of the nested fields is itself a list of records, pass record_path to json_normalize to pull each nested list item into its own row.

title: 17  Structured Web Data
17  Structured Web Data
text:
17.7 HTML and Web Scraping
When a publisher offers neither an API nor a downloadable file, but does publish the data on a web page, scraping is the fallback.

17.7.1 Fetching a page

headers = {'User-Agent': 'info3401-class-project (your-email@example.edu)'}
response = requests.get('https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Colorado', headers=headers)
response.raise_for_status()  # raise an exception if the request failed
print(response.status_code)  # 200 means success

The User-Agent header identifies you to the server. Many sites block the default python-requests/x.y agent. Use a descriptive string that includes your contact information. raise_for_status() turns a failed request into a Python exception you can catch.


17.7.2 Parsing with Beautiful Soup

soup = BeautifulSoup(response.text, 'html.parser')

# All links on the page
links = [a.get('href') for a in soup.find_all('a') if a.get('href')]
print(f'Found {len(links)} links')

# The first table on the page
first_table = soup.find('table')

Beautiful Soup parses HTML into a tree you navigate with .find(), .find_all(), and CSS selectors via .select(). The selector syntax is the same one you’d use in a browser’s developer tools ('div.content table.infobox').


17.7.3 Extracting HTML tables
When the page contains an actual <table> element, pandas has a shortcut:

tables = pd.read_html('https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_the_United_States')
print(f'Found {len(tables)} tables')
print(tables[0].head())

pd.read_html() returns a list of DataFrames — one per <table>. This one line replaces dozens of manual parsing lines, when the publisher was kind enough to use real <table> elements.


17.7.4 Scraping ethics

Read robots.txt — append /robots.txt to any site’s root URL (e.g., https://example.com/robots.txt). It tells automated clients which paths the operator prefers you not to crawl.
Rate limit — pause between requests (time.sleep(1) between each). A burst of hundreds of requests per second from one IP can be indistinguishable from a denial-of-service attack.
Cache aggressively — save every response to disk during development so you aren’t refetching the same page dozens of times while you debug.
Read the terms of service — some sites explicitly prohibit scraping. Others tolerate it for non-commercial use. Knowing the terms protects you.
Identify yourself — a descriptive User-Agent string is good etiquette and makes it easier for a confused webmaster to contact you before blocking your IP.







WarningScraping is brittle by design



The HTML structure of a page can change at any time. A CSS selector that works today may return nothing tomorrow. Build scrapers defensively: log how many records you extracted, alert on suspiciously low counts, and always cache the raw HTML so you can reparse when the page changes.

title: 17  Structured Web Data
text:
17.8 Working with APIs
APIs are the friendliest way to get structured data from a publisher, when they exist and are free. Here’s a short example querying the OpenAlex scholarly metadata API — no authentication required.

url = 'https://api.openalex.org/works'
params = {
    'search': 'public interest data science',
    'per-page': 25,
    'mailto': 'your-email@example.edu',  # polite pool, faster rate limits
}

r = requests.get(url, params=params)
r.raise_for_status()
payload = r.json()

print(f"Total results: {payload['meta']['count']:,}")
print(f"Returned on this page: {len(payload['results'])}")

df = pd.json_normalize(payload['results'])
print(df[['id', 'title', 'publication_year']].head())

A few patterns worth noticing: the query parameters are passed as a dict; the response is JSON parsed via r.json(); pd.json_normalize handles the nesting; and adding a mailto parameter puts you in OpenAlex’s “polite pool” for higher rate limits.

17.8.1 Pagination
Most APIs don’t return all results in one response — they paginate. OpenAlex paginates via a page parameter or a cursor. Always read the API docs, then loop.

import time

all_results = []
page = 1
while True:
    r = requests.get(url, params={**params, 'page': page})
    r.raise_for_status()
    batch = r.json()['results']
    if not batch:
        break
    all_results.extend(batch)
    page += 1
    time.sleep(0.2)  # be polite
    if page > 40:    # safety stop — don't run away
        break

print(f'Collected {len(all_results)} records')

The if not batch: break pattern stops when the API returns an empty page. The explicit page > 40 safety stop prevents infinite loops during development.
Take a moment to appreciate what you just did: you queried a scholarly database, handled pagination, and loaded thousands of records into a DataFrame without ever opening a web browser.

title: 17  Structured Web Data
section: 17.9 Public Interest Context: Access as Infrastructure
text:
17.9 Public Interest Context: Access as Infrastructure
APIs are infrastructure. When a public-interest organization depends on an API for accountability reporting, that API becomes a piece of civic infrastructure — and its terms of service, authentication requirements, and continued existence all become public concerns.
The post-API age is best understood as enclosure plus exemption. Enclosure is the gating: what was freely queryable becomes paid, rate-limited, or authenticated-only. Exemption is the refusal: platforms that used to share data stop sharing, and what they know about their users becomes invisible to the public. The skills in this chapter — scraping, parsing, API-hopping between the free endpoints that remain — are responses to both pressures.
Building redundant data pipelines, caching aggressively, and documenting every source is not paranoia. It is the reasonable response to a landscape where the data you rely on may not be accessible next year.






NoteConnection to Chapter 4



This section connects to the concepts of enclosure and exemption introduced in Chapter 4. APIs are how access is gated and platform data is made available (or not). Structured-data skills let public-interest work survive the shift from open APIs to closed ones.

title: 17  Structured Web Data
text:
17.10 Common Issues in This Chapter






Warningrequests.exceptions.HTTPError: 403 Forbidden



What it means: The server rejected your request — often because of a default User-Agent, a missing authentication header, or a known rate-limit violation.
How to fix it: Add a User-Agent header with your contact info. If the API requires authentication, confirm your key is set. If you’ve been hitting the site hard, wait several minutes before retrying.








Warningjson.decoder.JSONDecodeError: Expecting value



What it means: The response body is not valid JSON — often HTML instead (an error page) or an empty string (a silent failure).
How to fix it: Print response.status_code and response.text[:500] to see what the server actually returned. If the status is 200 but the body is HTML, you hit a login wall or a generic error page.








WarningBeautiful Soup returns None when you expected a match



What it means: Your CSS selector is wrong, or the structure of the page is different from what you expected.
How to fix it: Open the page in a browser, open developer tools, and copy the exact selector. Check that the element exists in the raw HTML (response.text) — it may be injected later by JavaScript, which means you need a browser automation tool like Playwright.








Warningpd.read_html() returns the wrong table or none at all



What it means: pd.read_html only sees real <table> elements. If the page uses a grid of <div> elements styled to look like a table, it won’t work.
How to fix it: Fall back on Beautiful Soup and iterate the rows manually. Or use pd.read_html(io=response.text, match='Some unique string in the header') to filter to the table you want when the page has many.








WarningYou hit an API rate limit and all subsequent requests fail



What it means: The API responded with HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) or started returning empty results. You need to back off.
How to fix it: Add time.sleep(1) or more between requests. Check the API’s documented rate limits and stay well under them. For APIs that expose a Retry-After header, honor it. Cache every response to disk so you don’t refetch what you already have.

title: 17  Structured Web Data
text:
17.11 Key Takeaways

JSON and XML are the two dominant structured data formats. json.loads + pd.json_normalize and xml.etree.ElementTree handle most cases.
Beautiful Soup and pd.read_html() cover HTML scraping. Identify yourself with a real User-Agent and respect robots.txt.
APIs beat scraping when they exist — but the post-API age means they exist less often. Build resilience by knowing both.
Pagination, authentication, and rate limits are the three things that break first. Read the API docs, then build conservative defaults.
Public-interest data work is increasingly structured-data work. Treat every source as potentially transient and cache everything.

title: 17  Structured Web Data
text:
17.12 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on structured web data






requests docs — requests.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ is the canonical reference.
Beautiful Soup docs — crummy.com/software/BeautifulSoup/bs4/doc/ is friendly and complete.
Real Python: Beautiful Soup web scraper tutorial — realpython.com/beautiful-soup-web-scraper-python/.
Playwright for Python — playwright.dev/python/ handles JavaScript-rendered pages that Beautiful Soup cannot see.
Scrapy — scrapy.org/ is the industrial-strength framework for large, long-running scraping projects.

text:
17.13 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Query the OpenAlex API





Use the OpenAlex API to search for works published in 2023 with the word “redlining” in the title. Retrieve the first 100 results, parse them into a DataFrame, and report the top 5 author-affiliated institutions by frequency. Remember to include a mailto parameter for the polite pool.
