Use Beautiful Soup or pd.read_html to extract the list of US state capitals from Wikipedia’s “List of capitals in the United States” page. Return a DataFrame with columns state, capital, and population. Handle the disambiguation case where multiple tables on the same page have similar structure. Document any rows that required manual cleanup in a short Known Issues Log entry.

title: 18  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
18.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 18  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
Move beyond df.describe() to characterize the shape, skewness, and spread of a distribution using quantiles and the coefficient of variation.
Identify outliers with the IQR method and the z-score method, and decide whether each outlier reflects an error or a legitimate extreme observation.
Produce frequency tables with .value_counts() and cross-tabulations with pd.crosstab().
Build a correlation matrix with df.corr() and interpret its structure.
Write a short descriptive summary that documents N, shape, outliers, and missingness for any new dataset.

title: 18  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
18.2 Why Descriptive Exploration Comes First
Before you compare groups or test hypotheses, you need to understand what your data looks like. Descriptive exploration is the process of characterizing a dataset’s central tendencies, variability, shape, and outliers — building an informed mental model of the data that guides every subsequent analytical decision. This chapter goes beyond the .describe() method you have already used and introduces systematic techniques for understanding distributions, identifying unusual observations, examining categorical patterns, and constructing frequency tables and cross-tabulations.
The philosophy here is qualitative in the broadest sense: you are looking for patterns, surprises, and anomalies rather than computing formal test statistics. The goal is to know your data well enough that nothing in the later chapters catches you off guard.

title: 18  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
18.3 Setup
This chapter uses pandas, numpy, matplotlib, and seaborn.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.3f}'.format)

sns.set_style('whitegrid')







WarningIf you get an ImportError





Install missing packages with conda install pandas numpy matplotlib seaborn (or pip install pandas numpy matplotlib seaborn) and restart the Jupyter kernel. seaborn is sometimes missing from minimal environments; that is a common first-time cause. See Chapter 1.

title: 18  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
18.4 Datasets
We continue with the datasets from earlier chapters, loading and preparing them with the derived columns already established.

# Lahman batting with derived metrics
batting = pd.read_csv('Batting.csv')
batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['TB'] = batting['H'] + 2*batting['2B'] + 3*batting['3B'] + 4*batting['HR']
batting['SLG'] = batting['TB'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['HBP'] = batting['HBP'].fillna(0)
batting['SF'] = batting['SF'].fillna(0)
batting['OBP'] = (
    (batting['H'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'])
    / (batting['AB'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'] + batting['SF']).replace(0, float('nan'))
)
batting['OPS'] = batting['OBP'] + batting['SLG']
batting['decade'] = (batting['yearID'] // 10) * 10

# Filter to qualified player-seasons for meaningful rate statistics
qualified = batting[batting['AB'] >= 100].copy()

print('Qualified player-seasons:', qualified.shape[0])


# Colorado population estimates
url = 'https://storage.googleapis.com/co-publicdata/sya-county.csv'
pop = pd.read_csv(url, skiprows=1, dtype={'countyfips': str})
estimates = pop[pop['datatype'] == 'Estimate'].copy()


# Cannabis sales (cleaned)
cannabis = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2, skipfooter=8, engine='python', na_values=['NR']
)
cannabis.columns = ['month', 'year', 'county', 'medical', 'retail']
cannabis['county'] = cannabis['county'].str.strip()
for col in ['medical', 'retail']:
    cannabis[col] = pd.to_numeric(
        cannabis[col].str.replace('$', '', regex=False).str.replace(',', '', regex=False),
        errors='coerce'
    )
cannabis = cannabis[cannabis['county'] != 'Total']

title: 18  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
18.5 Beyond .describe(): Understanding Distributions
The .describe() method gives you the five-number summary (min, 25th percentile, median, 75th percentile, max) plus the mean and count. This is a useful starting point, but it hides important features of a distribution: its shape, its skewness, and whether it has multiple modes.

18.5.1 Skewness
Many real-world variables are right-skewed — a long tail extends toward high values while most observations cluster near the low end. Income, city populations, and home prices all follow this pattern. Cannabis sales across Colorado counties are a clear example:

annual_sales = (
    cannabis[cannabis['year'] == 2023]
    .groupby('county')['retail']
    .sum()
    .dropna()
)

print(annual_sales.describe())
print(f'\nSkewness: {annual_sales.skew():.2f}')
print(f'Mean:     ${annual_sales.mean():,.0f}')
print(f'Median:   ${annual_sales.median():,.0f}')

When the mean is substantially larger than the median, the distribution is right-skewed, and the mean is being pulled upward by a few very large values. In this case, the median is a better measure of the “typical” county than the mean.


18.5.2 Quantiles and percentiles
Quantiles divide your data into equal-sized groups. The .quantile() method lets you compute arbitrary percentiles:

# Decile boundaries for batting average
percentiles = qualified['BA'].quantile(np.arange(0, 1.1, 0.1))
print('Batting average deciles:')
print(percentiles.round(3))

Quantiles are useful for creating bins (as with pd.qcut() in Chapter 8), for identifying where a specific value falls in the distribution, and for detecting whether the distribution has unusual gaps or concentrations.


18.5.3 The coefficient of variation
Standard deviation tells you how spread out values are, but its meaning depends on the scale of the variable. A standard deviation of 1,000 is enormous for a variable with a mean of 500 but negligible for a variable with a mean of 10,000,000. The coefficient of variation (CV) — the ratio of the standard deviation to the mean — provides a scale-free measure of relative variability:

for col in ['medical', 'retail']:
    mean_val = cannabis[col].mean()
    std_val = cannabis[col].std()
    cv = std_val / mean_val
    print(f'{col}: mean={mean_val:,.0f}, std={std_val:,.0f}, CV={cv:.2f}')

A CV above 1.0 indicates very high variability relative to the mean — typical of skewed distributions.

title: 18  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
18.6 Identifying Outliers
An outlier is an observation that is unusually far from the rest of the data. Outliers are not automatically errors — they may be legitimate extreme values that are analytically important. The goal is to identify them, investigate them, and make a documented decision about whether to include them in your analysis.

18.6.1 The IQR method
The interquartile range (IQR) is the distance between the 25th and 75th percentiles. The standard convention defines outliers as values below Q1 − 1.5 × IQR or above Q3 + 1.5 × IQR:

def find_outliers_iqr(series, multiplier=1.5):
    """Identify outliers using the IQR method."""
    Q1 = series.quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = series.quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    lower = Q1 - multiplier * IQR
    upper = Q3 + multiplier * IQR

    outliers = series[(series < lower) | (series > upper)]

    print(f'Q1={Q1:.3f}, Q3={Q3:.3f}, IQR={IQR:.3f}')
    print(f'Lower fence: {lower:.3f}, Upper fence: {upper:.3f}')
    print(f'Outliers: {len(outliers)} of {len(series)} ({len(outliers)/len(series):.1%})')

    return outliers

hr_outliers = find_outliers_iqr(qualified['HR'])



18.6.2 The z-score method
A z-score measures how many standard deviations a value is from the mean. Values with |z| > 3 are conventionally considered outliers:

qualified['hr_zscore'] = (
    (qualified['HR'] - qualified['HR'].mean()) / qualified['HR'].std()
)

extreme = qualified[qualified['hr_zscore'].abs() > 3]
print(f'Observations with |z| > 3: {len(extreme)}')
print(extreme[['playerID', 'yearID', 'HR', 'hr_zscore']].sort_values('hr_zscore', ascending=False).head(10))



18.6.3 Investigating outliers
Once you identify outliers, investigate them before deciding what to do. Are they data entry errors, measurement artifacts, or genuinely extreme observations?

# Are the extreme HR seasons real or errors?
# Check: do the player names and years match known record holders?
print(extreme[['playerID', 'yearID', 'teamID', 'HR']].sort_values('HR', ascending=False).head(5))

If these are legitimate records (Barry Bonds’ 73 HR in 2001, for instance), removing them would be analytically dishonest. If they are data entry errors (a player with 999 HR), removing them is necessary. Document the decision either way.

title: 18  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
18.7 Categorical Patterns
For non-numeric columns, the primary descriptive tools are frequency tables and cross-tabulations.

18.7.1 Frequency tables
The .value_counts() method is your workhorse for categorical exploration. Use it with normalize=True to get proportions instead of counts:

# County distribution of cannabis sales records
county_counts = cannabis['county'].value_counts()
print(f'Counties with data: {len(county_counts)}')
print(f'\nTop 10:')
print(county_counts.head(10))
print(f'\nBottom 5:')
print(county_counts.tail(5))


# Age band distribution in Colorado (2020)
bins = [0, 18, 65, 101]
labels = ['Children (0-17)', 'Working Age (18-64)', 'Seniors (65+)']
est_2020 = estimates[estimates['year'] == 2020].copy()
est_2020['age_band'] = pd.cut(est_2020['age'], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)

band_totals = est_2020.groupby('age_band', observed=True)['totalpopulation'].sum()
print('Statewide population by age band (2020):')
print(band_totals)
print(f'\nProportions:')
print((band_totals / band_totals.sum()).round(3))



18.7.2 Cross-tabulations
A cross-tabulation (crosstab) shows the joint distribution of two categorical variables. Use pd.crosstab() to build one:

# Decades × league for batting data
batting_with_league = batting.copy()

decade_counts = pd.crosstab(
    qualified['decade'],
    qualified['teamID'].str[:2],  # rough proxy
    margins=True
)
print('Player-seasons by decade (first 5 decades):')
print(decade_counts.head())

Cross-tabulations become more informative with normalize to show proportions and with margins=True to include row and column totals.

title: 18  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
18.8 Summarizing Multiple Variables Together
When you have several numeric columns, examining them individually is tedious. A correlation matrix provides a compact summary of pairwise relationships:

batting_metrics = qualified[['AB', 'H', 'HR', 'BB', 'BA', 'SLG', 'OBP', 'OPS']].copy()

corr_matrix = batting_metrics.corr().round(2)
print(corr_matrix)

We will visualize this matrix as a heatmap in Chapter 19 and test individual correlations formally in Chapter 20.

title: 18  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
18.9 The Descriptive Summary Template
For any new dataset or analytical question, produce a descriptive summary that covers these elements. You can structure this as a Markdown cell in your notebook:
## Descriptive Summary: [Dataset / Variable]

**Unit of analysis:** [what each row represents]
**N:** [number of observations after filtering]
**Key statistics:** mean, median, std, min, max
**Shape:** [symmetric, right-skewed, bimodal, etc.]
**Outliers:** [count, method used, decision made]
**Missing data:** [count, pattern, impact on analysis]
**Notable patterns:** [any surprises, clusters, or anomalies]

title: 18  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
18.10 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningThe mean looks “off” but the data is fine



What it means: The distribution is heavily skewed. A few extreme values pull the mean in one direction, even though the rest of the data sits in a narrow range.
How to fix it: Report the median alongside the mean, and name the shape explicitly (“right-skewed”). The median is the better “typical” measure for skewed variables like income or sales.








WarningYour outlier detector flagged half the data



What it means: The 1.5 × IQR or |z| > 3 rule assumes a roughly symmetric distribution. On highly skewed data, both methods produce far too many “outliers.”
How to fix it: Log-transform the variable (np.log1p) before applying outlier rules, or use a more robust method like median absolute deviation (MAD). Investigate what “unusual” means in the domain before applying a mechanical rule.








Warningcrosstab margins look wrong



What it means: Missing values are excluded from crosstab by default, so the marginal totals may not match len(df).
How to fix it: Pass dropna=False to include NaN as its own category, or pre-fill missing values with a sentinel like 'Unknown' before crosstabbing. Document the choice.








WarningA correlation is high but the relationship is noise



What it means: Correlation summarizes a monotonic trend. A strong correlation can hide outliers, nonlinearity, or a relationship driven by a handful of points.
How to fix it: Always visualize before concluding. A scatter plot or pairplot will show what the correlation coefficient cannot. See Chapter 19.

title: 18  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
18.11 Key Takeaways

Descriptive exploration is about building intuition — shape, skew, outliers, missingness — before any formal comparison.
The mean lies when the distribution is skewed. Always check the median, and name the shape out loud.
Outlier detection is a starting point, not an answer. Every outlier deserves an investigation and a documented decision.
.value_counts(), pd.crosstab(), and .corr() are three tools that cover most categorical-and-multivariate exploration.
Summarize every new dataset with the descriptive summary template before moving on. It forces you to answer the “what is this data?” question explicitly.

title: 18  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
18.12 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on descriptive statistics






pandas: Descriptive statistics — pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/basics.html#descriptive-statistics documents the full catalog of methods.
pandas: pd.crosstab — pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.crosstab.html is the API reference for cross-tabs.
scipy.stats: Summary statistics — docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/stats.html#summary-statistics covers skewness, kurtosis, and robust estimators.
Real Python: Python Statistics Fundamentals — realpython.com/python-statistics/ is an accessible walkthrough with examples.
“Exploratory Data Analysis” by John Tukey — the founding text on descriptive exploration. Dated but worth skimming for its unchanged wisdom about looking at data before testing it.

title: 18  Descriptive and Qualitative Exploration
text:
18.13 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Distribution profile





Compute the mean, median, standard deviation, skewness, and coefficient of variation for retail cannabis sales in 2023 across all counties. Based on these statistics, describe the shape of the distribution in one or two sentences. Which measure of central tendency (mean or median) better represents the “typical” county?






NoteSolution






annual = cannabis[cannabis['year'] == 2023].groupby('county')['retail'].sum().dropna()
print(f'Mean:     ${annual.mean():,.0f}')
print(f'Median:   ${annual.median():,.0f}')
print(f'Std:      ${annual.std():,.0f}')
print(f'Skewness: {annual.skew():.2f}')
print(f'CV:       {annual.std() / annual.mean():.2f}')

The distribution is strongly right-skewed (skewness >> 1), with a few large urban counties pulling the mean well above the median. The median better represents the typical county.












NoteExercise 2: Outlier investigation





Using the IQR method, identify outlier counties in 2023 retail cannabis sales. For each outlier, look up the county’s population. Are these outliers because they are genuinely unusual, or because they are large counties?






NoteSolution






outliers = find_outliers_iqr(annual)
print('Outlier counties:')
print(outliers.sort_values(ascending=False))

These are almost certainly large urban counties (Denver, El Paso, Arapahoe, etc.). They are outliers in sales volume because they have more people, not because their per-capita sales are unusual. Computing per-capita sales (by dividing by population) would likely eliminate most of these as outliers.












NoteExercise 3: Cross-tabulation





Create a cross-tabulation of cannabis counties by year and whether they have any medical sales (yes/no). Has the number of counties reporting medical sales changed over time?






NoteSolution






cannabis['has_medical'] = cannabis['medical'].notna() & (cannabis['medical'] > 0)
crosstab = pd.crosstab(cannabis['year'], cannabis['has_medical'], margins=True)
crosstab.columns = ['No Medical', 'Has Medical', 'Total']
print(crosstab)

title: 19  Visual Exploration
text:
19.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 19  Visual Exploration
text:
Build matplotlib figures using plt.subplots and annotate them with titles, labels, and reference lines.
Visualize distributions with histograms, KDEs, box plots, and seaborn violin plots.
Explore relationships with scatter plots, seaborn pair plots, and correlation heatmaps.
Plot trends over time with sns.lineplot, including confidence bands.
Use seaborn’s FacetGrid for small-multiple comparisons across groups.
Choose the right plot type for a given analytical question.

title: 19  Visual Exploration
text:
19.2 Why Visualize
A well-chosen visualization reveals structure that no table of numbers can convey: the shape of a distribution, the trajectory of a trend, the clustering of groups, the isolation of outliers. This chapter introduces a systematic repertoire of plot types for exploratory data analysis using matplotlib and seaborn, organized by what you are trying to see — distributions, relationships, trends, and comparisons. The goal is not to produce publication-ready graphics (though some of these will be close) but to build visual intuitions that inform your analytical decisions.
Every plot in this chapter answers a question. Before writing any plotting code, state the question in a Markdown cell. After producing the plot, write one or two sentences interpreting what it shows. This habit turns exploratory plots from pretty pictures into analytical evidence.

title: 19  Visual Exploration
text:
19.3 Setup
This chapter uses pandas, numpy, matplotlib, and seaborn.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.3f}'.format)

# Seaborn defaults: clean, readable, and appropriately sized
sns.set_style('whitegrid')
sns.set_context('notebook', font_scale=1.1)







WarningIf you get an ImportError





Install missing packages with conda install pandas numpy matplotlib seaborn (or pip install pandas numpy matplotlib seaborn) and restart the Jupyter kernel. seaborn depends on matplotlib, so if one is missing, the other likely is too. See Chapter 1.

title: 19  Visual Exploration
text:
19.4 Datasets

# Lahman batting — qualified player-seasons
batting = pd.read_csv('Batting.csv')
batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['TB'] = batting['H'] + 2*batting['2B'] + 3*batting['3B'] + 4*batting['HR']
batting['SLG'] = batting['TB'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['HBP'] = batting['HBP'].fillna(0)
batting['SF'] = batting['SF'].fillna(0)
batting['OBP'] = (
    (batting['H'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'])
    / (batting['AB'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'] + batting['SF']).replace(0, float('nan'))
)
batting['OPS'] = batting['OBP'] + batting['SLG']
batting['decade'] = (batting['yearID'] // 10) * 10
qualified = batting[batting['AB'] >= 100].copy()

# Salaries
salaries = pd.read_csv('Salaries.csv')
bat_sal = pd.merge(
    qualified, salaries[['playerID', 'yearID', 'salary']],
    on=['playerID', 'yearID'], how='inner'
)


# Colorado population
url = 'https://storage.googleapis.com/co-publicdata/sya-county.csv'
pop = pd.read_csv(url, skiprows=1, dtype={'countyfips': str})
estimates = pop[pop['datatype'] == 'Estimate'].copy()
estimates['age_band'] = pd.cut(
    estimates['age'], bins=[0, 18, 65, 101],
    labels=['Children (0-17)', 'Working Age (18-64)', 'Seniors (65+)'],
    right=False
)


# Cannabis sales
cannabis = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2, skipfooter=8, engine='python', na_values=['NR']
)
cannabis.columns = ['month', 'year', 'county', 'medical', 'retail']
cannabis['county'] = cannabis['county'].str.strip()
for col in ['medical', 'retail']:
    cannabis[col] = pd.to_numeric(
        cannabis[col].str.replace('$', '', regex=False).str.replace(',', '', regex=False),
        errors='coerce'
    )
cannabis = cannabis[cannabis['county'] != 'Total']

title: 19  Visual Exploration
text:
19.5 Visualizing Distributions
The first question to ask about any numeric variable is: what does its distribution look like? Is it symmetric, skewed, bimodal, or uniform? Distributions guide your choice of summary statistics (mean vs. median), your approach to outliers, and your selection of statistical tests.

19.5.1 Histograms
A histogram divides the range of values into bins and counts how many observations fall in each. It is the most direct visualization of a distribution’s shape.

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
ax.hist(qualified['BA'], bins=50, edgecolor='white', alpha=0.7)
ax.set_xlabel('Batting Average')
ax.set_ylabel('Count')
ax.set_title('Distribution of Batting Average (qualified player-seasons, AB ≥ 100)')
ax.axvline(qualified['BA'].mean(), color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'Mean: {qualified["BA"].mean():.3f}')
ax.axvline(qualified['BA'].median(), color='orange', linestyle='--', label=f'Median: {qualified["BA"].median():.3f}')
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The mean and median are close together, suggesting a roughly symmetric distribution. If they diverged substantially, the distribution would be skewed.


19.5.2 Kernel density estimation (KDE)
A KDE plot smooths the histogram into a continuous curve, making it easier to see the overall shape:

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
sns.kdeplot(data=qualified, x='BA', ax=ax, fill=True, alpha=0.3)
ax.set_title('KDE of Batting Average')
ax.set_xlabel('Batting Average')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



19.5.3 Comparing distributions across groups
Overlaying distributions for different groups reveals how they differ. Here we compare batting averages across eras:

era_filter = qualified['decade'].isin([1920, 1960, 2000])
era_data = qualified[era_filter].copy()
era_data['era'] = era_data['decade'].map({1920: '1920s', 1960: '1960s', 2000: '2000s'})

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
sns.kdeplot(data=era_data, x='BA', hue='era', ax=ax, fill=True, alpha=0.3)
ax.set_title('Batting Average Distribution by Era')
ax.set_xlabel('Batting Average')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



19.5.4 Box plots
Box plots show the five-number summary (min, Q1, median, Q3, max) plus individual outlier points. They are especially effective for comparing distributions across many groups:

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
decade_data = qualified[qualified['decade'] >= 1900]
sns.boxplot(data=decade_data, x='decade', y='BA', ax=ax, color='lightblue')
ax.set_title('Batting Average by Decade (AB ≥ 100)')
ax.set_xlabel('Decade')
ax.set_ylabel('Batting Average')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The boxes show the interquartile range (middle 50%), the horizontal line is the median, and the dots are outliers beyond the IQR fences.


19.5.5 Violin plots
A violin plot combines a box plot with a KDE, showing the full shape of each group’s distribution:

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
selected_decades = decade_data[decade_data['decade'].isin([1920, 1950, 1980, 2010])]
sns.violinplot(data=selected_decades, x='decade', y='HR', ax=ax, inner='quartile')
ax.set_title('Home Run Distribution by Selected Decades')
ax.set_xlabel('Decade')
ax.set_ylabel('Home Runs')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: 19  Visual Exploration
text:
19.6 Visualizing Relationships
When you want to understand how two numeric variables relate to each other, scatter plots and correlation heatmaps are your primary tools.

19.6.1 Scatter plots

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
sample = bat_sal[bat_sal['yearID'] == 2015].copy()
ax.scatter(sample['salary'] / 1e6, sample['OPS'], alpha=0.4, s=20)
ax.set_xlabel('Salary (millions $)')
ax.set_ylabel('OPS')
ax.set_title('Salary vs. OPS (2015 season, qualified batters)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

If the points form a rising cloud, the variables are positively correlated. If the cloud is flat or shapeless, there is little linear relationship. If the cloud curves, the relationship may be nonlinear — something a correlation coefficient alone would not reveal.


19.6.2 Adding context with color and size
Seaborn’s scatterplot() can encode additional variables using color (hue) and size:

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 6))
sns.scatterplot(
    data=sample, x=sample['salary']/1e6, y='OPS',
    hue='AB', size='HR', sizes=(20, 200), alpha=0.6, ax=ax
)
ax.set_xlabel('Salary (millions $)')
ax.set_title('Salary vs. OPS — sized by HR, colored by AB')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



19.6.3 Pair plots
When you have several numeric variables, a pair plot shows every pairwise scatter plot and each variable’s distribution in a single figure:

pair_cols = ['BA', 'SLG', 'OBP', 'HR']
pair_data = qualified[qualified['decade'] == 2010][pair_cols].dropna().sample(500, random_state=42)

sns.pairplot(pair_data, diag_kind='kde', plot_kws={'alpha': 0.3, 's': 15})
plt.suptitle('Pairwise Relationships — 2010s Batting (sample)', y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



19.6.4 Correlation heatmaps
A heatmap visualizes the correlation matrix from Chapter 18, making it easy to spot strong relationships at a glance:

metrics = qualified[['AB', 'H', 'HR', 'BB', 'BA', 'SLG', 'OBP', 'OPS']].dropna()
corr = metrics.corr()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='coolwarm', center=0, ax=ax,
            square=True, linewidths=0.5)
ax.set_title('Correlation Matrix — Batting Metrics')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Red cells indicate strong positive correlations, blue cells indicate negative correlations, and white cells indicate little linear relationship.

title: 19  Visual Exploration
text:
19.7 Visualizing Trends Over Time
Time series plots — line charts showing how a variable changes across time — are essential for longitudinal analysis.

19.7.1 Single trend line

# Statewide cannabis retail sales by year
annual_state = cannabis.groupby('year')['retail'].sum().reset_index()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
ax.plot(annual_state['year'], annual_state['retail'] / 1e9, marker='o')
ax.set_xlabel('Year')
ax.set_ylabel('Retail Sales ($ billions)')
ax.set_title('Colorado Statewide Retail Cannabis Sales')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



19.7.2 Multiple trend lines for comparison

# Compare top 5 counties
top_counties = (
    cannabis.groupby('county')['retail'].sum()
    .nlargest(5).index.tolist()
)
top_data = cannabis[cannabis['county'].isin(top_counties)]
county_annual = top_data.groupby(['year', 'county'])['retail'].sum().reset_index()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.lineplot(data=county_annual, x='year', y='retail', hue='county', marker='o', ax=ax)
ax.set_ylabel('Annual Retail Sales ($)')
ax.set_title('Retail Cannabis Sales — Top 5 Counties')
ax.legend(title='County', bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc='upper left')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



19.7.3 Trends with confidence bands
Seaborn can compute and display confidence intervals around aggregated trends:

decade_ba = qualified[qualified['decade'] >= 1900]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.lineplot(data=decade_ba, x='yearID', y='BA', ax=ax, ci=95)
ax.set_xlabel('Year')
ax.set_ylabel('Batting Average')
ax.set_title('League-Wide Batting Average Over Time (95% CI)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The shaded band shows the 95% confidence interval around the mean at each year, giving you a sense of both the trend and the variability.

title: 19  Visual Exploration
text:
19.8 Visualizing Comparisons
When you want to compare a metric across categorical groups, bar charts, grouped bar charts, and faceted plots make the differences visible.

19.8.1 Bar charts for group comparisons

# Dependency ratio by county type (top vs bottom 10)
dep_wide = (
    estimates[estimates['year'] == 2020]
    .groupby(['county', 'age_band'], observed=True)
    .agg(pop=('totalpopulation', 'sum'))
    .reset_index()
    .pivot_table(index='county', columns='age_band', values='pop', aggfunc='sum')
    .reset_index()
)
dep_wide.columns.name = None
dep_wide['dep_ratio'] = (
    (dep_wide['Children (0-17)'] + dep_wide['Seniors (65+)'])
    / dep_wide['Working Age (18-64)']
)

top5 = dep_wide.nlargest(5, 'dep_ratio')
bot5 = dep_wide.nsmallest(5, 'dep_ratio')
comparison = pd.concat([top5, bot5]).sort_values('dep_ratio', ascending=True)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
colors = ['steelblue' if x in bot5['county'].values else 'coral' for x in comparison['county']]
ax.barh(comparison['county'], comparison['dep_ratio'], color=colors)
ax.set_xlabel('Dependency Ratio')
ax.set_title('Highest and Lowest Dependency Ratios — Colorado Counties (2020)')
ax.axvline(dep_wide['dep_ratio'].median(), color='gray', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, label='Median')
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()



19.8.2 Faceted plots (small multiples)
Faceting breaks a single plot into a grid of subplots, one per group. This is one of the most effective techniques for comparing patterns across categories:

# Batting average distribution across four selected decades
four_decades = qualified[qualified['decade'].isin([1930, 1960, 1990, 2020])].copy()
four_decades['era'] = four_decades['decade'].astype(str) + 's'

g = sns.FacetGrid(four_decades, col='era', col_wrap=2, height=3.5, aspect=1.3)
g.map(sns.histplot, 'BA', bins=30, kde=True)
g.set_axis_labels('Batting Average', 'Count')
g.set_titles('{col_name}')
g.figure.suptitle('Batting Average Distribution by Era', y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: 19  Visual Exploration
text:
19.9 Choosing the Right Plot








Question
Plot type
When to use




What does this variable look like?
Histogram, KDE, box plot
Single numeric variable


How do distributions differ across groups?
Overlaid KDE, grouped box plot, violin plot
One numeric + one categorical


How are two variables related?
Scatter plot, pair plot
Two numeric variables


What are all pairwise relationships?
Correlation heatmap, pair plot
Many numeric variables


How has a variable changed over time?
Line plot
Numeric variable + time


How do groups compare on a metric?
Bar chart, grouped bar chart
One numeric + one categorical


How do patterns vary across subgroups?
Faceted plot (small multiples)
Any plot + a grouping variable

title: 19  Visual Exploration
text:
19.10 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningThe plot is too small or text is overlapping



What it means: The default figure size is often too small for more than a toy example.
How to fix it: Pass figsize=(10, 5) (or similar) to plt.subplots(). Call plt.tight_layout() before plt.show() to prevent label overlap. For long category labels, rotate with plt.xticks(rotation=45).








WarningSeaborn’s ci parameter is deprecated



What it means: In seaborn 0.12+, ci=95 was replaced with errorbar=('ci', 95) in functions like lineplot. Your code may throw a FutureWarning.
How to fix it: Replace ci=95 with errorbar=('ci', 95). For no confidence interval, use errorbar=None instead of ci=None. See seaborn’s release notes.








WarningThe plot shows <Figure size ... Axes: ...> text instead of a figure



What it means: You used a plotting call that returned the figure/axes object without rendering. This is common when the last expression in a notebook cell is a plot object.
How to fix it: End the cell with plt.show(), or assign the figure to an underscore: _ = ax.plot(...). Or put the call in a different cell.








WarningFacetGrid or hue does not color the way you expect



What it means: Seaborn infers color order from the column’s dtype. Numeric columns get a continuous palette; categorical columns get a discrete one.
How to fix it: Cast the column to str or category before plotting if you want a discrete palette. Or pass an explicit palette argument: palette='Set2'.

title: 19  Visual Exploration
text:
19.11 Key Takeaways

Every plot should answer a specific question. Write the question first, the code second, the interpretation third.
Distributions: histograms, KDEs, box plots, violin plots. Relationships: scatter, pairplot, heatmap. Trends: line plots. Comparisons: bar charts, faceted plots.
Seaborn and matplotlib compose. You can start a plot with sns. and keep refining it with ax. methods.
Always label axes, set a title, and include units. A plot without labels is not evidence.
Small multiples (faceted plots) beat one crowded plot almost every time.

title: 19  Visual Exploration
text:
19.12 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on visualization






matplotlib gallery — matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/ is the single best place to find a plot that resembles what you want, with code you can adapt.
seaborn tutorial — seaborn.pydata.org/tutorial.html walks through each plot family with realistic datasets.
seaborn API reference — seaborn.pydata.org/api.html documents every function.
“Fundamentals of Data Visualization” by Claus Wilke — free online at clauswilke.com/dataviz/. The best modern textbook on designing plots that communicate.
From Data to Viz — data-to-viz.com is a decision tree for choosing a plot type based on your data shape.

title: 19  Visual Exploration
text:
19.13 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: Distribution comparison





Create overlaid KDE plots comparing home run distributions for the 1990s and 2010s. What differences do you observe in the shape, center, and spread of the two distributions?






NoteSolution






era_hr = qualified[qualified['decade'].isin([1990, 2010])].copy()
era_hr['era'] = era_hr['decade'].map({1990: '1990s', 2010: '2010s'})

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
sns.kdeplot(data=era_hr, x='HR', hue='era', fill=True, alpha=0.3, ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Home Run Distribution: 1990s vs 2010s')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()













NoteExercise 2: Scatter plot with interpretation





Create a scatter plot of medical vs. retail cannabis sales by county-year. Add a 45-degree reference line. What does the plot tell you about the relationship between the two sales categories?






NoteSolution






county_year = cannabis.groupby(['county', 'year'])[['medical', 'retail']].sum().dropna().reset_index()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 7))
ax.scatter(county_year['medical'], county_year['retail'], alpha=0.3, s=15)
lim = max(county_year['medical'].max(), county_year['retail'].max())
ax.plot([0, lim], [0, lim], 'r--', alpha=0.5, label='1:1 line')
ax.set_xlabel('Medical Sales ($)')
ax.set_ylabel('Retail Sales ($)')
ax.set_title('Medical vs. Retail Cannabis Sales by County-Year')
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Most points fall above the 1:1 line, indicating that retail sales generally exceed medical sales — increasingly so over time as the recreational market has grown.












NoteExercise 3: Faceted trend plot





Create a faceted line plot showing the annual dependency ratio trend for four Colorado counties of your choice. Use FacetGrid or col_wrap in seaborn. What similarities and differences do you observe across counties?

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
20.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
Frame a comparison as a null hypothesis vs. an alternative hypothesis and explain what a p-value does (and does not) mean.
Run an independent and paired t-test using scipy.stats, and compute Cohen’s d as an effect-size measure.
Run a chi-square test of independence on categorical variables, and compute Cramér’s V.
Compute Pearson and Spearman correlation coefficients and interpret the difference between them.
Report statistical results with four elements: what you tested, the result, the test statistic + p-value, and the effect size + interpretation.
Recognize when statistical significance does and does not imply practical significance.

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
20.2 Why Hypothesis Testing
Descriptive statistics and visualizations tell you what your data looks like. Hypothesis testing tells you whether the patterns you see are likely to be meaningful or whether they could have arisen by chance. This chapter introduces a small set of statistical tests — enough to make principled statements about group differences and variable relationships — and focuses on interpreting results in plain language rather than on statistical theory.
The framework is deliberately simple. You will learn to ask three questions: Is there a difference between these groups? How strong is the relationship between these variables? Could what I’m seeing be due to random variation? The tools that answer these questions — t-tests, chi-square tests, and correlation coefficients — cover a large proportion of the exploratory comparisons you will encounter with tabular data.
If you have never worked with p-values before, they can feel counterintuitive at first — the interpretation flips in a way most introductions gloss over. Re-read the interpretation paragraphs slowly, and come back to them after running a few tests yourself. The intuition settles with practice.

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
20.3 Setup
This chapter uses pandas, numpy, matplotlib, seaborn, and scipy.stats.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from scipy import stats

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.3f}'.format)
sns.set_style('whitegrid')







WarningIf you get an ImportError





Install missing packages with conda install pandas numpy matplotlib seaborn scipy (or pip install pandas numpy matplotlib seaborn scipy) and restart the Jupyter kernel. scipy is the most likely miss if you are coming from a minimal pandas environment. See Chapter 1.

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
20.4 Datasets

# Lahman batting — qualified player-seasons with salary
batting = pd.read_csv('Batting.csv')
batting['BA'] = batting['H'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['TB'] = batting['H'] + 2*batting['2B'] + 3*batting['3B'] + 4*batting['HR']
batting['SLG'] = batting['TB'] / batting['AB'].replace(0, float('nan'))
batting['HBP'] = batting['HBP'].fillna(0)
batting['SF'] = batting['SF'].fillna(0)
batting['OBP'] = (
    (batting['H'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'])
    / (batting['AB'] + batting['BB'] + batting['HBP'] + batting['SF']).replace(0, float('nan'))
)
batting['OPS'] = batting['OBP'] + batting['SLG']
batting['decade'] = (batting['yearID'] // 10) * 10
qualified = batting[batting['AB'] >= 100].copy()

salaries = pd.read_csv('Salaries.csv')
bat_sal = pd.merge(
    qualified, salaries[['playerID', 'yearID', 'salary']],
    on=['playerID', 'yearID'], how='inner'
)


# Cannabis sales
cannabis = pd.read_csv(
    'colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv',
    header=2, skipfooter=8, engine='python', na_values=['NR']
)
cannabis.columns = ['month', 'year', 'county', 'medical', 'retail']
cannabis['county'] = cannabis['county'].str.strip()
for col in ['medical', 'retail']:
    cannabis[col] = pd.to_numeric(
        cannabis[col].str.replace('$', '', regex=False).str.replace(',', '', regex=False),
        errors='coerce'
    )
cannabis = cannabis[cannabis['county'] != 'Total']

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
20.5 The Logic of Hypothesis Testing
Every hypothesis test follows the same structure:

State the null hypothesis (H₀): There is no difference between the groups, or there is no relationship between the variables. The observed pattern is due to chance.
State the alternative hypothesis (H₁): There is a real difference or relationship.
Compute a test statistic that measures how far the observed data deviates from what the null hypothesis would predict.
Compute a p-value — the probability of seeing a test statistic this extreme (or more extreme) if the null hypothesis were true.
Interpret the result. A small p-value (conventionally below 0.05) means the observed pattern is unlikely to have arisen by chance alone. A large p-value means you do not have enough evidence to reject the null hypothesis.

A p-value does not tell you the probability that the null hypothesis is true. It tells you how surprising your data would be if the null hypothesis were true. This distinction matters: a p-value of 0.03 means “if there were truly no difference, we’d see data this extreme only 3% of the time” — not “there is a 97% chance the difference is real.”






WarningStatistical significance is not practical significance



A large dataset can produce a tiny p-value for a difference that is too small to matter. Always report and interpret the effect size — the magnitude of the difference — alongside the p-value. A statistically significant but tiny difference is not analytically useful.

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
20.6 Comparing Two Groups: The t-Test
The independent samples t-test asks: Is the mean of one group different from the mean of another group? Use it when you have one numeric outcome and two categorical groups.

20.6.1 Example: Batting average before and after the steroid era
The late 1990s through early 2000s are sometimes called baseball’s “steroid era.” Did batting averages differ between the pre-steroid period (1980–1993) and the steroid era (1994–2004)?

pre_steroid = qualified[
    (qualified['yearID'] >= 1980) & (qualified['yearID'] <= 1993)
]['BA'].dropna()

steroid_era = qualified[
    (qualified['yearID'] >= 1994) & (qualified['yearID'] <= 2004)
]['BA'].dropna()

print(f'Pre-steroid: n={len(pre_steroid)}, mean={pre_steroid.mean():.4f}, std={pre_steroid.std():.4f}')
print(f'Steroid era: n={len(steroid_era)}, mean={steroid_era.mean():.4f}, std={steroid_era.std():.4f}')

Visualize the distributions before testing:

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
sns.kdeplot(pre_steroid, label='Pre-steroid (1980–93)', fill=True, alpha=0.3, ax=ax)
sns.kdeplot(steroid_era, label='Steroid era (1994–2004)', fill=True, alpha=0.3, ax=ax)
ax.set_xlabel('Batting Average')
ax.set_title('Batting Average Distribution: Pre-Steroid vs. Steroid Era')
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Now run the t-test:

t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(pre_steroid, steroid_era)

print(f't-statistic: {t_stat:.3f}')
print(f'p-value: {p_value:.4f}')



20.6.2 Interpreting the result
The mean difference between the two eras can be computed directly:

diff = steroid_era.mean() - pre_steroid.mean()
print(f'Mean difference: {diff:.4f}')

Here is what you can conclude: if the p-value is below 0.05, the difference in mean batting average between the two eras is statistically significant — unlikely to have arisen by chance. The effect size (the mean difference) tells you whether the difference is large enough to be practically meaningful. A difference of 0.005 in batting average is detectable with thousands of player-seasons but may not be substantively interesting. A difference of 0.015 would be more notable.
What you cannot conclude: that steroids caused the difference. These are different groups of players measured in different years with different rules, ballparks, and training methods. The comparison is between-subjects and observational — all the caveats from Chapter 3 apply.


20.6.3 Effect size: Cohen’s d
Cohen’s d measures the difference in means in terms of standard deviations. It provides a standardized measure of effect size that is comparable across different variables and studies.

pooled_std = np.sqrt((pre_steroid.std()**2 + steroid_era.std()**2) / 2)
cohens_d = (steroid_era.mean() - pre_steroid.mean()) / pooled_std

print(f"Cohen's d: {cohens_d:.3f}")

Rules of thumb for interpreting Cohen’s d: 0.2 is a small effect, 0.5 is medium, and 0.8 is large. These are guidelines, not thresholds — context determines what counts as meaningful in your domain.

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
section: 20.7 Comparing Two Related Measurements: The Paired t-Test
text:
20.7 Comparing Two Related Measurements: The Paired t-Test
A paired t-test is appropriate when you have two measurements from the same entity — for example, medical and retail cannabis sales from the same county in the same month. The question is: Is there a systematic difference between the two measurements?

# Compare medical vs retail sales for county-months where both exist
paired = cannabis[cannabis['medical'].notna() & cannabis['retail'].notna()].copy()

print(f'Paired observations: {len(paired)}')
print(f'Mean medical: ${paired["medical"].mean():,.0f}')
print(f'Mean retail:  ${paired["retail"].mean():,.0f}')

t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_rel(paired['retail'], paired['medical'])

print(f'\nPaired t-test:')
print(f't-statistic: {t_stat:.3f}')
print(f'p-value: {p_value:.6f}')

Here is what you can conclude: if the p-value is very small, retail sales are systematically different from medical sales across county-months. The direction and magnitude of the difference tell you which is larger and by how much. This is a within-subjects comparison — the same counties are measured on both variables — so many county-level confounds (population, geography) are controlled for.

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
section: 20.8 Comparing Categorical Variables: The Chi-Square Test
text:
20.8 Comparing Categorical Variables: The Chi-Square Test
The chi-square test of independence asks: Are two categorical variables associated, or are they independent? Use it when both your variables are categorical.

20.8.1 Example: Has the distribution of home run tiers changed across eras?

# Bin HR into categories
hr_data = qualified[qualified['decade'].isin([1980, 2000])].copy()
hr_data['hr_tier'] = pd.cut(
    hr_data['HR'], bins=[-1, 5, 15, 30, 100],
    labels=['0-5', '6-15', '16-30', '31+']
)
hr_data['era'] = hr_data['decade'].map({1980: '1980s', 2000: '2000s'})

# Build contingency table
contingency = pd.crosstab(hr_data['era'], hr_data['hr_tier'])
print('Contingency table:')
print(contingency)


chi2, p_value, dof, expected = stats.chi2_contingency(contingency)

print(f'\nChi-square statistic: {chi2:.2f}')
print(f'Degrees of freedom: {dof}')
print(f'p-value: {p_value:.6f}')

Here is what you can conclude: if the p-value is small, the distribution of home run tiers differs significantly between the two eras — the two variables (era and HR tier) are not independent. To understand how they differ, compare the observed counts to the expected counts (what you would see if era and HR tier were independent):

expected_df = pd.DataFrame(
    expected, index=contingency.index, columns=contingency.columns
).round(1)

print('Expected counts (if independent):')
print(expected_df)
print('\nObserved - Expected:')
print((contingency - expected_df).round(1))

Positive residuals indicate cells with more observations than expected; negative residuals indicate fewer. This tells you which specific categories drive the overall association.


20.8.2 Effect size: Cramér’s V
Cramér’s V is the chi-square analog of Cohen’s d — a measure of how strongly the two categorical variables are associated, scaled from 0 (independent) to 1 (perfectly associated):

n = contingency.sum().sum()
min_dim = min(contingency.shape) - 1
cramers_v = np.sqrt(chi2 / (n * min_dim))

print(f"Cramér's V: {cramers_v:.3f}")

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
20.9 Measuring Linear Relationships: Correlation
The Pearson correlation coefficient (r) measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two numeric variables. It ranges from -1 (perfect negative linear relationship) to +1 (perfect positive linear relationship), with 0 indicating no linear relationship.

20.9.1 Example: Salary and performance

# Filter to a single recent season with enough data
season_2015 = bat_sal[bat_sal['yearID'] == 2015].dropna(subset=['salary', 'OPS'])

r, p_value = stats.pearsonr(season_2015['salary'], season_2015['OPS'])

print(f'Pearson r: {r:.3f}')
print(f'p-value: {p_value:.4f}')
print(f'n: {len(season_2015)}')

Visualize alongside the statistic:

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.scatter(season_2015['salary'] / 1e6, season_2015['OPS'], alpha=0.4, s=20)
ax.set_xlabel('Salary (millions $)')
ax.set_ylabel('OPS')
ax.set_title(f'Salary vs. OPS (2015) — r = {r:.3f}, p = {p_value:.4f}')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Here is what you can conclude: the correlation coefficient tells you the strength and direction of the linear relationship. A positive r means higher salary is associated with higher OPS. The p-value tells you whether this correlation is statistically significant. The magnitude of r tells you how tightly the points cluster around a line — an r of 0.3 indicates a weak-to-moderate positive relationship, meaning salary explains about 9% (r² = 0.09) of the variation in OPS.
What you cannot conclude: that paying a player more would improve their performance. As discussed in Chapter 3, the causal arrow is ambiguous, and the relationship is confounded by age, experience, and roster construction.


20.9.2 Spearman correlation for non-linear monotonic relationships
Pearson’s r measures linear relationships. If the relationship is monotonic but curved (both variables increase together, but not in a straight line), Spearman’s rank correlation is more appropriate:

rho, p_value = stats.spearmanr(season_2015['salary'], season_2015['OPS'])

print(f'Spearman rho: {rho:.3f}')
print(f'p-value: {p_value:.4f}')

Spearman’s ρ (rho) works by ranking both variables and computing the Pearson correlation on the ranks. It is robust to outliers and does not assume the relationship is linear.

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
20.10 Correlation Across Many Variables
When you have several numeric columns, compute the full pairwise correlation matrix and identify which pairs have the strongest relationships:

metrics = bat_sal[['AB', 'HR', 'BB', 'BA', 'SLG', 'OBP', 'OPS', 'salary']].dropna()
corr = metrics.corr()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='coolwarm', center=0,
            square=True, linewidths=0.5, ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Correlation Matrix — Batting Metrics and Salary')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Look for the cells with the strongest colors. Correlations between derived metrics (like OBP and OPS) will be high by construction. The interesting correlations are between conceptually distinct variables — salary and performance metrics, counting stats and rate stats.

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
20.11 Summary: Choosing a Test








Question
Test
Input




Is the mean different between two independent groups?
Independent t-test (stats.ttest_ind)
One numeric + one binary categorical


Is there a systematic difference between two paired measurements?
Paired t-test (stats.ttest_rel)
Two numeric columns from the same entity


Are two categorical variables associated?
Chi-square test (stats.chi2_contingency)
Two categorical variables (contingency table)


How strong is the linear relationship between two variables?
Pearson correlation (stats.pearsonr)
Two numeric variables


How strong is the monotonic (but possibly non-linear) relationship?
Spearman correlation (stats.spearmanr)
Two numeric variables

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
20.12 Reporting Results
When reporting the result of a statistical test, include four elements:

What you tested: “We compared mean batting average between the pre-steroid (1980–93) and steroid (1994–2004) eras.”
The result: “The steroid era had a slightly higher mean batting average (0.271 vs. 0.267).”
The test statistic and p-value: “t(8452) = -3.21, p = 0.001”
The effect size and interpretation: “Cohen’s d = 0.07, a small effect. While the difference is statistically significant due to the large sample size, the practical difference of 4 points of batting average is modest.”

This pattern — what, result, statistics, interpretation — keeps your reporting honest and complete. It prevents the common mistake of reporting a p-value without context about the magnitude of the effect.

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
20.13 Common Issues in This Chapter






WarningYour p-value is tiny but the effect is invisible



What it means: With a large sample, almost any non-zero difference is “statistically significant.” This is a feature of the test, not evidence that the difference matters.
How to fix it: Always report effect size (Cohen’s d, Cramér’s V, r) alongside the p-value. Interpret the magnitude in plain language — “a 4-point difference in batting average” — not the p-value in isolation.








Warningttest_ind complains about unequal variances or NaN values



What it means: By default ttest_ind assumes equal variances and breaks on NaN. If one group has much more spread than the other, or if either input has missing values, you will get misleading results or errors.
How to fix it: Drop NaNs with .dropna() before passing to the test. If variances differ substantially, use stats.ttest_ind(a, b, equal_var=False) to run Welch’s t-test.








WarningChi-square warns about low expected counts



What it means: The chi-square approximation breaks down when any expected cell count is below 5. The test still runs but the p-value may be unreliable.
How to fix it: Collapse rare categories before testing, or use stats.fisher_exact for 2×2 tables. The expected array returned by chi2_contingency tells you which cells are problematic.








WarningPearson r is near zero but the scatter plot shows a clear pattern



What it means: Pearson measures linear relationships. A U-shaped or curved pattern can have near-zero Pearson correlation but obvious structure.
How to fix it: Visualize first, always. If the pattern is monotonic but curved, switch to Spearman. If the pattern is not monotonic, correlation is not the right tool — consider binning the predictor and comparing means across bins.

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
20.14 Key Takeaways

Every test follows the same structure: state the hypotheses, compute the statistic, interpret the p-value and the effect size.
A p-value tells you how surprising your data would be under the null hypothesis — not the probability that the null is true.
Statistical significance ≠ practical significance. Always report effect sizes. Tiny differences can be “significant” with a large enough sample.
Pearson for linear, Spearman for monotonic-but-curved, t-test for two groups, chi-square for two categorical variables, paired t-test for repeated measurements on the same entity.
Visualize before you test. A scatter plot or histogram catches the problems a p-value cannot.

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
20.15 Further Reading






Tip📚 Going deeper on statistical inference






scipy.stats reference — docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/stats.html covers every test introduced in this chapter and many more.
scipy.stats tutorial — docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/tutorial/stats.html walks through distributions, tests, and descriptive statistics.
“Statistical Thinking for the 21st Century” by Russell Poldrack — free online at statsthinking21.org. A modern introduction that emphasizes interpretation over mechanics.
ASA Statement on p-values — amstat.org is the American Statistical Association’s short, authoritative statement on what p-values mean and do not mean. Two pages; worth a careful read.
statsmodels — For regression, ANOVA, and generalized linear models beyond the tests covered here, see statsmodels. Its formula-based interface will feel familiar if you have used R or Stata.
scikit-learn — When your question shifts from inference (what explains my outcome?) to prediction (what outcome should I expect?), scikit-learn provides a consistent API for classical machine learning algorithms.

title: 20  Hypothesis Testing and Comparisons
text:
20.16 Exercises






NoteExercise 1: t-test for home runs





Compare the mean number of home runs per qualified player-season between the 1980s and the 2010s. Run an independent t-test and compute Cohen’s d. Is the difference statistically significant? Is it practically meaningful?






NoteSolution






hr_80s = qualified[qualified['decade'] == 1980]['HR'].dropna()
hr_10s = qualified[qualified['decade'] == 2010]['HR'].dropna()

print(f'1980s: n={len(hr_80s)}, mean={hr_80s.mean():.2f}')
print(f'2010s: n={len(hr_10s)}, mean={hr_10s.mean():.2f}')

t, p = stats.ttest_ind(hr_80s, hr_10s)
pooled = np.sqrt((hr_80s.std()**2 + hr_10s.std()**2) / 2)
d = (hr_10s.mean() - hr_80s.mean()) / pooled

print(f'\nt = {t:.3f}, p = {p:.4f}')
print(f"Cohen's d = {d:.3f}")













NoteExercise 2: Chi-square for cannabis counties





Create a contingency table of Colorado counties by year (pick two years at least 5 years apart) and whether they reported any retail sales (yes/no). Run a chi-square test. Has the proportion of counties with retail sales changed significantly?






NoteSolution






for_chi = cannabis[cannabis['year'].isin([2016, 2023])].copy()
for_chi['has_retail'] = for_chi['retail'].notna() & (for_chi['retail'] > 0)
yearly = for_chi.groupby(['year', 'county'])['has_retail'].any().reset_index()

ct = pd.crosstab(yearly['year'], yearly['has_retail'])
ct.columns = ['No Retail', 'Has Retail']
print(ct)

chi2, p, dof, exp = stats.chi2_contingency(ct)
print(f'\nChi-square = {chi2:.2f}, p = {p:.4f}')













NoteExercise 3: Correlation exploration





Compute the Pearson and Spearman correlations between at-bats (AB) and salary for the 2010s. Which is larger, and what does the difference tell you about the shape of the relationship? Produce a scatter plot to visually confirm your interpretation.






NoteSolution






recent = bat_sal[bat_sal['decade'] == 2010].dropna(subset=['AB', 'salary'])

r_pearson, p_pearson = stats.pearsonr(recent['AB'], recent['salary'])
r_spearman, p_spearman = stats.spearmanr(recent['AB'], recent['salary'])

print(f'Pearson r = {r_pearson:.3f} (p = {p_pearson:.4f})')
print(f'Spearman ρ = {r_spearman:.3f} (p = {p_spearman:.4f})')

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.scatter(recent['AB'], recent['salary']/1e6, alpha=0.3, s=15)
ax.set_xlabel('At Bats')
ax.set_ylabel('Salary (millions $)')
ax.set_title(f'AB vs. Salary — Pearson r={r_pearson:.3f}, Spearman ρ={r_spearman:.3f}')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

If Spearman is larger than Pearson, the relationship is monotonic but nonlinear — likely because salary increases steeply at high AB counts but flattens at low counts.

title: Appendix A — Datasets
text:
A.1 What This Appendix Covers
This appendix is the reference you come back to whenever you need to remember how a dataset is structured or what its known quirks are. For every dataset used in the book, the entry below lists:
The “Known quirks” block for each dataset is your per-dataset troubleshooting guide. Read it before loading the file for the first time; return to it whenever a new error surprises you.
All datasets are available in the accompanying GitHub repository.

title: Appendix A — Datasets
section: A.2 Colorado Cannabis Sales by County
text:
A.2 Colorado Cannabis Sales by County
Source: Colorado Department of Revenue, Marijuana Enforcement Division
URL: https://cdor.colorado.gov/data-and-reports/marijuana-data/marijuana-sales-reports
File: colorado_cannabis_sales_by_county.csv
Used in: Chapter 5, Chapter 9
This file contains monthly medical and retail cannabis sales totals for each Colorado county from 2014 to 2024. The original file is an Excel export with a two-row title block at the top and an eight-row footnote section at the bottom.
Structure: Each row represents one county’s sales for one month.



Column
Type
Description




Month
integer
Calendar month (1–12)


Year
integer
Calendar year (2014–2024)


County
text
Colorado county name


Medical Sales
currency string
Medical cannabis sales in dollars


Retail Sales
currency string
Retail cannabis sales in dollars



Known quirks:

Header row is on row 2 (zero-indexed), not row 0. Use header=2 in read_csv.
Eight footer rows contain footnotes and source information. Use skipfooter=8.
Sales columns are formatted as currency strings ($1,234,567) and must be stripped of $ and , before numeric conversion.
NR (Not Reported) appears in sales columns for counties with fewer than three licensees. Treat as missing with na_values=['NR'].
Rows with County == 'Total' are monthly statewide aggregates and should be removed before county-level analysis.
County names may contain leading or trailing whitespace.

title: Appendix A — Datasets
text:
A.9 Non-Tabular Data Sources
The five chapters in Part IV use specialized datasets — shapefiles, edge lists, text corpora, time series, and API responses. Each chapter’s examples assume one or more of the sources listed below.

A.9.1 Census TIGER/Line Shapefiles (Chapter 13)
Source: US Census Bureau URL: https://www.census.gov/geographies/mapping-files/time-series/geo/tiger-line-file.html Files: tl_2023_08_county.shp (Colorado counties) and companion .shx, .dbf, .prj files Used in: Chapter 13
County and state boundary polygons for the entire United States, republished annually. The Colorado-specific file (08 = state FIPS) is a good default for the book’s spatial examples.
Known quirks:

A shapefile is actually several files (.shp, .shx, .dbf, .prj). Keep them together in the same folder.
Default CRS is EPSG:4269 (NAD83 latitude/longitude). Reproject to EPSG:3857 for overlays on web basemaps.
Each county has a GEOID / COUNTYFP that matches the countyfips column in the State Demography Office data.



A.9.2 ICON / KONECT / Network Repository Edge Lists (Chapter 14)
Sources: ICON (CU Boulder), KONECT (Koblenz), Network Repository Example files: soc-karate.edges (Zachary’s karate club), soc-dolphins.edges (dolphin social network) Used in: Chapter 14
Small, curated network datasets suitable for teaching. Zachary’s karate club (34 nodes, 78 edges) is the single most common beginner example.
Known quirks:

Edge-list files often start with comment lines prefixed by % (KONECT) or #. Use comment='%' in pd.read_csv to skip them.
Many datasets ship as whitespace-delimited .edges files, not .csv. Pass sep=r'\s+' or sep=' ' to pd.read_csv.
Node IDs are typically integers. Cast consistently before building a graph to avoid “node not in graph” errors.



A.9.3 Project Gutenberg and NLTK Corpora (Chapter 15)
Sources: Project Gutenberg, NLTK built-in corpora Example files: 2021-biden.txt (Project Gutenberg), inaugural corpus (NLTK built-in) Used in: Chapter 15
The NLTK inaugural corpus contains every US presidential inaugural address (1789–present) as plain text, accessible via nltk.corpus.inaugural. Project Gutenberg distributes public-domain literature in UTF-8 text.
Known quirks:

Project Gutenberg files include a boilerplate header and footer that must be stripped before analysis.
NLTK corpora require a one-time nltk.download('inaugural') (and similar for other corpora and resources like punkt, stopwords, wordnet, vader_lexicon).
Encoding is UTF-8 for recent files; older Gutenberg texts may arrive as Latin-1. Pass encoding= explicitly.



A.9.4 FRED / World Bank / IMF Time Series (Chapter 16)
Sources: FRED, World Bank Open Data, IMF Data Example files: UNRATE.csv (US unemployment rate, monthly), CPIAUCSL.csv (CPI, monthly), HOUST.csv (housing starts, monthly) Used in: Chapter 16
FRED publishes thousands of US macroeconomic series as downloadable CSVs with two columns: DATE and the series value. World Bank data arrives in wide format (one column per year) and requires melt to reshape into long.
Known quirks:

FRED files use DATE as an ISO date column. Parse with pd.to_datetime(df['DATE']) and set as the index.
Some series have “.” or blank rows for missing observations (e.g., during data-collection gaps). Treat these as NaN via na_values=['.', ''].
World Bank CSVs include 4 metadata rows at the top. Use skiprows=4.
IMF files sometimes require joining a separate country-code reference file before they are usable.



A.9.5 Public APIs for Structured Data (Chapter 17)
Sources: FRED API, Census Bureau API, OpenAlex API, Wikipedia/MediaWiki API, Open Library API Example file: example_openalex_response.json (cached API response), any static Wikipedia page for HTML scraping Used in: Chapter 17
All five APIs above are free; most require only a User-Agent header (or, for the OpenAlex “polite pool,” a mailto query parameter). The chapter includes worked examples against OpenAlex.
Known quirks:

FRED, Census, and Open Library require registration for an API key. OpenAlex and Wikipedia are unauthenticated.
Every API paginates differently — some use page=N, some use a cursor token, some use Link: headers. Read the docs before looping.
Rate limits are real. Respect them with time.sleep() between requests, and cache responses to disk during development.
API responses are JSON; flatten with pd.json_normalize() to get a clean DataFrame.








NotePublic Interest Context: Exemption



The datasets described in this appendix represent what is publicly available — what agencies have chosen to publish, in the formats they chose to publish it. That is a narrower set than what exists or what would be useful for public accountability.
Noticeably absent from what is publicly available at a useful granularity:

Housing displacement records at the parcel level — public records exist, but parcel-level linkage to eviction or code enforcement actions is rarely published.
Policing data at the incident level — Colorado requires some reporting, but stop-and-frisk data, use-of-force details, and officer-level records are inconsistently available across jurisdictions.
Detention and incarceration populations — county jail populations are not systematically published in machine-readable form.
Environmental health data — air quality monitoring stations cover urban centers but are sparse in rural counties and on Tribal lands.

Exemption — data that does not exist publicly, or is not released — is itself a data quality problem. The questions you cannot answer because the data is missing are often the most important ones to ask.
When you finish an analysis, it is worth asking: what would you study if you had access to data that is not currently available? That question is as analytically important as what you did study. See Chapter 4 for the broader framework.

text:

Finding new datasets: Data.gov aggregates federal sources, CO Information Marketplace publishes Colorado state data, and your local city/county usually has an open-data portal. When evaluating a new source, always check what is published, when it was last updated, and what machine-readable formats are available.

title: AI Use Disclosure
text:
Why This Disclosure Exists
This appendix discloses the role of large language models (LLMs) in producing this book and discusses the methodological, epistemological, and ethical implications of that role. It is written in the spirit of the emerging disclosure standards in scholarly publishing and journalism: not as a legal disclaimer, but as a substantive account that a careful reader can use to evaluate the text.
When readers encounter a textbook, they make reasonable assumptions: that a named author wrote the sentences, that examples were chosen for pedagogical reasons the author can explain, that errors are the author’s errors and can be traced back to a source decision. AI involvement in content production complicates each of these assumptions. This appendix addresses that complication directly.
The standards informing this disclosure include the Artificial Intelligence Disclosure (AID) Framework developed by Weaver [-@weaver2024aid], which adapts the CRediT contributor taxonomy to AI tool use; the STM Association’s recommendations for classifying AI use in manuscript preparation [-@stm2025classification]; and transparency principles from the Associated Press [-@ap2023ai] and journalism research [-@journalistsresource2024audiences] on communicating AI use to audiences. Each of these frameworks emphasizes that disclosure should be specific enough to be informative and honest enough to support independent evaluation.

title: AI Use Disclosure
text:
Source Materials
This book grew out of five years of course materials developed for INFO 3401: Information Exploration at the University of Colorado Boulder. The source materials that preceded and informed the book include:

Jupyter notebooks — functional, executable notebooks developed semester by semester, containing Python and SQL code, narrative markdown cells, and inline commentary explaining pedagogical choices. These notebooks are the authoritative technical record for every code example in the book.
Lecture slides — slide decks from recorded class sessions covering conceptual framing, worked examples, and discussion prompts.
Problem sets and assessments — homework assignments, in-class exercises, and exam questions that informed the exercise structure.
Course discussion records — anonymized transcripts and notes from office hours and course forums that identified the questions students most frequently asked and the misconceptions they most frequently held.

All datasets used in the book were assembled and documented before the AI-assisted writing process began. The appendix-datasets.qmd chapter reflects the author’s direct knowledge of each dataset’s source, structure, and known quirks accumulated through repeated use in the classroom.

title: AI Use Disclosure
text:
What LLMs Were Used For
This book was written with assistance from Claude, an LLM developed by Anthropic. Using the AID Framework’s activity taxonomy as a guide, the following categories of use apply:
Writing — Original Draft. The primary AI contribution was converting source notebook content into book chapter prose. The Jupyter notebooks contained the code, the logic, and often sentence-length explanatory comments; Claude converted these into fully articulated chapter narratives with section structure, transitional prose, and pedagogical framing. The resulting drafts were reviewed, revised, and approved by the author.
Writing — Review and Editing. Claude was used to revise specific sections — rewriting chapter introductions when chapters moved in the book’s structure, adding bridge paragraphs between sections, and improving transitions. All such revisions were proposed by Claude and approved or further revised by the author.
Conceptualization. For content not derived from existing course materials — specifically the public interest data science framework in 4  Public Interest Data Science — the author provided a conceptual description and bibliographic pointers, and Claude elaborated this into chapter content. The three dynamics (enclosure, erosion, exemption) and three pillars (openness, oversight, ownership) reflect the author’s framework; the examples connecting that framework to course datasets were jointly developed.
Information Collection. Claude drew on its training data for several types of content: framing the Load–Clean–Extend–Reshape workflow in plain-language terms, summarizing database concepts, and characterizing statistical methods. Because Claude’s training data has a knowledge cutoff and may contain errors, all factual claims about software behavior, statistical properties, and data sources were verified against primary documentation by the author.
Structural Editing. Later in the production process, Claude was used to implement structural reorganization decisions — moving chapters between parts of the book, adding cross-references, and redistributing content across chapter boundaries. These changes were specified by the author through iterative review and implemented by Claude as a structured editing task.
The following categories from the AID Framework were not applicable or were handled entirely by the author without AI assistance:

Data Collection / Execution: All datasets were identified, downloaded, and documented by the author from primary sources.
Data Analysis: The analytical approaches demonstrated in the book reflect the author’s disciplinary judgment; Claude did not select methods.
Data Curation: Dataset documentation in the appendix was written by the author.
Visualization: No AI image or figure generation was used; all visualizations are generated from code.

title: AI Use Disclosure
section: What the Author Is Responsible For
text:
What the Author Is Responsible For
The author reviewed every chapter before publication. Responsibility for the book’s contents rests with the author in all respects:

All code examples were drawn from or validated against working course notebooks. If a code example produces incorrect output when executed, that is an error the author is accountable for.
All factual claims about Python, pandas, SQL, and statistical methods were reviewed against primary documentation. LLM-generated factual claims that were not independently verified were removed or corrected.
All pedagogical decisions — which topics to include, which datasets to use, what constitutes a useful exercise, how to sequence concepts — reflect the author’s judgment formed through years of teaching this material.
All framing and interpretation — the public interest framework, the choice to introduce research questions before technical material, the emphasis on silent errors and validation — reflect the author’s intellectual commitments, not defaults produced by an AI system.

title: AI Use Disclosure
text:
Limitations This Workflow Creates
Readers should be aware of several limitations that follow from AI-assisted prose generation:
Fluency without correctness. LLMs produce grammatically coherent, confident-sounding prose even when the underlying claim is wrong. The author reviewed drafts for factual accuracy, but not all errors are catchable on review. If you find a technical error in this book, please report it via the GitHub repository’s issue tracker.
Averaging over sources. LLMs generate text by drawing on statistical patterns in training data, which means AI-drafted explanations tend toward consensus descriptions rather than precise technical definitions. In some cases — particularly for edge cases in pandas behavior, SQL semantics, or statistical assumptions — the “standard” explanation is subtly wrong in ways that matter for programming. Code cells should always be treated as more authoritative than prose explanations; when they conflict, trust the code.
Voice consistency. The book was written across multiple AI-assisted sessions. The prose voice may vary across chapters more than a single-author text written in one sustained effort. The author worked to normalize this in review, but variation may persist.
Exercise quality. Exercises were partly generated by Claude and partly drawn from existing course problem sets. AI-generated exercises have been reviewed for correctness but may occasionally have ambiguous instructions or unintended solution paths. If an exercise seems broken, it may be.
The disclosure chapter is itself AI-assisted. This appendix was drafted by Claude based on the author’s description of the workflow and the disclosure frameworks provided. The author reviewed and revised it. The recursive nature of this situation — an LLM disclosing LLM use — is acknowledged rather than papered over.

title: AI Use Disclosure
text:
How to Evaluate the Text
Several practices can help you evaluate this book’s content given its production method:
Run the code. The code cells are the ground truth. Any claim made in prose that can be tested by running code should be tested. The repository includes a data/ directory with all course datasets; every code example in the book should execute without modification when the working directory is set correctly.
Compare against primary documentation. For Python syntax and pandas behavior, the authoritative sources are the pandas documentation and Python documentation. For SQL semantics, consult the SQLite documentation. For statistical methods, consult a statistics textbook. This book’s prose explanations should be consistent with those sources; where they diverge, the primary documentation is correct.
Treat descriptions of tradeoffs skeptically. LLMs are prone to generating balanced-sounding descriptions that present multiple options as equally valid when one is clearly preferable, or that oversimplify tradeoffs. Claims about when to use one method versus another should be evaluated against additional sources.
Report errors. The book’s GitHub repository is at https://github.com/cuinfoscience/Information-Exploration-Book. Issues can be filed at any time and will be addressed in subsequent revisions. Noting the specific claim, the chapter, and (if applicable) the correct information is the most useful form of error report.

title: AI Use Disclosure
text:
On Authorship and Transparency
The disclosure frameworks cited at the start of this appendix were designed primarily for journal articles, where questions of authorship carry formal weight — determining credit, accountability, and research integrity. A textbook is a different genre. Its purpose is pedagogical rather than knowledge-claiming; its relationship to originality is different; the standards of accountability are both more and less demanding in different ways.
What this book asserts is not “I, the author, wrote every word.” It asserts: “The intellectual framework, pedagogical judgments, source materials, code examples, and dataset expertise in this book are the author’s. The prose that communicates them was produced collaboratively with an AI tool, reviewed by the author, and is the author’s responsibility.” That is a different claim from either “AI wrote this book” or “AI merely cleaned up grammar.” It is an honest description of a production process that is becoming common in technical and educational writing and that deserves its own vocabulary — which these emerging frameworks are beginning to provide.
The author’s name on the cover reflects intellectual ownership and accountability, not the claim of having typed every sentence. The reader’s ability to evaluate that claim depends on disclosures like this one existing and being read.

title: AI Use Disclosure
text:
